Question: Does national legislation clearly define the legal conditions under which gold mining is permitted, and distinguish them from activities considered illegal?
Bolivia_Justification:
Bolivia has Law No. 535, enacted in May 2014. This law aims to regulate all mining and metallurgical activities, establishing principles, guidelines, and procedures for granting, maintaining, and terminating mining rights (Art. 1). However, the mining regulatory framework does not include specific conditions for gold mining. Furthermore, Article 104 defines illegal mining as any activity involving the exploitation of natural resources without the authorization or rights granted in accordance with Law No. 535. While the law does not specify the type of mining and minerals to be exploited, it provides the general criteria governing mining within the national territory. Law No. 535 itself empowers the Mining Authority (AJAM) to be the body representing the Bolivian State in granting mining rights (Art. 40). Within this framework, the aforementioned authority promulgated the Regulations for the Granting and Termination of Mining Rights, approved by Ministerial Resolution No. 023/2015, dated January 30, 2015, and subsequently amended by Ministerial Resolution No. 096/2020, dated April 14, 2020.

Law No. 535 establishes the scope of mining rights, stipulating in Article 93, paragraph II, that mining activities may not be carried out in:

a) Within cities, towns, cemeteries, and public or private buildings.

b) In the vicinity of highways, canals, pipelines, railways, and power and communications transmission lines, up to one hundred (100) meters.

c) In the vicinity of watersheds, lakes, rivers, slopes, and reservoirs, restrictions will be subject to Environmental Impact Assessments with a multisectoral approach.

d) In the vicinity of airports, up to one thousand (1000) meters.

e) In the vicinity of military barracks and installations, up to three hundred (300) meters.

f) In areas of historical and archaeological monuments declared by law, up to one thousand (1000) meters.

With regard to subsection c), it should be noted that Bolivia does not yet have regulations establishing the procedure for the preparation and processing of Environmental Impact Assessments with a multisectoral approach, which creates a legal gap.

It should be considered that the aforementioned exclusions do not apply to Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) granted before the enactment of Law No. 535.

Brazil_Justification: Brazilian legislation establishes a legal framework for gold mining, with clear distinctions between permitted and illegal activities, especially regarding artisanal and small-scale mining (MAPE). The main legal instrument regulating artisanal mining is Law No. 7,805, of July 18, 1989 [1], which instituted the Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG) regime. This regime was created to formalize and regulate artisanal mining activity, distinguishing it from illegal practices.
Brazil_Sources: Law No. 7,805, of July 18, 1989. Available at: https://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/leis/l7805.htm
Colombia_Justification:
Colombian legislation establishes a clear and detailed legal framework that defines the conditions for legal gold mining and distinguishes it from illegal activities. Law 685 of 2001, or the Mining Code, is the main regulation governing the sector and establishes that the right to explore and exploit state-owned minerals can only be acquired through a mining concession contract duly granted and registered in the National Mining Registry.

Article 159 of the Mining Code classifies the illicit exploitation of mineral deposits as a crime, establishing a clear distinction between legal activities covered by a mining title and those that are not. This distinction is reinforced by Decree 1666 of 2016, which classifies mining activities as subsistence, small-scale, medium-scale, and large-scale mining, establishing specific requirements and conditions for each.

The criminal classification of the offense is clarified, as Article 332 of Law 599 of 2000 identifies the illegal exploitation of mineral deposits and other materials as a crime.

The National Mining Agency (ANM) is the authority responsible for administering mining titles and public registries, which allows for a clear distinction between legal and illegal operations. Through its information systems, such as the National Mining Registry, the ANM provides public access to information on current mining titles, contributing to transparency and ensuring legality in the sector.

Peru_Justification: Peruvian legislation, through Legislative Decree No. 1100, establishes a clear framework for the interdiction of illegal mining and defines the conditions under which gold mining is illegal. This law explicitly prohibits the use of dredges and other similar devices in all waterways, rivers, lakes, lagoons, oxbow lakes, bodies of water, wetlands, and palm swamps, as well as the use of mercury and cyanide in illegal mining activities. Furthermore, Law No. 27651, the Law for the Formalization and Promotion of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining, establishes the conditions for legal small-scale mining, distinguishing it from illegal mining. The General Mining Law – Consolidated Text of Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM – also defines mining activity, who may engage in it, and under what conditions. It regulates mining concessions, rights, obligations, authorizations, and oversight. Finally, Legislative Decree No. 1105 (2012) – Mining Formalization Process – establishes an exceptional process for the formalization of informal small-scale and artisanal gold mining. It outlines the conditions for registration, production declaration, and compliance with environmental and tax obligations. Therefore, Peruvian legislation clearly distinguishes between legal and illegal mining.

Ecuador_Justification:
Mining activity is recognized by the Ecuadorian Constitution (CRE) as part of the strategic sectors, under the responsibility of the State and governed by environmental principles (Arts. 313 and 316 CRE). Minerals are state property, but the State may delegate its participation to private initiatives, mixed-ownership companies, or the so-called popular and solidarity economy (Art. 1 Mining Law, LM). Accordingly, the LM clearly defines the legal conditions that differentiate legal from illegal mining, which lie in the granting of mining rights (Art. 16 LM) derived from: concession titles, contracts, or authorizations issued by the competent authority (Art. 17 LM) depending on the regime (Arts. 133 to 137 LM) and phases (Art. 27 LM) of the mining activity. Hence, Art. Article 56 of the Mining Law defines illegal mining as “mining operations, work, and activities in any of their phases without any title or corresponding legal permit.” This is complemented by Article 57 of the Mining Law: “Illegal mining activity carried out by natural or legal persons, or groups of persons, whether national or foreign, without the titles, authorizations, permits, or licenses…”.

Therefore, legal mining activity is that carried out by an authorized holder. Conversely, anyone who carries out such activity without authorized title is engaging in illegal mining, which is considered (classified) as an administrative offense (Article 57 of the Mining Law) and even a criminal offense (Article 260 of the Comprehensive Organic Criminal Code, COIP). In criminal matters, a more technical term is used: illicit activity of mining resources since the unlawfulness refers not only to the Mining Law but also to the other rules of the legal system applicable to the matter, including the Regulation to the Mining Law (RLM), the Environmental Regulation of Mining Activities (RAAM) and applicable secondary regulations (agreements, ministerial resolutions).

Guyana_Justification: Guyana's national legislation provides a clear and comprehensive framework that defines the legal conditions for gold mining and distinguishes permitted activities from illegal ones. The Mining Act 1989 establishes that all minerals are vested in the State and that any person wishing to engage in mining must do so under a license or permit granted by the Guyana Geology and Mines Commission (GGMC). Section 14(1)(a) of the Act explicitly prohibits mining without such authorization, thereby creating a clear legal distinction between legal and illegal mining operations. This legal structure provides a solid foundation for regulating the mining sector and enforcing compliance.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname's Mining Decree 1986 clearly establishes that mining activities are only permitted if they comply with lawful regulations and after rights have been granted by the competent authority [1]. This implies a distinction between legal, permitted mining activities and those conducted without such authorization, which would be considered illegal. The decree sets the foundational legal conditions for mining operations.

Venezuela_Justification:
Current Venezuelan legislation—comprising the Organic Law Decree reserving to the State the Exploration and Exploitation of Gold and other Strategic Minerals (Decree No. 2,165, of December 30, 2015), the Mining Law (1999), and its General Regulations (2001)—establishes the conditions under which gold mining can be carried out legally.
Any mining activity carried out without state participation, without official registration, or that fails to comply with the environmental or commercialization conditions established by law is considered illegal.

Conditions for legality: Exploitation is reserved to the State. Private participation is only possible through partnerships with the State via Joint Ventures or through the formation of Strategic Alliances, signed between the State and groups of people organized in companies or other associative forms permitted by law, for the execution of primary and related activities in the exploitation of gold and other strategic minerals, with the exception of marketing, which is exclusively reserved for the State.
Activities considered illegal: Exploration and exploitation activities carried out without authorization are illegal; that is, those carried out by individuals who are not part of a Strategic Alliance, who are not registered in the Single Mining Registry, or who violate environmental regulations and/or the inspections of the relevant Ministry.

Question: Does national legislation provide a clear legal definition of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), distinct from large-scale or industrial mining?
Bolivia_Justification:
Indeed, Law No. 535 does not define MAPE (Small-Scale Mining), and there is no Bolivian legislation that defines or characterizes MAPE. However, as mentioned in the justification for Criterion 2, Article 33, paragraph II of Law No. 535 introduces the concept of small-scale mining, indicating that it refers to those who work on a small scale, using manual, semi-mechanized, or mechanized methods, and further specifying that their work can be individual, family-based, jointly owned, or through a company.

Furthermore, the Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities (RAAM), in Article 93, define minor mining activities with known but insignificant environmental impacts (AMIAC) as “underground mining activities located in unprotected areas of the Western Cordillera, the Altiplano, and the Eastern Cordillera on both flanks, comprising:
1) exploration, development, preparation, and exploitation through galleries (cut-offs and stopes), pits, ramps, shafts, raises, and stopes with an extraction capacity equal to or less than three hundred (300) tons/month; and/or
2) mineral concentration on a scale equal to or less than 300 tons/month with one or more of the following processes: crushing and grinding (manual and mechanical), gravimetric and magnetic classification and concentration, sulfide separation by surface flotation, amalgamation, and drying, storage, and transportation of the resulting concentrates.”

According to the above, AMIAC could also be considered small-scale activities. However, it is necessary to have a characterization and definition of mining activities that can be classified as MAPE, in order to have clear policies and specific regulations for MAPE.

 - Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities. Available at: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/149_DS_24782.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Brazilian national legislation provides a legal definition for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASSM), primarily through Law No. 7,805 of July 18, 1989, and the Prospector Statute (Law No. 11,685 of June 25, 2008). While these laws define ASSM, the differentiation from large-scale or industrial mining is more implicit, based on the nature of the activity, the scale, and the legal regime under which it operates, rather than explicit comparative definitions in the same legal texts.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has legislation that clearly defines artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) and distinguishes it from large-scale mining. Decree 1666 of 2016 is the key regulation establishing this classification, defining specific criteria based on the concession area (in hectares) and the annual production volume (in grams or tons).

The decree defines "Subsistence Mining" as an activity carried out by individuals or groups, limited to open-pit mining and the collection of river minerals, using only hand tools and without mechanized equipment. This definition includes traditional panning, which is also regulated in the Mining Code (Article 155).

The classification by scale is established as follows:

Small-scale mining: Concessions of up to 150 hectares and production of up to 10,000 grams of precious metals per year.

The decree defines "Subsistence Mining" as an activity carried out by individuals or groups, limited to open-pit mining and the collection of river minerals, using only hand tools and without mechanized equipment. Medium-scale mining: Concessions of more than 150 and up to 5,000 hectares, with production of more than 10,000 and up to 1,000,000 grams per year.

Large-scale mining: Concessions of more than 5,000 hectares and production of more than 1,000,000 grams per year.

This detailed classification provides a clear basis for the differentiated regulation of small-scale artisanal and small-scale mining, allowing for the application of specific policies and requirements for each type of operation.

Peru_Justification: Article 91 of the Consolidated Text of the General Mining Law (Supreme Decree 014-92-EM) defines artisanal and small-scale mining producers based on their installed production and/or processing capacity. The law establishes technical parameters expressed in metric tons per day, clearly differentiating these categories from medium and large-scale mining, which exceed these values.

Ecuador_Justification:
The Mining Law (LM) establishes mining regimes for artisanal, small-scale, medium-scale, and large-scale mining, which differ in several factors such as production volume, deposit characteristics, and the means employed. Within this framework, the LM provides clear definitions of artisanal and small-scale mining (the term "small-scale mining" is not used in Ecuador).

Artisanal Mining: Article 134 of the LM, in conjunction with Article 18 of the Regulations for the Special Regime of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining, defines it as mining carried out through "individual, family, or associative work by those who perform mining activities in open areas, solely and exclusively as a means of subsistence..." Article 134 of the LM adds the following characteristics: (i) use of machinery and equipment with limited load and production capacities, (ii) carrying out the activity within the permitted territorial jurisdiction, (iii) not subject to the payment of royalties or patents, but subject to the tax regime. and (iv) production capacity: a) for metallic minerals up to 10 tons per day in underground mining and 120 cubic meters per day in alluvial mining, b) for non-metallic minerals up to 50 tons per day, c) for construction materials up to 100 cubic meters per day for alluvial or unconsolidated material mining and 50 metric tons per day in open-pit mining in hard rock.

Small-scale mining.—Article 138 of the Mining Law, in conjunction with Article 4 of the Regulations for the Special Regime for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining, defines it as that which, “due to the geological and mining characteristics and conditions of the deposits of metallic and non-metallic mineral substances and construction materials, as well as their technical and economic parameters, makes their rational exploitation viable directly, without prejudice to prior exploration work, or to the simultaneous carrying out of exploration and exploitation.” 138.1 LM indicates this production capacity: a) for metallic minerals: up to 300 tonnes per day in underground mining; up to 1000 tonnes per day in open-pit mining; and, up to 1500 cubic meters per day in alluvial mining; b) for non-metallic minerals: up to 1000 tonnes per day; and, c) for construction materials: up to 800 cubic meters for alluvial terrace mining; and, 500 metric tons per day in open-pit mining in hard rock (quarry).

Guyana_Justification: While Guyana's legal framework recognizes different scales of mining (small, medium, and large), it does not provide a clear and distinct legal definition of "artisanal" mining. The National Mineral Sector Policy Framework and Actions, 2019-2029 notes that the term "artisanal" is considered a "socially-loaded term" and is not typically used in the country's legal framework. Instead, the framework uses the term "small-scale mining" to encompass what is generally understood as artisanal mining. The Mining Act 1989 distinguishes between different categories of mining operations but does not specifically define "artisanal" mining as a distinct category. This lack of a specific legal definition for artisanal mining can create ambiguity and may hinder the development of targeted policies and support mechanisms for this sub-sector.

Venezuela_Justification:
Current Venezuelan mining legislation distinguishes between small-scale, medium-scale, and large-scale mining. While the concept of artisanal mining is included in the 1999 Mining Law—whose Article 82 defines it as characterized by personal and direct labor in the exploitation of alluvial gold and diamonds, using simple, portable, manual equipment, rudimentary extraction and processing techniques, and which can only be carried out by Venezuelan citizens—this modality appears to disappear in Decree No. 2,165, the most recent and highest-ranking regulation (Organic Law).

This decree not only fails to separate artisanal mining from small-scale mining, considering it part of the latter, but also eliminates the possibility of personal or subsistence mining, leaving the use of rudimentary and portable tools as the sole differentiating criterion—a criterion that loses practical relevance within the alliance framework defined by said Organic Law as the only means of formally carrying out the activity.

Note: The concept of MAPE, as such, is not contemplated in Venezuelan legislation.

Question: Does the legal framework set distinct conditions or restrictions for mining activities in Indigenous territories, protected areas, mining exclusion zones and community lands, including provisions related to consent, well-informed prior consultation, or land tenure?
Bolivia_Justification:
The Bolivian Constitution, with regard to protected areas, recognizes them only as a common good, forming part of the natural and cultural heritage, and fulfilling environmental, social, cultural, and economic functions (Art. 385, paragraph I). Law No. 1333 states that these areas are declared under the protection of the State (Art. 60), constituting State property and of public and social interest (Art. 61). However, neither law establishes any restrictions regarding mining activities.
Art. 220 of Law No. 535 states in paragraph I that mining activities may be carried out within protected areas and forest reserves, provided they do not affect the area's protection objectives. In the same article, paragraph II implicitly recognizes mining activities that were granted before the creation of the protected areas, as it instructs that these activities must comply with environmental regulations.

Article 33 of the General Regulations for Protected Areas (RGAP) stipulates that in exceptional cases, and when declared to be of national interest by Supreme Decree (D.S.), the exploitation of non-renewable resources will be permitted. If there is a risk of altering the objectives for which the area was created, a law will be required. Following this guideline, of the 24 nationally protected areas, only 15 (Noel Kempff Mercado National Park, Beni Biological Station Reserve, El Palmar Integrated Management Natural Area, Serranía del Iñao National Park and Integrated Management Natural Area, Manuripi Amazonian Wildlife Reserve, Pilón Lajas Biosphere Reserve, San Matías Integrated Management Natural Area, Amboro National Park and Integrated Management Natural Area, Sama Biosphere Reserve, Kaa Iya del Gran Chaco National Park and Integrated Management Natural Area, Serranía del Aguarague National Park and Integrated Management Natural Area, Madidi National Park and Integrated Management Natural Area, Cotapata National Park and Integrated Management Natural Area, Toro Toro National Park, Apolobamba Integrated Management Natural Area, and El The regulations governing the creation of mining concessions in Chore prohibit the granting of new concessions; however, they do not explicitly mention "mining concessions." Only in eight of these concessions do the regulations allow for the exploitation of mineral resources, among other activities, provided that a declaration of national interest is made through an express regulation.

With regard to Indigenous peoples, the Constitution recognizes Indigenous autonomies, as established in Article 30, paragraph 15, which stipulates that, in the case of exploitation of non-renewable natural resources within Indigenous territories, mandatory prior consultation must be guaranteed. Participation in this consultation is an exclusive competence of the Indigenous and Native Peasant Autonomies (Article 304, paragraph 21). Law No. 535, in Article 207, paragraph I, guarantees the right to prior, free, and informed consultation. Paragraph II excludes mining prospecting and exploration activities from prior consultation, and paragraph III states that prior consultation will only be carried out on new applications for mining administrative contracts. In other words, mining rights that are in the process of being adapted (known as ATEs) are not subject to prior consultation, as they are pre-established rights.

Article 208 of the same law, as indicated in the justification for Criterion 3, states that prior consultation does not imply a right of veto by indigenous communities. Paragraph III of the same article, which is supported by ILO Convention 169 and the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, does not grant those subject to prior consultation the right to veto the execution of mining activities. Article 15 of ILO Convention 169 states that, where possible, indigenous peoples should share in the benefits and receive compensation; and Article 32 of the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples states that consultations must be held to obtain the consent of indigenous peoples. However, neither of these latter regulations establishes that indigenous peoples may veto mining activities, leaving them defenseless because prior consultation is not binding.

Brazil_Justification: Although Brazil has a legal framework addressing mining in indigenous territories, protected areas, and community lands, and there are ongoing efforts to regulate these activities, significant gaps and challenges exist in practice. The lack of clear and comprehensive legislation, the contentious nature of prior consultation, and persistent illegal mining activities undermine the effective protection of these areas and the rights of their inhabitants. The legal framework establishes some conditions and restrictions, but its implementation is often insufficient, leading to ongoing environmental damage and social conflict.

Colombia_Justification:
The Colombian legal framework establishes specific conditions and restrictions for mining activities in indigenous territories, protected areas, and community lands. The 1991 Political Constitution, in Article 330, establishes that the exploitation of natural resources in indigenous territories must be carried out without affecting the cultural, social, and economic integrity of the communities and requires prior consultation.

The Mining Code, in Article 122, establishes the concept of "Indigenous Mining Zones," which are areas delimited by the mining authority within indigenous territories where communities have priority for carrying out mining activities. Furthermore, Decree 1076 of 2015, which regulates the environmental sector, establishes that an environmental license cannot be granted without the formalization of prior consultation when required [5].

Constitutional Court Ruling C-418 of 2002 reaffirmed the constitutionality of Article 122 of the Mining Code, consolidating the protection of the rights of indigenous communities in the mining context [6]. Regarding protected areas, the National System of Protected Areas (SINAP) establishes restrictions and prohibitions for mining activities in these areas, in order to protect biodiversity and ecosystems.

[6] Judgment C-418 of 2002. Available at: https://www.suin-juriscol.gov.co/viewDocument.asp?id=20025809
Peru_Justification: Peru has a robust legal framework for the protection of the rights of indigenous peoples and protected natural areas. Law No. 29785, the Law on the Right to Prior Consultation of Indigenous or Native Peoples, recognized in ILO Convention 169, establishes the obligation to conduct prior, free, and informed consultation before authorizing mining projects in indigenous territories. Legislative Decree No. 1100 prohibits mining in bodies of water and buffer zones. Furthermore, the Political Constitution of Peru (Art. 89) recognizes the ownership of peasant and native communities over their lands, which are inalienable, imprescriptible, and unseizable. Finally, Article 115.2 of the Regulations of the Law of Protected Natural Areas (Supreme Decree No. 038-2001-AG) establishes that: "The exploitation of non-renewable natural resources is incompatible with Protected Natural Areas of indirect use; except when there are acquired rights established by the legislation of the matter prior to the creation of the Area."

Ecuador_Justification:
The Ecuadorian legal system establishes the following constitutional and legislative conditions, restrictions, and prohibitions on mining activities:

Protected Areas: Article 407 of the Constitution (CRE) prohibits extractive activities in protected areas. The prohibition is: (i) absolute for metallic mining, in all its phases; and (ii) relative for non-metallic mining, since—as an exception based on national interest—it could be authorized by the National Assembly (legislative branch) at the request of the President of the Republic. This prohibition applies only to areas that are part of the National System of Protected Areas (SNAP), which covers approximately 20% of the national territory; and does not apply to areas that are part of other biodiversity or forest heritage conservation mechanisms.

Indigenous Territories and Community Lands: Unlike protected areas, mining activities can be carried out in these territories, subject to prior consultation. This is a right of citizen participation specifically recognized by Article 407 of the Constitution. Article 57, paragraph 7 of the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador (CRE) establishes prior, free, and informed consultation as a collective right of Indigenous communities, peoples, and nationalities. This right is exercised before the State adopts decisions regarding plans and programs for the prospecting, exploitation, and commercialization of non-renewable resources, including mineral resources, located on their lands and which may affect them environmentally or culturally. Article 3 of the Organic Law of Rural Lands and Ancestral Territories (LOTRTA) defines ancestral land and territory as “the physical space over which a community, commune, people, or nationality of ancestral origin has historically generated an identity based on social, cultural, and spiritual construction, developing economic activities and its own forms of production in a current and uninterrupted manner.”

Both the Mining Law (Article 90), the Organic Law of Citizen Participation (Article 83), and the Organic Environmental Code (Article 184) recognize the right to citizen participation in environmental matters. The Escazú Agreement (Art. 7) also commits Ecuador to act in support of this right. However, to date, no specific law has been enacted regarding this type of consultation. This legislative gap has caused confusion over time, which has been clarified over the years by the Constitutional Court of Ecuador (CCE) by establishing standards based on “genuine intercultural dialogue” and the “duty to accommodate,” whereby the State must be flexible to the interests at stake (Judgment No. 273-19-JP/22). In 2024, the national mining authority issued Ministerial Agreement MEM-MEM-2024-0002-AM, which contains a Manual for the operationalization of prior, free, and informed consultation for the issuance of administrative measures in mining concessions. This regulatory framework foresees two scenarios arising from intercultural dialogue: (i) that there is consent from the community, people, or indigenous nationality, in which case the project is implemented; or (ii) that such consent is not obtained. In this second scenario, Article 83 of the Organic Law on Citizen Participation applies, whereby the State must decide whether or not to implement the project. If it decides to implement it, Article 18 of the Manual indicates that its decision must be justified with reasons and criteria that warrant such implementation despite majority opposition.

Territories of Peoples in Voluntary Isolation: In the case of these territories, the penultimate paragraph of Article 57 of the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador (CRE) stipulates that “all types of extractive activity shall be prohibited.” These are intangible zones defined as protected areas of exceptional cultural importance and for the development of the Quichua, Siona, Cofán, and Huaorani indigenous communities, as established in Executive Decrees No. 551 and 552 issued in 1999.

Guyana_Justification: Guyana's legal framework provides some protections and specific conditions for mining in Indigenous territories, but these are not without significant limitations. The Amerindian Act 2006 requires that miners obtain the consent of at least two-thirds of the Village Council to operate on Village lands, which aligns with the principle of Free, Prior, and Informed Consent (FPIC). However, Section 50 of the same Act allows the government to override this requirement for large-scale mining projects if they are deemed to be in the "public interest," which represents a significant loophole that weakens Indigenous rights protections. The Mining Act also contains provisions related to Amerindian lands, but it is the Amerindian Act that provides the more detailed framework for consultation and consent. The existence of the "public interest" clause significantly undermines the overall protection afforded to Indigenous communities.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname's legal framework is currently in flux regarding the rights of Indigenous and Tribal communities. A proposed land rights bill has sparked controversy because it would replace the principle of Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) with a consultation process that does not grant communities decision-making power [1]. The bill would also allow the government to grant mining concessions on Indigenous and Tribal land if deemed in the “public interest.” This indicates that while there are discussions and proposed conditions, they do not yet provide clear and enforceable restrictions on mining activities in these territories.

enous-communities/
Venezuela_Justification:
The Venezuelan legal framework, both at the constitutional level and in special laws, establishes conditions, restrictions, and prohibitions for mining activities in these territorial areas.

Regarding Indigenous territories, it should be noted that the legislation does not recognize Indigenous "territories" in the strict sense, but rather "habitats and lands." These areas are currently undergoing demarcation, with few having been formally recognized by the State. Indigenous communities have demanded this demarcation, as stipulated in the Constitution (1999) and the Law on Demarcation and Guarantee of the Habitat and Lands of Indigenous Peoples (2001), as a mechanism to guarantee their right to collective ownership of their lands. For its part, the Organic Law of Indigenous Peoples and Communities (2005) establishes that any activity likely to directly or indirectly affect Indigenous peoples and communities must be consulted with them. Mining legislation, specifically Article 18 of Decree No. 2,165 (2015), reaffirms this principle by stating that the State will promote the effective participation of Indigenous peoples, communities, and organizations in matters regulated by this instrument, as well as in the formulation of public policies that affect them, when mining activities are carried out on Indigenous lands.

With respect to protected areas, the Venezuelan regulatory framework is even broader and more explicit: it expressly prohibits mining and other extractive activities within these zones. This legal framework encompasses Articles 127 and 128 of the Constitution, as well as the Partial Regulations of the Organic Law for the Management and Administration of National Parks and Natural Monuments (Decree No. 276 of 1989). It also includes the Law Approving the Washington Convention (1941), the Organic Law of the Environment, the Organic Law for Territorial Planning, the Environmental Criminal Law, the Wildlife Law, the Forestry, Soil and Water Law, the Biological Diversity Law, the Forestry Law, the Water Law, and the Environmental Protection Regulations, among others.

Note: The problem in Venezuela lies not in the absence of legislation, but in the State's ineffectiveness in enforcing it. For at least fifteen years, the Government itself has engaged in prevarication by omission, by not acting against malicious activities in these spaces, by evading the proper consultation with local populations and the current territorial planning, as well as by omitting the environmental and sociocultural impact assessment studies required by law as a prerequisite to the start of any activity likely to degrade the environment; or by directly promoting or tolerating the violation of these rules in protected areas.

Categories: Ownership and Allocation of Mineral Rights
Question: Does the law allocate ownership rights over minerals, including gold?
Bolivia_Justification:
It should be clarified that Article 5 of Law No. 535, mentioned in the justification for Criterion 4, corresponds to the principles established in the law. This article does not establish that mineral resources are administered by the State. It is in Article 2, paragraph I, that it is established that mineral resources existing in the soil or subsoil are the direct property and domain of the Bolivian people and are administered by the State.
Article 369, paragraph I of the Bolivian Constitution states verbatim, “The State shall be responsible for the mineral wealth found in the soil and subsoil, whatever its origin, and its application shall be regulated by law.” Therefore, the law does assign property rights over minerals, including gold.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Political Constitution of the State. Available at: https://diputados.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/cpe.pdf
 - Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
Brazil_Justification: In Brazil, the Federal Constitution, specifically Article 176, clearly establishes that mineral resources, including gold, are distinct from the soil and belong to the Union. This means that private landowners do not have inherent property rights over the minerals found on their land. The exploration and exploitation of these resources can only be carried out with the authorization or concession of the Union, according to the law. The law also guarantees the concessionaire ownership of the product of the mining.

Constitution of the Federative Republic of Brazil of 1988. Available at: http://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/constituicao/constituicao.htm
 Decree-Law No. 227, of February 28, 1967 (Mining Code). Available at: https://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/decreto-lei/del0227.htm
Colombia_Justification:
Colombian legislation clearly assigns property rights to minerals, including gold. The 1991 Political Constitution, in Article 332, establishes that "The State owns the subsoil and non-renewable natural resources" [4]. This provision is further developed in the Mining Code (Law 685 of 2001), which, in Article 5, reaffirms that "Minerals of any kind and location, lying in the soil or subsoil, in any natural physical state, are the exclusive property of the State" [1].

The Mining Code also establishes that state ownership of mineral resources is inalienable and imprescriptible, and that the right to explore and exploit these resources can only be acquired through a mining title granted by the State. This means that, although the State owns the minerals, it can grant private parties the right to exploit them through a concession contract.
Colombia_Sources: Law 685 of 2001 (Mining Code). Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=9202 Political Constitution of Colombia of 1991.
Peru_Justification: Article 66 of the Political Constitution of Peru establishes that natural resources, both renewable and non-renewable, are the patrimony of the Nation. The State is sovereign in their exploitation. The General Mining Law (Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM) establishes that the State grants mining concessions that confer upon their holder the right to explore and exploit the granted mineral resources. Therefore, while the State owns the minerals, the law assigns exploitation rights to the concession holders.

Ecuador_Justification: Article 408 of the Constitution (CRE) establishes that non-renewable natural resources, including mineral deposits, “are the inalienable, imprescriptible, and unseizable property of the State.” In accordance with this, Article 16 of the Mining Law reiterates that the State’s ownership of the subsoil “shall be exercised independently of the right of ownership over the surface lands covering the mines and deposits.” Similarly, Article 607 of the Civil Code states that the State “owns all mines and deposits…”.

Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador.
Mining Law of Ecuador.
Guyana_Justification: The legal framework in Guyana is unequivocal regarding the ownership of mineral resources. The Mining Act 1989, in Section 6, explicitly states that all minerals, including gold, are vested in the State. This principle establishes clear state ownership over all mineral resources within Guyana's territory. While the State retains ownership, it grants licenses and permits for the exploration and extraction of these resources through the Guyana Geology and Mines Commission, creating a clear and well-defined system for mineral allocation that is consistent with international practice.

Suriname_Justification:
The Mining Decree of 1986 explicitly states that all minerals within the territory of Suriname, including its territorial sea and subsoil, are the property of the state [1]. 
This provision clearly allocates the ownership of all minerals, including gold, to the state.
Suriname_Sources: Mining Decree 1986, Article 2, paragraph 2
Venezuela_Justification: In Venezuela, ownership of minerals is the exclusive property of the State. The Constitution establishes that all mineral and hydrocarbon deposits within the national territory, regardless of their nature, belong to the Republic, and that this ownership is inalienable and imprescriptible. The exploration and exploitation of gold and other strategic minerals has been reserved for the State or for associations with majority state participation, as stipulated by Decree No. 2,165 of 2015.

Categories: Ownership and Allocation of Mineral Rights
Question: Are there transparent and publicly accessible registries of mining titles, licenses, or concessions for ASM (mining cadastres)?
Bolivia_Justification:
Article 40, paragraph I, point a. of Law No. 535 grants the Mining Authority (AJAM) the power to administer the Mining Registry, as well as the Mining Cadastre and Grid. Indeed, the AJAM has a computerized Mining Cadastre and Grid system for all mining areas, Special Economic Zones (ATEs), immobilized areas, reserved areas, and those under application. However, this system is not publicly accessible, and the information is handled with strict confidentiality by the AJAM.
The lack of access to this information violates the Bolivian Constitution, specifically Article 21, point 6, which states that one of the rights of Bolivians is the right "to information, to interpret it, to analyze it, and to communicate it freely, individually or collectively." Furthermore, Supreme Decree No. 28168 guarantees access to information as a fundamental right (Article 1). Furthermore, it violates the rights of access to information recognized by the Escazú Agreement, of which Bolivia is a party.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, in Brazil, the National Mining Agency (ANM) is the main government body responsible for regulating and supervising mining activities. A fundamental instrument for transparency and public access to information on mining titles, licenses, and concessions is the Mining Registry. This official system, maintained by the ANM, provides public data on mining processes throughout the country.
Brazil_Sources: National Mining Agency - Mining Registry. Available at: https://www.gov.br/anm/pt-br/acesso-a-informacao/servicos/cadastro-mineiro
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia maintains transparent and publicly accessible records of mining titles, including the National Mining Registry, an online system managed by the National Mining Agency (ANM) [3]. This system allows anyone to consult information on current mining titles, their location, holder, area, and status.

In addition to the Mining Registry, the Colombian Mining Information System (SIMCO) provides statistics and data on the mining sector, contributing to transparency [8]. Decree 1993 of 2002 establishes SIMCO as the official source of information for the sector, and Law 1712 of 2014 (Transparency and Access to Public Information Law) guarantees access to this information [9, 10].

These public information systems enable social oversight of the allocation of mining rights and contribute to transparency in the management of the sector.

Law 1712 of 2014. Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=56884
Peru_Justification: Yes, the Geological, Mining and Metallurgical Institute (INGEMMET) of Peru manages the national mining registry, which is publicly accessible through its website. The GEOCATMIN and E-Mining Registry tools allow anyone to consult information about mining concessions, including their location, owner, and status. The Comprehensive Registry of Mining Formalization (REINFO) is also publicly accessible and allows users to check the status of miners undergoing the formalization process.

Ecuador_Justification:
Yes, a registry and inventory of mining concessions exists, as stipulated by the Mining Law (Article 9, section d) and its Regulations (Articles 9 to 13), and is managed by the Mining Regulation and Control Agency (ARCOM). The Mining Law mandates its publication through digital and electronic means, which is publicly and freely accessible via a geoportal available on the ARCOM website.

According to Resolution No. 001-DE-ARCOM-2018, the mining inventory was closed in February 2018, as it was deemed “necessary to have a plan for areas suitable for mining exploration and exploitation, prioritizing the rational use of natural resources, consistent with the creation of new development zones under the principle of regional balance.”

After seven years of closure, the mining registry was reopened in June of this year, 2025. The reopening is stipulated in Agreement No. MEM-MEM-2025-0018-AM and has been implemented gradually and progressively, beginning with the small-scale non-metallic mining sector.

Within this framework, emphasis has been placed on strengthening access to public information, as reflected in Resolution No. ARCOM-ARCOM-2025-0035-R, which mandates all ARCOM personnel to comply with transparency functions and procedures (active, passive, targeted, and collaborative), ensuring accountability, citizen access to information, and social oversight in the mining sector.

Guyana_Justification: Guyana currently lacks a transparent and publicly accessible registry of mining titles, licenses, or concessions specifically for Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASM). While the Guyana Geology and Mines Commission provides an interactive map for medium and large-scale mineral tenures through the GGMC Mineral Tenure StoryMap, it explicitly states that small-scale mining tenure data is not publicly available. The Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI) has noted the absence of a publicly available cadastre system for mining in Guyana, particularly for small-scale operations. This lack of transparency for ASM represents a significant governance gap that hinders accountability and public oversight of the sector.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname does not have a publicly accessible mining concession registry. The Geology and Mining Department (GMD) processes mining rights applications, but there is no systematic register that displays the status or outcome of these applications. While there are efforts to improve transparency in the extractive sector, the current system does not provide a public access to mining concession information.

Venezuela_Justification: While a Single Mining Registry (RUM) exists, compiling information on miners, their associations, and the mine cadastre, it is for the exclusive internal use of the Ministry of People's Power for Ecological Mining Development and is not available for public consultation. In practice, these registries are neither transparent nor accessible; the government often invokes security and strategic value reasons to keep this information confidential, which opens the door to potential political manipulation.

Categories: Ownership and Allocation of Mineral Rights
Question: Is there an official and up-to-date mining registry that includes information allowing authorities to prevent overlapping concessions with existing rights, such as other titles, protected areas, or indigenous lands?
Bolivia_Justification: The AJAM has a Mining Registry system, as well as a Mining Cadastre and Grid, which is constantly updated; what does not exist is an interconnected system between the AJAM and SERNAP, for example, to avoid or consider overlap with protected areas. However, since approximately 2010, when SERGEOMIN was in charge of administering the Mining Registry as well as the Mining Cadastre and Grid, information was exchanged between both institutions. Along the same lines, in 2015 an inter-institutional agreement was signed between SERNAP and the AJAM, which established the exchange of information. Thus, when requesting the certificate of free mining area, the AJAM can immediately verify if there is an overlap with protected areas, an aspect that is reported in the aforementioned certificate. Furthermore, overlaps with other mining rights (contracts, ATEs) or reserves are checked. If such an overlap exists with another mining right or application, the mining area certificate will indicate that the area is not free, and therefore, a mining administrative contract cannot be requested. It is likely that during the identification of stakeholders for prior consultation, the corresponding unit of the AJAM will check for overlaps with indigenous lands; however, since this information is not publicly available, this cannot be confirmed.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Firsthand knowledge of the procedure for obtaining certificates of free mining areas and work activities carried out at SERNAP.
 - Mining Registry Regulations. Available at: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/index.php/reglamentos-2/
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil has an official and up-to-date mining registration system, SIGMINE (Mining Geographic Information System), which is designed to avoid overlapping concessions with existing rights, such as other titles, protected areas, or indigenous lands. SIGMINE is a geospatial tool that allows the visualization and analysis of data on mining processes throughout the national territory.
Brazil_Sources: National Mining Agency - SIGMINE. Available at: https://www.gov.br/anm/pt-br/acesso-a-informacao/servicos/sistema-de-informacoes-geograficas-da-mineracao-sigmine
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has an official and up-to-date mining registry that prevents overlapping concessions. The National Mining Registry, administered by the National Mining Agency (ANM), uses a georeferenced coordinate system that allows for the precise delimitation of mining title areas.

Article 16 of the Mining Code establishes the principle of "first in time, first in right," meaning that concession applications take precedence according to the order in which they are submitted. Before granting a new title, the ANM verifies any overlap with existing titles, protected areas, and indigenous territories to avoid conflicts of rights.

The National Mining Registry, regulated by Article 78 of the Mining Code, is the instrument that provides publicity and authenticity to acts and contracts related to mining rights, thus guaranteeing legal certainty and preventing overlapping concessions.
Colombia_Sources:
Law 685 of 2001 (Mining Code). Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=9202
National Mining Agency (ANM). Available at: https://www.anm.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: While INGEMMET's mining registry is a powerful tool that helps prevent overlapping mining concessions, the existence of socio-environmental conflicts and overlapping rights in practice suggests that the system is not infallible. Overlap of concessions with protected natural areas and indigenous territories has been a recurring problem in Peru. Although efforts have been made to improve coordination among different government entities, overlapping rights remain a challenge.
Peru_Sources: GEOCATMIN: https://geocatmin.ingemmet.gob.pe/geocatmin/
Ecuador_Justification:
Article 11 of the Mining Regulations stipulates that the mining cadastre must include the registration of mining concession titles, as well as areas "off-limits or restricted to mining activity" and other territorial aspects.

Additionally, Article 22 of the Annex to Resolution No. ARCOM-005/25, issued in June of this year, which contains the Instructions for the preparation of cadastral reports and certifications, requires that the cadastral report examine specific overlaps with: intangible zones, protected areas, water protection areas, forest heritage, and protective forests.

This specificity is consistent with Ecuadorian environmental legislation and encompasses areas where mining is prohibited (protected areas and intangible zones) and others where mining is permitted, albeit with stricter regulations and controls (forest heritage and protective forests). Article 11 of the Annex to this Resolution also includes in the overlap examination territories held in the ancestral possession and ownership of communities, communes, peoples, or nationalities. The geo portal available on the ARCOM website does allow you to view the overlapping areas, although indigenous territories are not yet included.
Ecuador_Sources:
General Regulations of the Mining Law.
ARCOM. Resolution No. ARCOM-005/25.
Guyana_Justification: Guyana has an official and up-to-date mining registry through the GGMC Mineral Tenure StoryMap, which is publicly accessible and provides comprehensive information on mineral tenures, protected areas, and Amerindian titled lands. The Mining Act 1989, specifically Section 26, prohibits the granting of overlapping licenses for the same mineral, providing a legal framework to prevent conflicts. The GGMC StoryMap integrates different land use data and indicates that a 1km buffer zone is maintained around Amerindian Titled Lands and Protected Areas to prevent encroachment. This system effectively integrates spatial data to prevent overlapping concessions and represents a positive feature of Guyana's mining governance framework.

Suriname_Justification: While Suriname has a GLIS register for mining and forest concessions which is not public, the National REDD+ Strategy of Suriname explicitly states that there is "no checkpoint to identify overlaps" between these concessions and other land uses [1]. This indicates a significant gap in the system, as it does not effectively prevent overlapping concessions with protected areas or Indigenous lands. Reports also highlight instances of concessions being granted on Indigenous lands and illegal mining occurring in these territories, further demonstrating the lack of preventative measures [2, 3]. There is a National Land Monitoring System of Suriname where concesssion are visualized. however the information is not up to date  for some years.

Venezuela_Justification: The Ministry responsible for mining approved Resolution No. 0010 of May 14, 2018, which created the Single Mining Registry (RUM). According to information available on the official website, this registry operates through the platform called the Integrated System for the Management of Ecological Mining Development, designed to centralize the data of individuals or legal entities, public or private, that carry out mining, related, or auxiliary activities, although its stated purpose is to simplify procedures for miners before the State.

Question: Is there a formalization process in place for ASM workers operating without a formal permit, license and/or concession?
Bolivia_Justification: Bolivia lacks regulations establishing formalization procedures for mining activities, especially for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), operating without permits, licenses, or formal concessions. Activities operating without licenses, authorizations, or concessions are considered illegal mining under Law No. 535, Article 104. Furthermore, Article 232 ter of the Penal Code stipulates that the penalty for exploiting minerals without authorization or granted rights is imprisonment for four to eight years. Therefore, there are also no procedures in place for the formalization of ASM workers.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
 - Internal Regulations on Illegal Mining – AJAM. Available at: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/Reglamento_Interno_Mineria_Ilegal
 - Law No. 367, amending the Penal Code. Available at: https://diputados.gob.bo/leyes/ley-no-367/
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil has a formalization process for artisanal mining workers who operate without permission, license, and/or formal concession.

The main mechanism is the Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG), established by Law No. 7,805 of July 18, 1989, and regulated by the National Mining Agency (ANM). The PLG grants mining rights to individuals or cooperatives of artisanal miners to exploit mineable mineral substances such as gold in previously delimited areas, provided that environmental, labor, and safety regulations are respected.

Additionally, Decree No. 9,406 of June 12, 2018, which regulates the Mining Code (Decree-Law No. 227/1967), reinforces the legal framework for artisanal mining activity and defines the administrative procedures for requesting and maintaining the PLG with the ANM.

In 2017, Decree No. 9,406 also established the Artisanal Mining Permit Registry (RPLG) and enabled the integration of information into the ANM's Mining Registry System, which facilitates monitoring and transparency of the process.

 Decree No. 9,406, of June 12, 2018 – Regulates the Mining Code and provides for the PLG regime. Available at: https://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/_ato2015-2018/2018/decreto/D9406.htm
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has established specific processes for the formalization of artisanal and small-scale (ASS) miners operating informally. Decree 1666 of 2016, which classifies mining by scale, is an important step in this direction, as it recognizes the existence of subsistence mining and establishes a framework for its formalization.

The Ministry of Mines and Energy has developed mining formalization programs that aim to integrate informal miners into the legal economy, offering technical assistance, training, and support in obtaining mining titles and environmental licenses. The Mining Code also regulates artisanal and small-scale mining (barequeo) as a traditional activity that can be formalized.

These formalization processes are fundamental to improving the living conditions of ASS miners, reducing the environmental and social impacts of informal mining, and strengthening governance in the sector.

Peru_Justification:
Yes. Peru has a comprehensive mining formalization process aimed at artisanal and small-scale miners (ASM) who operate without a formal title, license, or concession. This process was created by Legislative Decree No. 1293 (2016), which established the Comprehensive Mining Formalization Registry (REINFO). Registration in this registry allows informal miners to continue their activities temporarily while they meet the technical, environmental, labor, and tax requirements for full formalization.

The formalization process is also regulated by complementary regulations, such as Supreme Decree No. 018-2017-EM (which approves the regulations of Legislative Decree 1293) and its amendments, which establish procedures for registration, verification, and exclusion. It also includes the involvement of regional governments, which are responsible for evaluating and approving Environmental Management Instruments for Formalization (IGAFOM).

Ecuador_Justification:
The National Mining Development Plan 2020-2030 promotes the formalization of mining activities, and the State has therefore implemented formalization processes focused on small-scale mining. Furthermore, since the reopening of the mining registry, attention has been given, particularly to mining applications that had been backlogged since its closure.

However, it is important to clarify that Ecuador prioritizes the regularization of this activity over its formalization, as Ecuadorian law considers mining without authorization to be illegal.

Therefore, when the Mining Law was enacted, the Regulations for the Special Regime for Small-Scale Mining were also issued (2009); and subsequently, the Instructions for Granting Mining Concessions were issued (2014). In July of this year, the State also issued Agreement No. MEM-MEM-2025-0023-AM to amend the 2014 Instructions.

Guyana_Justification: Guyana has established a formalization process for ASM workers, but it faces implementation challenges and is not fully comprehensive. The National Mineral Sector Policy Framework and Actions, 2019-2029 recognizes the need for a national policy on artisanal mining and a plan for regularization. The government has promoted the formation of syndicates to help informal miners gain legal access to mining grounds, and the GGMC provides guidance on the licensing process. However, the policy framework also acknowledges the challenges that small-scale miners face in terms of compliance, suggesting that the formalization process is not as effective as it could be. The lack of a clear, step-by-step process specifically designed for individual informal miners to transition to formal operations represents a notable gap in the current system.

small-scale mining (ASM) workers operating without formal permits, licenses, or concessions could be identified. While there are discussions and projects aimed at improving the ASGM
sector, a defined process for formalization of existing informal miners is not readily apparent in the consulted sources [1, 2, 3].

Venezuela_Justification:
The legal framework applicable to this process is Decree No. 2,165. However, it is imperative to point out that its implementation largely responds to political criteria: small-scale miners are offered future legalization as an incentive to join government-controlled mining brigades.

Until approximately 2019, the model promoted by government agencies was the cooperative; however, this scheme was modified. Article 25 of Decree No. 2,165 establishes the possibility of creating "mining brigades" for carrying out small-scale mining activities. In February 2025, the Partial Regulations of the Organic Law Decree that Reserves to the State the Activities of Exploration and Exploitation of Gold and other Strategic Minerals regarding Mining Brigades (Decree No. 5,097, of February 24, 2025) were approved, which regulate the operation of these brigades. Article 26 of Decree No. 2,165 empowers these groups to carry out procedures and enter into service contracts in accordance with applicable legal provisions.

Question: Are there clear procedures for acquiring rights on ASM for gold?
Bolivia_Justification: It should be clarified that mining regulations do not discriminate based on the type of activity or mineral to be exploited, and that small-scale mining (ASM) is not defined in the regulations. The procedures for acquiring mining rights are the same for all mining producers (MPs) in the mining sector. The procedure for acquiring mining rights is established in the Regulations for Granting and Terminating Mining Rights (approved by Ministerial Resolution No. 023/2015), which sets out the procedure for obtaining mining rights through administrative mining contracts (AMCs) for exploitation activities, and licenses for prospecting, exploration, operation, and/or commercialization of minerals. Furthermore, the aforementioned regulations differentiate the types of requirements according to the mining producer (MP), identifying three types: cooperatives, private companies, and state-owned mining.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Regulations on the Granting and Termination of Mining Rights. Available at: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/index.php/reglamentos-2/
 - Ministerial Resolution No. 023/2015 dated January 30, 2015. Available at: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/index.php/resoluciones-ministeriales-y-bi-ministerial/
 - Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil has clear procedures for acquiring artisanal and small-scale mining rights for gold. The main instrument for this is the Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG), which is a regime for extracting mineral substances with immediate exploitation of the mineral deposit. The PLG is granted by the National Mining Agency (ANM) and is intended for Brazilians or cooperatives of artisanal miners.

The process of obtaining a PLG is digital and transparent, through the ANM's Digital Protocol. The main steps include: Application for available area via SIGMINE/SIPLOM, accompanied by the georeferenced delimitation of the polygon; Technical and legal analysis by the ANM, which verifies overlap with other mining titles or protected areas; Issuance of the PLG title, valid for up to 5 years, renewable, provided that the legal and environmental conditions are maintained; Registration and monitoring in the ANM's Mining Registry System, which ensures transparency and traceability; Compliance with environmental requirements, including obtaining specific licenses and permits from the relevant environmental agencies (municipal, state, or federal).
Brazil_Sources:
National Mining Agency - Small-Scale Mining Permit. Available at: https://www.gov.br/anm/pt-br/assuntos/exploracao-mineral/regimes-de-exploracao-mineral/permissao-de-lavra-garimpeira
 National Mining Agency – Mining Registry and Digital Protocol. Available at: https://www.gov.br/anm/pt-br/assuntos/cadastro-mineiro
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has clear and specific procedures for acquiring artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) rights. The Mining Code, in Chapter VII, establishes the procedures for submitting concession proposals, and Article 271 specifically regulates small-scale mining.

Decree 1666 of 2016 complements this regulation, establishing differentiated procedures for subsistence mining and small-scale mining. The National Mining Agency (ANM) has published guides and manuals detailing the steps to follow to apply for a mining title and has implemented an online system for submitting applications, facilitating access to the procedures.

These clear and differentiated procedures are essential to promote the formalization of ASM and ensure that operations are carried out legally and responsibly.

Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=77883 National Mining Agency (ANM). Available at: https://www.anm.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: Yes, there are clear procedures for acquiring mining rights for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) in Peru. These procedures are established in the General Mining Law and its regulations, and are managed by INGEMMET. The formalization process through REINFO also establishes a clear path for informal miners to obtain their rights. All mining rights, including those for ASM, are acquired through a mining concession granted by INGEMMET or by the Regional Governments with delegated authority (Articles 9 and 10 of the Consolidated Text of the General Mining Law, Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM).

Ecuador_Justification: Yes, there are clear procedures and they are stated in articles 134 to 141 of the Mining Law and developed by the Regulation of the Special Regime of Small Mining and Artisanal Mining; and, detailed in two Instructions from 2014 and 2017 respectively for the granting of metallic and non-metallic mining concessions in small mining.

Guyana_Justification: Guyana has established a legal framework for acquiring mining rights for ASM through the Mining Act 1989 and regulations administered by the GGMC, but the procedures are not as clear and accessible as they could be. The Mining Act provides the general framework for obtaining licenses and permits, and the GGMC website contains information about licensing procedures. However, the detailed regulations and step-by-step procedures that would provide full clarity on the acquisition process for ASM gold rights are not always easily accessible to potential applicants. The lack of a clear definition of "artisanal mining" in the legal framework, as noted in Criterion 2, also creates some ambiguity in the application process. While the basic legal structure exists, the practical accessibility and clarity of procedures for ASM operators could be significantly improved.

Suriname_Justification: While there is a general application process for mining rights that begins at the Geological and Mining Department (GMD) and is identified with a serial number [1], and formal small and medium-scale mining is based on licenses assigned by the Ministry of Natural Resources (Min NH) under the Mining Decree 1986 [2], there are indications that these procedures may not be entirely clear or accessible for all artisanal and small-scale miners. Many small-scale miners reportedly operate without permits [3], suggesting that the existing procedures might not be sufficiently clear or tailored to facilitate formal acquisition of rights for all ASM.

Venezuela_Justification:
The acquisition of rights to engage in small-scale mining—a modality that includes artisanal mining—is carried out through a mechanism that allows small-scale miners to partner with the State to legally exploit gold resources. This mechanism consists of forming Strategic Production Alliances with the State, within which environmental regulations, workplace safety standards, and the elimination of intermediaries are established, with the aim of selling the extracted gold directly to the Central Bank of Venezuela.

The procedure comprises the following stages:

Formalization: Small-scale miners must organize themselves into cooperatives, mining brigades, or other associative forms of what is known as "people's power."

Strategic Alliances: They must formalize a request for intent to form a strategic alliance with the Venezuelan State, submitting the required documentation to the corresponding state-owned company, primarily the Venezuelan Mining Corporation (CVM).

Technical Documentation: A Descriptive Mining Report is required, which includes: information about the organization; territorial and geographical aspects; Mining and processing plan using environmentally friendly technologies; economic and marketing aspects; mine closure plan; and occupational safety measures.
Sale and Marketing: The produced mineral must be sold to the State, complying with the transport guidelines and the sworn declaration of origin.
Oversight: The Ministry of People's Power for Ecological Mining Development, along with other State entities, must carry out oversight, monitoring, surveillance, and control to guarantee responsible mining and environmental remediation.

Question: Is there a legal framework that requires the buyer of gold to verify its legal origin?
Bolivia_Justification: The law requires that all gold purchases and sales be declared and registered in an official system. Specifically, Article 87, paragraph 12 of Law 535 establishes SENARECOM's responsibility to "verify the origin of minerals and metals traded in the domestic market." Additionally, the law stipulates that a Trading License issued by the mining authority is required to engage in the trading of minerals, and that traders must register with SENARECOM for control purposes. Therefore, the only legal buyers of gold are those registered with SENARECOM (trading companies, industrial jewelers that purchase raw materials, authorized financial institutions, etc.). Informal or occasional buyers without a license cannot, in practice, operate legally; if they do, they will be engaging in illegal trade.
Bolivia_Sources: Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy
Brazil_Justification: In the judgment of Direct Actions of Unconstitutionality 7273 and 7345, the Brazilian Supreme Court declared unconstitutional the presumption of the legality of gold and the good faith of the buyer, reestablishing their duty of due diligence. In other words, since 2025, Brazilian regulations explicitly require that companies purchasing gold (such as securities and exchange dealers – DTVMs) conduct due diligence on the origin of the gold. Furthermore, the Brazilian government has adopted complementary measures: for example, since 2023 the Federal Revenue Service has required the use of a specific Electronic Invoice for the commercialization of gold, and geochemical tracking of gold is being implemented to identify its geological origin. The obligation of verification falls on formal buyers (securities and exchange dealers), since any informal buyer would be operating illegally.
Brazil_Sources:
https://noticias.stf.jus.br/postsnoticias/stf-invalida-norma-que-estabelecia-presuncao-de-boa-fe-no-comercio-de-ouro/
 
 https://www.gov.br/secom/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/03/agu-garante-fiscalizacao-mais-rigorosa-sobre-comercio-de-ouro#:~:text=Other%20measures%20already%20adopted%20by,Photo%3A%20Beto%20Nociti%2FBCB
Colombia_Justification: In Colombia, the obligation to verify the legal origin of gold is established in the regulations governing the marketing of minerals. Article 112 of Law 1450 of 2011 (National Development Plan) mandated the implementation of controls on the marketing of minerals and the creation of a registry of authorized agents. To comply with this, Decree 276 of 2015 (later incorporated into Single Decree 1073 of 2015) regulated the Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM) and the use of the Certificate of Origin. Consequently, since 2015, all gold buyers must ensure that the mineral comes from an authorized miner or trader and has a certificate of origin, in accordance with current mining regulations. Thus, anyone who buys gold, whether formally or informally, can only acquire it from accredited legal sources, under penalty of engaging in illicit trade.
Colombia_Sources:
Law 1450 of 2011 (National Development Plan)
Decree 276 of 2015
Peru_Justification: In Peru, both the General Mining Law and special regulations for combating illegal mining require gold buyers to verify its legal origin before purchasing it. The General Mining Law (Legislative Decree 708 of 1991), in Article 4, expressly states that “the buyer is obligated to verify the origin of the mineral substances,” and that purchasing from an unauthorized person makes the buyer responsible. This principle was reinforced by recent legislation: Legislative Decree No. 1107 of 2012, enacted to combat illegal mining, establishes in Article 11 that “every purchaser of mining products [...] must verify their origin, requesting the corresponding documents” and confirming their authenticity in official information systems.
Peru_Sources:
General Mining Law: https://www.osinergmin.gob.pe/seccion/centro_documental/Institucional/Estudios_Economicos/Libros/Libro_Panorama_de_la_Mineria_en_el_Peru.pdf

Legislative Decree No. 1107 of 2012
Ecuador_Justification: Pursuant to Article 50 of the Mining Law, individuals or legal entities who, without holding mining concessions, engage in the commercialization or export of metallic mineral substances or the export of non-metallic mineral substances, must obtain the corresponding license from the relevant Ministry, in accordance with the provisions of the general regulations of this law. Mining concession holders who trade metallic mineral substances or export non-metallic mineral substances from areas outside their concessions must also obtain the same license. By virtue of Article 55, any trade conducted without the required license is considered clandestine.
Ecuador_Sources: Mining Law, No. 45/2009
Guyana_Justification: There is a strict legal framework in Guyana that requires the verification of the legal origin of gold. The sale and purchase of gold are heavily regulated under the Guyana Gold Board Act, Chapter 66:01 and the Mining Act of 1989. These regulations ensure that all gold mined in Guyana is sold only through authorized, licensed, and traceable channels, requiring sellers to prove they have produced the gold legally.
Guyana_Sources: https://omaigoldmines.com/guyana-1/mining-act-1/#:~:text=The%20Guyana%20Mining%20Act%20of,all%20mineral%20title%20in%20Guyana.
Suriname_Justification: There is no structured system that promotes traceability where the gold originates from in ASM. Gold buyers may lawfully purchase gold only if it originates from a holder of a valid concession or small‑scale mining license. The Mining Decree does not explicitly say “buyers must conduct due diligence”, it makes legality of origin a condition for lawful trade, which in practice obliges buyers to verify that gold is sourced from licensed operations. However, according to the environmental law, it is mandatory for companies that purchase gold to further proces this, to register and obtain a certificatipn of registration from the National Environmental Authority.

Venezuela_Justification: The legal framework that reserves gold mining rights to the State—either directly or through joint ventures or strategic alliances—and mandates that all production be sold to the State, establishes a monopoly that, in theory, guarantees the legality of the gold acquired. In this system, the ultimate buyer is the State, so, formally, there should be no gold of illegal origin outside of what the State purchases.
Venezuela_Sources: Decree No. 2,165, with the Rank, Value and Force of an Organic Law, reserving to the State the activities of exploration and exploitation of gold and other strategic minerals. Official Gazette of the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela No. 6,210 Extraordinary of December 30, 2015.
