Question: Are specific environmental licensing procedures established for ASM, including simplified or tailored requirements?
Bolivia_Justification:
Review and/or validation: Current environmental regulations do not establish differentiated procedures for small-scale mining (ASM). However, Article 93 of the RAAM (Regulations for the Management of Mining Activities) defines minor mining activities with known but insignificant environmental impacts (AMIAC) as “underground mining activities located in unprotected areas of the Western Cordillera, the Altiplano, and the Eastern Cordillera on both flanks, comprising:
1) exploration, development, preparation, and exploitation through galleries (cut-offs and cross-cuts), squares, ramps, shafts, raises, and open pits with an extraction capacity equal to or less than three hundred (300) tons/month; and/or
2) mineral concentration on a scale equal to or less than 300 tons/month with one or more of the following processes: crushing and grinding (manual and mechanical), gravimetric and magnetic classification and concentration, sulfide separation by surface flotation, amalgamation, and drying, storage, and transportation of the resulting concentrates.”

The procedure through an AMIAC-EMAP is relatively simpler, as it involves completing a form established in the RAAM itself (Annex II of the RAAM). However, each Autonomous Departmental Government has added additional environmental requirements and documents, which, it should be noted, are not supported by environmental regulations. Nevertheless, these entities are justified under Law No. 031 on Autonomies.

Likewise, Supreme Decree No. 3856 of April 3, 2019, which modifies Supreme Decree No. No. 3549, includes the categorization list, in which the environmental category (1, 2, 3 or 4) that the activity, work or project (AOP) merits is established, according to certain technical aspects to be considered, in order to establish the type of regulatory instrument of particular scope (according to the environmental category) that must be carried out, it should be clarified that this pre-categorization does not depend on whether the AOP is or is not a MAPE.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities. Available at: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/149_DS_24782.pdf
 - Supreme Decree No. 3549. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
 - Supreme Decree No. 3856. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
 - Law No. 031 Framework Law on Autonomy and Decentralization. Available at: https://rpc.transparencia.gob.bo/normativas/7-Ley-031.pdf
 - Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil has established specific environmental licensing procedures for small-scale mining (MAPE), including simplified or adapted requirements. CONAMA Resolution No. 237/1997 establishes that environmental licensing for small-scale mining activities, such as MAPE, can be simplified at the discretion of the competent environmental agency. Article 12, § 1, expressly provides for the possibility of adopting simplified procedures for small-scale or low-polluting potential projects, at the discretion of the competent environmental agency. This legal provision is the normative basis for states and municipalities to adopt differentiated procedures for MAPE, avoiding the application of requirements incompatible with the scale of artisanal operations.
Brazil_Sources:
CONAMA Resolution No. 237, dated December 19, 1997. Available at: https://www.mma.gov.br/port/conama/res/res97/res23797.html
 Complementary Law No. 140, of December 8, 2011. Available at: http://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/leis/lcp/lcp140.htm
Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, there are differentiated procedures for environmental licensing of small-scale mining, but these are not yet fully simplified or adapted to the technical and economic capacities of artisanal and small-scale (ASM) miners.

Decree 1076 of 2015, Article 2.2.2.3.1.2, establishes the competent environmental authorities for granting licenses, including the National Environmental Licensing Authority (ANLA), the Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs), and the Sustainable Development Corporations, which may delegate this function to territorial entities with sufficient technical and administrative capacity. In the case of metallic mining, an environmental license is required when the projected removal of useful and waste material exceeds 2,000,000 tons per year, which excludes most artisanal and small-scale mining operations.

Despite this threshold and the existence of decentralized powers, the environmental procedures applicable to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) remain complex, with technical and economic requirements that are difficult for small-scale producers to meet. Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs) have implemented simplified assessment mechanisms and some support programs, but these are not standardized nationally nor do they constitute a formal simplified regime.

The Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development has issued guidance documents, such as the Environmental Guide for the Formalization of Subsistence Mining (2020), which seeks to adapt environmental requirements, although more specific regulations are still needed to facilitate the effective environmental formalization of ASM.
Colombia_Sources:
Decree 1076 of 2015 (Single Regulatory Decree for the Environment and Sustainable Development Sector), Article 2.2.2.3.1.2. Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=78153

Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development. “Environmental Guide for the Formalization of Subsistence Mining,” 2020. Available at: https://www.minambiente.gov.co/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/guia-formalizacion-mineria-subsistencia.pdf

National Environmental Licensing Authority – ANLA. Available at: https://www.anla.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: Yes, Peru has established specific and simplified environmental licensing procedures for small-scale mining (ASM). Legislative Decree No. 1105 created the Corrective Environmental Management Instrument (IGAC), applicable exclusively to ASM undergoing formalization. Legislative Decree No. 1293 (2016) and complementary regulations establish that miners registered in the REINFO (National Registry of Informal Mining Operators) must submit an IGAC or an Environmental Management Instrument for Formalization (IGAFOM). Supreme Decree No. 004-2012-MINAM approved the Complementary Provisions that regulate the Corrective Environmental Management Instrument - IGAC. The Environmental Management Instrument for the Formalization of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining Activities (IGAFOM) is an example of an environmental requirement adapted to the reality of ASM. The IGAFOM seeks to facilitate compliance with environmental regulations by small-scale miners.
Peru_Sources:
Ministry of the Environment (MINAM): https://www.gob.pe/minam
Legislative Decree No. 1105 (Legislative Decree establishing provisions for the formalization process of small-scale and artisanal mining activities)
https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H1053469
Legislative Decree No. 1336 (Legislative Decree establishing provisions for the comprehensive mining formalization process)
https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H1171584
Supreme Decree No. 038-2017-EM (Establishing Regulatory Provisions for the Environmental Management Instrument for the Formalization of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining Activities) (Artisanal)
https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H1192891
Ecuador_Justification:
Article 78 of the Mining Law (LM) establishes the general obligation to "prepare and submit environmental studies or documents to prevent, mitigate, control, and repair the environmental and social impacts arising from their activities," which will be "specific and simplified" for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

This general obligation is further developed in the Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities (RAAM), which establish the following parameters:

A mining title is required prior to applying for and obtaining an environmental administrative authorization, which may be: (i) an environmental registration, or (ii) an environmental license (Articles 8 and 9).

Projects, works, or activities within the special regime for artisanal mining will require an environmental registration (Article 10).

Projects, works, or activities within the small-scale mining regime:
To carry out exploration activities with or without test or reconnaissance boreholes, an environmental registration must be obtained.

To carry out exploration activities that exceed the number of test boreholes permitted in these regulations, an environmental license is required.

To carry out mining or exploration and simultaneous mining activities, as well as subsequent activities, an environmental license is required.

The RAAM (Environmental Authorization System for Mining) develops this scheme of mining environmental authorizations in accordance with the general scheme of environmental authorizations established in the Organic Environmental Code (COAM) and its Regulations (RCOAM). The COAM establishes the Single Environmental Management System (SUMA) to determine and regulate the procedures and mechanisms for the prevention, control, monitoring, and remediation of environmental pollution. Within this framework, environmental administrative authorizations are included as instruments of environmental regularization (Art. 177 COAM). These authorizations are of two types, depending on the categorization of the environmental impact or risk of the project, work, or activity: (i) low impact, through an environmental registration, or (ii) medium and high impact, through an environmental license (Art. 426 RCOAM).

The requirements for requesting these authorizations as well as the procedures for granting them are provided for in the RCOAM and the RAAM and are framed within the Single Environmental Information System (SUIA) which is the instrument that articulates the information on projects, works and activities that generate environmental risk or impact, which is administered by the national environmental authority (art. 19 COAM).
Ecuador_Sources:
Mining Law.
Organic Environmental Code.
Regulations to the Organic Environmental Code.
Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities.
Guyana_Justification: Guyana has established environmental guidelines and procedures that apply to ASM, but these are not always integrated into a simplified or specifically tailored licensing procedure for small-scale operations. Small-scale mining operations are typically exempt from the full Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) process required for larger operations, which can be considered a tailored requirement. The Guidelines for Environmental Mining Regulations (2004) provide detailed environmental management practices for all scales of mining, with some sections specifically addressing small-scale mines. However, the process for obtaining an environmental permit from the Environmental Protection Agency can still be complex for small-scale miners, and the integration between mining licenses and environmental permits could be better streamlined.

Suriname_Justification: Legislation applicabe to gold mining and its eviroinmental and health management is spread out over several laws and regulations. The Mining Decree of 1986 mentions the procedures to obtain license for  small scale mining, whereas the Environmental Framework Law of 2020 (and subsequent updates, including the shift to the National Environmental Authority or NMA) provides the legal basis for issuing environmental permits. However, according to the First National Action Plan on Artisinal and Small-Scale goldmining in Suriname, there is no specific legislation for the ASM sector.

Venezuela_Justification: The execution of any activity likely to degrade the environment — such as mining, regardless of its scale or modality — requires mandatory completion of an Environmental Impact Assessment Study, in accordance with the provisions of Decree No. 1,257: Standards on the Environmental Assessment of Activities Likely to Degrade the Environment (1996).
Venezuela_Sources: Decree No. 1,257 Regulations on Environmental Assessment of Activities Likely to Degrade the Environment. Official Gazette of the Republic of Venezuela No. 35,946 of April 25, 1996.

Question: Is there a national, regional, or state government entity that oversees compliance with the implementation of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) operations’ commitments in accordance with mining environmental licenses?
Bolivia_Justification:
Yes. In Bolivia, the Mining Administrative Jurisdictional Authority (AJAM) and the Ministry of Environment and Water (MMAyA) share oversight responsibilities. AJAM is responsible for monitoring the legality of mining activities, including artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), ensuring that operations comply with the Mining and Metallurgy Law (Law No. 535 of 2014) and the licenses granted.

For its part, MMAyA, through the Vice-Ministry of Environment, Biodiversity, Climate Change, and Forest Management and Development, and the Competent Environmental Authorities (AAC) at the departmental and municipal levels, is responsible for verifying compliance with environmental licenses and the commitments assumed under the Environmental Law (Law No. 1333) and its regulations.

In practice, the Competent Environmental Authorities are responsible for directly supervising compliance with environmental licenses, while the AJAM is responsible for enforcing mining titles and the legality of operations.
Bolivia_Sources:
Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy (2014)
 
 Law No. 1333 on the Environment (1992)
 
 Regulations on Environmental Prevention and Control (DS No. 24176);
 
 AJAM official website: [https://www.ajam.gob.bo](https://www.ajam.gob.bo).
Brazil_Justification:
In Brazil, the enforcement of environmental licenses for artisanal miners is a shared responsibility between federal and state agencies, depending on the location and scope of the activity.

The primary responsibility lies with the state environmental agencies, which are part of the National Environmental System (SISNAMA). Each state has its own institution to grant and oversee environmental licenses, such as the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) at the federal level, the Secretariat of Environment and Sustainability (SEMAS) in Pará, or the Acre Institute of Environment (IMAC). These agencies have the authority to issue, monitor, and, if necessary, suspend environmental licenses.

IBAMA acts directly when activities involve indigenous lands, federal conservation units, or more than one state, as well as in cases of serious environmental infractions with national repercussions.

The National Mining Agency (ANM) is responsible for overseeing the legality of mining operations, verifying whether they possess a valid Mining Permit (PLG) and whether they comply with the provisions of the Mining Code (Decree-Law No. 227/1967) and Law No. 7,805/1989, which establishes the mining regime.

In practice, the verification of environmental licenses falls to the competent environmental agency, whether state or federal, while the control of mining activity is the responsibility of the ANM. Both act in a complementary manner and may carry out joint operations with the Federal Police, the Federal Public Prosecutor's Office, and state agencies.
Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, the monitoring of compliance with environmental licenses by artisanal and small-scale miners (ASM) is the responsibility of several institutions, depending on the type of mining, the project's location, and the authority that granted the license.

The National Environmental Licensing Authority (ANLA) is responsible for granting, controlling, and monitoring environmental licenses for large-scale mining projects or those that extend beyond the boundaries of a single department. However, in the case of artisanal and small-scale mining, jurisdiction falls primarily to regional environmental authorities, such as the Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs), the Sustainable Development Corporations (CDSs), and the Urban Environmental Authorities, in accordance with Law 99 of 1993. On the other hand, the National Mining Agency (ANM) monitors the legality of mining operations, ensuring that miners have a valid mining title or contract and that they comply with the requirements established in the Mining Code (Law 685 of 2001) and in Decree 1666 of 2016, which regulates the formalization of small-scale mining.
Peru_Justification:
In Peru, the verification of compliance with environmental licenses by artisanal and small-scale miners (ASM) is the responsibility of different entities depending on the phase of mining activity and the relevant level of government.

The National Environmental Enforcement Authority (OEFA), an agency attached to the Ministry of the Environment (MINAM), is the institution in charge of supervising and penalizing compliance with environmental obligations in the mining sector. OEFA acts as the governing body of the National System for Environmental Assessment and Enforcement (SINEFA) and coordinates with regional entities to ensure the proper application of environmental regulations.

In the case of artisanal and small-scale mining, direct responsibility lies with the regional governments, which are responsible for granting and overseeing ASM environmental authorizations, permits, and licenses in accordance with Legislative Decree No. 1105 and Supreme Decree No. 018-2015-EM, which regulate the mining formalization process. These regional entities must verify compliance with the commitments made in the Environmental Management Instruments for the Formalization of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining Activities (IGAFOM), which are mandatory for legal operation.

The General Directorate of Mining Formalization (DGFM) of the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) oversees the formalization process and coordinates with regional governments and the OEFA (Environmental Assessment and Enforcement Agency) to ensure that miners registered in the Comprehensive Mining Formalization Registry (REINFO) comply with environmental and technical obligations.
Ecuador_Justification:
In Ecuador, the enforcement of environmental licenses by artisanal and small-scale miners (ASM) is the responsibility of several institutions that operate in a coordinated manner, depending on the level of activity and the stage of the mining process.

The main Competent Environmental Authority (CEA) is the Ministry of Environment, Water and Ecological Transition (MAATE), which oversees compliance with the environmental obligations established in the environmental license and environmental management instruments, in accordance with the provisions of the Organic Environmental Code (COA) and its regulations. MAATE may delegate control and monitoring functions to provincial or municipal Decentralized Autonomous Governments (GADs) that have assumed environmental responsibilities.

In the mining sector, the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MEM), through the Agency for Regulation and Control of Energy and Non-Renewable Natural Resources (ARCERNNR), is responsible for ensuring the legality of mining operations, including ASM activities. The ARCERNNR verifies that mining concession holders have the corresponding authorizations and permits and comply with the established technical and environmental commitments.

MAATE is responsible for environmental oversight, monitoring compliance with environmental management plans and the conditions established in the licenses, while the ARCERNNR oversees the technical and mining aspects. Both institutions can coordinate joint actions with the National Police, the Attorney General's Office, and local governments to penalize illegal activities and environmental damage.
Guyana_Justification: There are national, state-level (regional/agency) government entities in Guyana that oversee compliance with the environmental license commitments of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) operations. The primary regulator is the Guyana Geology and Mines Commission (GGMC), which works in conjunction with the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Guyana.
Guyana_Sources: https://eiti.org/countries/guyana#:~:text=The%20sector%20is%20mainly%20regulated,procedures%20for%20granting%20mineral%20agreements.
Suriname_Justification: The National Environmental Authority (NMA), previously known as the National Institute for Environment and Development in Suriname (NIMOS) is actively working on finalizing environmental regulations, and there are ongoing efforts to develop specific procedures for the artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM) sector [1, 2]. However, the available information does not clearly confirm the full establishment and widespread implementation of distinct, simplified, or tailored environmental licensing procedures specifically for ASM. While progress is being made, the current state suggests partial implementation rather than fully established and accessible procedures.
Suriname_Sources:
https://www.nmasuriname.org/Nieuws/post/NMA-overhandigt-registratiebewijzen-aan-goudopkoop-bedrijven
https://www.nmasuriname.org/Nieuws/post/regionale-conferentie-belicht-duurzame-alternatieven-voor-kwikgebruik-in-kleinschalige-goudmijnbouw
Ministry of Natural Resources of Suriname. Scoping Report. URL:
https://gov.sr/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/Scoping-Report-FINAL-Incl-Appendix.pdf
Venezuela_Justification:
The Ministries of People's Power for Ecological Mining Development (MPPDME) and Ecosocialism (MINEC) are responsible for managing compliance with mining and environmental regulations, with the support of the Bolivarian National Guard (GNB) in matters of security, activity control, and environmental protection.
Technical mining oversight is the responsibility of the National Mining Inspection and Oversight Service (SENAFIM), a support body of the MPPDME, which exercises control over primary, related, and auxiliary mining activities through inspections at their various stages. The purpose of this oversight is to guarantee the rational use of mineral resources, contribute to environmental protection, and ensure the collection of revenues that strengthen state governance. This oversight framework is applicable to what in Venezuela constitutes the equivalent of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Question: Are mining concessions or licenses granted for a fixed period with mandatory reassessment before extension? *0.5 may be given in cases where the reassessment is mandatory, even when there is no fixed period for the mining concession
Bolivia_Justification:
The term of mining administrative contracts signed with private mining producers and cooperatives is 30 years, counted from the date the contract enters into force.

Mining administrative contracts entered into with producers in the state mining industry will remain in effect as long as the requirements established in Article 18 of the Mining Law, which refers to economic and social interest, are met.

When a private mining producer or a cooperative holding a mining administrative contract has a demonstrated need to continue an ongoing operation, it may request an extension of the term for another thirty (30) years, with a minimum of six (6) months' notice prior to the expiration of the original term.
Bolivia_Sources: Law No. 535, Mining and Metallurgy Law
Brazil_Justification: The artisanal mining permit will be valid for up to 5 (five) years, and may be successively renewed at the discretion of the National Department of Mineral Production - DNPM.
Brazil_Sources: Law No. 7805 of 1989, Article 5, Paragraph I.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia establishes a general term of thirty (30) years for mining concession contracts, counted from the date of registration of the contract in the National Mining Registry. Before the expiration of this period, the concession holder may request an extension for up to an additional thirty (30) years, which will be formalized through a document signed by the parties and registered in the same Registry. At the end of the extension, the concession holder will have priority to contract the same area again and continue mining operations, which must not be suspended while the new contract is being finalized.

Regarding artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), the formalization regulations, governed by Decree 1949 of 2017, Article 2.2.5.4.2.7, paragraph d, stipulate that the Mining Formalization Subcontract will be signed for a period of no less than four (4) years, renewable successively with the prior approval of the National Mining Authority, without exceeding the validity of the main mining title.
Colombia_Sources:
Law 685 of 2001
Decree 1666 of 2016
Decree 1949 of 2017
Peru_Justification: The Law for the Formalization and Promotion of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining does not establish a specific period for granting mining rights to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The validity of mining rights in Peru for ASM is maintained through the annual payment of the validity fee and the renewal of the concession, with varying terms and amounts. Furthermore, validity is linked to compliance with the minimum annual production (Articles 6 and 7), and laws and regulations such as the ASM Law aim to improve the conditions and access to ownership of these rights. There is no automatic renewal; the right expires if the validity fee is not paid or if operations do not commence.
Peru_Sources: Law on Formalization and Promotion of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining
Ecuador_Justification: The mining concession will have a duration of up to twenty-five years, which may be renewed for equal periods, provided that a written request from the concessionaire has been submitted to the Sectoral Ministry for this purpose, before its expiration and the favorable report of the Mining Regulation and Control Agency and the Ministry of the Environment has been obtained beforehand.
Ecuador_Sources: Mining Law.
Guyana_Justification: Mining concessions and licenses in Guyana are granted for fixed periods, ranging from one year for small-scale claims to five years for medium-scale mining permits. While renewals are common, they are not automatic; they require compliance with regulations, such as environmental, technical, and financial reporting, which effectively acts as a mandatory review before renewal.
Guyana_Sources:
Application for Prospecting License-GGMC
 https://www.ggmc.gov.gy/ggmc service/application-for-prospecting-license/#:~:text=Overview,authorization%20to%20commence%20prospecting%20operations.
Suriname_Justification: Yes, mining concessions and licenses in Suriname are granted for fixed periods, with requirements for renewal or extension, typically based on the 1986 Mining Decree and negotiated Mineral Agreements. Extension applications must be submitted within the valid term, and for medium-scale operations, a feasibility report and compliance update are mandatory for extension. Exploration and exploitation concessions have defined terms. Extensions are possible but must be requested prior to expiration, ensuring the license remains valid while the application is processed.For transition to exploitation or during extension, applicants must provide reports on activities, financial commitments, and project feasibility.International investors often negotiate specific agreements (Mineral Agreements) which are ratified by the National Assembly to set custom, longer-term terms for specific large projects.

Venezuela_Justification:
Decree No. 2,165 (2015) establishes the area and duration of mining activities for both large-scale and small-scale mining operations.

For large-scale operations, the mining period may not exceed twenty (20) years, extendable for a maximum of two periods of up to ten (10) years each.

For small-scale mining, the mining period may not exceed ten (10) years, extendable for a maximum of two periods of two (2) years each.

In both cases, extensions must be requested one (1) year prior to the expiration of the corresponding period, for analysis, approval, or denial by the Ministry with jurisdiction over mining matters.

Question: Is the use of machinery in ASM subject to regulation and licensing?
Bolivia_Justification:
Mining and environmental regulations do not govern or license the use of machinery in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) or any other mining activity. Only customs import regulations apply.

It is true that the lack of records or the absence of detailed machinery information (policies or vehicle registration documents) required during environmental licensing procedures means there is no control over the machinery used in ASM. However, it is also true that much of the heavy machinery used in ASM is not owned by the ASM, but by its investor, who is not legally registered. This is because mining cooperatives are prohibited from signing contracts with third parties or subleasing mining areas, and Law No. 845 mandates the reversion of areas held under shared-risk agreements, whether leased or subleased.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
 - Law No. 845 dated October 24, 2016. Available at: https://www.lexivox.org/norms/BO-L-N845.html
Brazil_Justification:
The regulation and licensing of the use of machinery in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) in Brazil is a complex issue, characterized by an outdated legal framework and significant challenges in its application. Although some regulations exist, they often fail to adequately address the scale and impact of modern mining operations, which increasingly rely on heavy machinery.

In June 2025, the Attorney General's Office proposed to Conama the creation of a national registry of hydraulic excavators to curb illegal mining. To come into effect, the proposal needs to be assessed in technical and legal terms by the specialized Chambers that make up Conama and, in the end, must be submitted to a vote by the Plenary, which is composed of representatives from the federal, state and municipal levels, as well as civil society, business entities and the Public Prosecutor's Office.
Brazil_Sources:
Decree-Law No. 227/1967 (Mining Code). Available at: https://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/decreto-lei/del0227.htm
 CONAMA Proposal: https://www.gov.br/agu/pt-br/comunicacao/noticias/agu-propoe-criacao-de-cadastro-nacional-de-informacoes-ambientais-de-escavadeiras-hidraulicas
Colombia_Justification:
The use of machinery in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) in Colombia is subject to clear regulations. Decree 1666 of 2016 establishes a fundamental distinction between subsistence mining, which is limited to hand tools, and small-scale mining, which may use machinery [2].

The Mining Code also regulates panning as a strictly manual activity [1]. To use machinery, miners must obtain a small-, medium-, or large-scale mining title and an environmental license authorizing the use of mechanized equipment. Regulating the use of machinery is a key element in controlling the environmental impacts of mining and in differentiating between the various scales of operation.
Colombia_Sources: Law 685 of 2001 (Mining Code). Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=9202 Decree 1666 of 2016. Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=77883
Peru_Justification: Yes, the use of machinery in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) is subject to regulation. The General Mining Law – Consolidated Text of Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM – establishes safety and operational obligations for concession holders, and the mining formalization process requires operators to register the machinery they use. An individual license is not required for each machine, but its use must comply with current safety and operational standards.

Guyana_Justification: The Mining Act 1989 provides a general framework for the regulation of mining activities, which would include the use of machinery, but it does not contain specific provisions for the licensing of machinery in ASM operations. While the GGMC has the authority to regulate mining operations and can impose conditions on the use of equipment, there is no dedicated licensing system specifically for machinery used in ASM. The regulatory framework focuses more on the mining operations themselves rather than on the specific equipment used. This represents a gap in the regulatory framework that could be strengthened to provide better oversight of machinery use in the ASM sector.

Question: Are environmental impact assessments (EIAs) required for mechanized ASM operations?
Bolivia_Justification:
It should be clarified that environmental regulations do not establish specific procedures for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).
Law No. 1333, in Article 25, establishes that all public or private AOPs must have an environmental classification prior to their pre-investment phase, and Article 26 of the same law indicates that all AOPs must have an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS), which is the environmental license, prior to their execution phase. Article 4 of the RAAM (Regulations for the Management of Mining Operations) establishes that all mining operations or concessions must have an environmental license.

However, it's important to note that the mining sector has different types of environmental licenses. For example, if we're talking about mining prospecting activities, the regulations require the submission of a Mining Prospecting Form (PM), which grants an environmental license under the name of Exemption Certificate CD-4. If the activities correspond to exploration, exploitation, and/or concentration, but the latter two are considered AMIAC (Areas of Environmental Impact Assessment), the environmental license will be the Exemption Certificate CD-3. In these cases, these mining activities are exempt from submitting an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA).
An EIA is only required if the environmental category is 1 or 2, according to the RPCA (Regulations for the Protection of Mining Activities) and Supreme Decree No. 3549. Therefore, the regulations do not explicitly mandate the submission of environmental impact assessments for mechanized artisanal and small-scale mining (ASSM) operations.

Furthermore, the environmental regulations also provided for those AOPs that had started activities prior to the environmental regulations, for which it established an environmental adaptation procedure through the Environmental Manifest, however, to date this instrument remains in force, being the most used in the mining sector (small mining).
Bolivia_Sources:
- Regulations on Environmental Prevention and Control. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
 - Law No. 1333 on the Environment. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf

 - Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities. Available at: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/149_DS_24782.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, in Brazil, mechanized mining operations are subject to the requirement of environmental impact assessments (EIA). CONAMA Resolution No. 001/1986 establishes that the licensing of activities that modify the environment, such as mineral extraction, will depend on the preparation of an environmental impact study and respective environmental impact report (EIA/RIMA).

CONAMA Resolution No. 001, of January 23, 1986, establishes in its article 2, item I, that activities that modify the environment are subject to the preparation of an Environmental Impact Study (EIA) and respective Environmental Impact Report (RIMA), expressly including, in Annex 1, item IX, "mineral extraction, including artisanal mining".

This means that artisanal mining operations using heavy machinery, dredges, hydraulic excavators, or mechanized processing systems are classified as activities that potentially cause significant environmental degradation and, therefore, must present an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA/RIMA) before the granting of environmental licenses.

Furthermore, CONAMA Resolution No. 237/1997, in its article 3, reinforces that environmental licensing must be preceded by environmental studies proportional to the size and polluting potential of the activity, with the licensing body (state or federal) determining the need for an EIA/RIMA or simplified studies. Thus, when artisanal mining is mechanized and presents significant impacts—such as dredging of riverbeds, large-scale soil movement, or the use of polluting substances (e.g., mercury)—a complete environmental impact study is mandatory.
Brazil_Sources: CONAMA Resolution No. 001, dated January 23, 1986. Available at: https://www.mma.gov.br/port/conama/res/res86/res0186.html
Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, conducting an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) is mandatory for all mechanized mining operations, regardless of scale, as stipulated in Decree 1076 of 2015, which regulates environmental licensing in the country.

This decree establishes that all mining activities using machinery or mechanized equipment require a prior environmental license, and that an EIA is an essential requirement for granting it. This requirement applies to both medium- and large-scale mining, as well as to mechanized artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), which is distinguished from artisanal mining precisely by the use of machinery.

Resolution 1402 of 2018, issued by the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MinAmbiente), adopts the general methodology for the preparation and submission of environmental studies, including EIAs. This methodology defines the minimum content, technical procedures, and impact assessment tools for the use of machinery in mining activities.

The Regional Autonomous Corporations (CAR) are the competent authorities to evaluate and approve the EIAs of smaller-scale projects, while the National Environmental Licensing Authority (ANLA) retains competence over larger-scale projects.
Colombia_Sources:
Decree 1076 of 2015. Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=78153
Resolution 1402 of 2018.
Peru_Justification: Yes, environmental impact assessments are required for mechanized artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) operations. As mentioned previously, all mining operations, regardless of scale, must have an approved environmental management instrument before commencing activities. For ASM, adapted instruments such as the IGAFOM are used. However, mechanized ASM operations are not required to have an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) as is the case for medium or large-scale mining.

Ecuador_Justification: Article 118 of the Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities only stipulates that artisanal miners must specify, on the form issued by the National Environmental Authority, the type of machinery they will use, which must comply with the corresponding sector regulations. If an artisanal miner needs to change their machinery, they must notify the National Environmental Authority. It is important to note that this provision does not refer to small-scale mining, for which there is also no express obligation to conduct an environmental impact assessment.

Guyana_Justification: As noted in previous criteria, small-scale mining operations, including mechanized ASM, are typically exempt from the full Environmental Impact Assessment process that is required for larger operations. However, the Guidelines for Environmental Mining Regulations do provide specific environmental management practices for mechanized operations, and the Environmental Protection Agency retains the discretion to require an EIA if the operation is deemed to have significant environmental impacts. This suggests that while a full EIA may not be automatically required for all mechanized ASM operations, there is some level of environmental oversight. The lack of clear thresholds for when an EIA is required for mechanized ASM represents a weakness in the current framework.

Suriname_Justification: The NMA is responsible for the environmental impact assessment (EIA) process for all activities concerning the environment. The EIA process involves several phases, including screening, scoping, environmental impact analysis, assessment, and decision-making. The NMA ensures compliance with the Environmental Framework Law and provides guidance on the procedures to be followed. However, given that Suriname lacks specific legislation for the Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM) sector and does not have regulations concerning the sale and general use of machinery in this sector [1], it is highly improbable that specific Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) would be mandated for mechanized ASM operations. Previous research for Criterion 12 also indicated a lack of clear mandatory EIAs for ASM over large areas. Therefore, there is no evidence to suggest that EIAs are required for mechanized ASM operations. In the NMA EIA repository no EIA reports can be found for ASM operations.

Question: Are there governmental controls on the import, sale, or leasing of mining machinery?
Bolivia_Justification:
As stated in the justification, there are no specific regulations governing the import, sale, or lease of mining machinery. Bolivia is governed by the General Customs Law No. 1990 and the General Customs Regulations, Supreme Decree No. 25870, which outline import procedures.

Furthermore, Law No. 1391 was enacted in 2021, establishing tax incentives for the import and sale of mining machinery, including heavy machinery for the mining sector.
Bolivia_Sources:
- General Customs Law No. 1990 dated July 28, 1999. Available at: https://www.aduana.gob.bo/aduana7/sites/default/files/kcfinder/files/legislacion/LEY%20GENERAL.pdf
 - General Customs Regulations, Supreme Decree No. 25870 dated August 11, 2000. Available at: https://www.aduana.gob.bo/aduana7/sites/default/files/kcfinder/files/legislacion/LEY%20GENERAL.pdfç
 - Law No. 1391 dated August 31, 2021. Available at: https://siip.produccion.gob.bo/repSIIP2/files/normativa_12345_010920217bd1.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
There are some government controls on the import, sale, or rental of mining machinery in Brazil, but they are limited and often ineffective in controlling the use of equipment in illegal mining activities.

The import of heavy machinery, such as hydraulic excavators, tractors, and dredges, is overseen by the Brazilian Federal Revenue Service (RFB), which requires the registration of import operations in the Integrated Foreign Trade System (SISCOMEX). The control is therefore customs and tax-related, but not specifically focused on the intended use of the equipment.

After entering the country, there is no national system to track the sale, resale, transfer, or rental of mining machinery, creating a regulatory gap. The domestic trade of this equipment is governed only by general rules of taxation and ownership, without mechanisms to verify its intended use.
Brazil_Sources:
Brazilian Federal Revenue Service. Available at: https://www.gov.br/receitafederal/pt-br
 https://www.gov.br/agu/pt-br/comunicacao/noticias/agu-propoe-criacao-de-cadastro-nacional-de-informacoes-ambientais-de-escavadeiras-hidraulicas
Colombia_Justification:
Government controls exist on the importation of mining machinery, primarily through customs controls by the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN) [16]. However, there are no specific and systematic controls on the sale and leasing of mining machinery domestically.

The lack of a national registry of mining machinery makes it difficult to track equipment once it enters the country, which can facilitate its use in illegal activities. While general regulations exist for the importation of industrial machinery, there is no specific framework for mining machinery that would allow for effective control of its sale and use.
Colombia_Sources: National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN). Available at: https://www.dian.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: While regulations exist regarding the import and sale of machinery in general, there is no specific and rigorous control over machinery used in mining, especially in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The ease with which heavy machinery reaches illegal mining areas suggests that controls are insufficient. Thus, the General Mining Law – Consolidated Text of Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM – establishes that concession holders must comply with safety and operational standards, but does not require individual licenses for each machine.

Question: Is there a national or subnational registry for heavy machinery used in mining?
Bolivia_Justification: Since there are no specific regulations for the importation of machinery for the mining sector, there is no national or subnational registry of heavy machinery.

Colombia_Justification: Colombia has implemented a national registry for heavy machinery used for agricultural, industrial, and construction purposes, as well as in small-scale gold mining operations. Resolution No. 1068/2015 of the Ministry of Transportation requires that heavy machinery be registered in the National Transit Registry (RUNT System) and equipped with GPS tracking devices. This measure was adopted following a presidential decree the previous year (Decree No. 723/2014), which established a series of actions to combat illegal mining as part of a regional plan by the Andean countries aimed at suppressing this activity through increased import controls and other measures. In addition, Colombia has been using various technological tools, such as satellite imagery, remote sensing, and GPS, to track machinery operating illegally in areas without mining authorization.

Article 3 of Agreement No. 002 of the Ministry of Transportation, issued in 2017, contains the regulations for the use of heavy machinery and equipment in mining activities. It stipulates that the National Transportation and Public Works Authority is responsible for the Registry and Cadastre of all machinery, equipment, and transportation used in mining activities.

Question: Is mercury import, sale, and use regulated under national law?
Bolivia_Justification: Complementing what was mentioned in the justification, within the framework of D.S. No. 4959, in 2024 Ministerial Resolution No. 639/2024 was enacted, approving the Regulation for the Written Consent Procedure for Prior Authorization on Mercury, regulations that complement what is established in D.S. No. 4959. In addition to the specific regulations mentioned, there is also the Regulation for Activities with Hazardous Substances, which establishes that for the importation of hazardous substances, a License for Activities with Hazardous Substances for Importation must be obtained.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Regulations for the Written Consent Procedure for Prior Authorization on Mercury. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/PDFs/RVMA/RM_639_2024.pdf
 - Ministerial Resolution No. 639/2024 dated December 30, 2024. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/PDFs/RVMA/RM_639_2024.pdf
 - Regulations for Activities Involving Hazardous Substances. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
 - Supreme Decree No. 4959 (2023). Available at: https://www.lexivox.org/norms/BO-DS-N4959.xhtml
 - Ministry of Environment and Water (2023). Government Approves Supreme Decree No. 4959 Regulating the Importation of Mercury in Bolivia. Available at: https://www.mmaya.gob.bo/2023/06/15/gobierno-aprueba-decreto-supremo-n-4959-que-regula-la-importacion-de-mercurio-en-bolivia/
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, the import, sale, and use of mercury are regulated by national legislation in Brazil. The country is a signatory to the Minamata Convention on Mercury, enacted by Decree No. 9,470 of August 14, 2018, which establishes measures to control, reduce, and eliminate mercury emissions and releases.

Furthermore, the control of the production and marketing of substances that pose a risk to life, quality of life, and the environment, including mercury, has been provided for since Decree No. 97,634, of April 10, 1989.
MMA Ordinance No. 175, of April 22, 2021, delegates to the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA), through the Directorate of Environmental Quality, the competence to act as the national authority responsible for receiving notifications, issuing consents, and expressing opinions regarding requests for the import and export of controlled substances, in accordance with the Stockholm, Rotterdam, and Minamata Conventions.

More recently, IBAMA Normative Instruction No. 26, of December 10, 2024, established the environmental control requirements and procedures applicable to the import, export, trade, transfer, recycling, recovery, use, and transportation of metallic mercury, as well as the disposal of mercury waste within the national territory. According to Article 5 of this regulation, all importers, exporters, and traders must register with IBAMA and record operations in a digital system, using the CTF/APP (Federal Technical Registry of Potentially Polluting Activities and Users of Environmental Resources). Article 6 establishes the mandatory prior notification and authorization for the movement of mercury, and Article 7 requires companies to annually inform IBAMA of their stocks and the final destination of controlled substances.

These regulatory instruments demonstrate that Brazil has a robust and up-to-date legal framework to guarantee the control and traceability of mercury at all stages of the supply chain, in accordance with the international commitments undertaken by the country.
Brazil_Sources:
Minamata Convention on Mercury. Available at: https://www.mercuryconvention.org/
 IBAMA - Mercury Control. Available at: https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/quimicos-e-biologicos/mercurio-metalico
 DECREE No. 97,634, OF APRIL 10, 1989 - Provides for the control of the production and marketing of substances that pose a risk to life, quality of life, and the environment, and makes other provisions.
 DECREE No. 9,470, OF AUGUST 14, 2018 - Enacts the Minamata Convention on Mercury, signed by the Federative Republic of Brazil in Kumamoto on October 10, 2013.
 MMA ADMINISTRATIVE RULE No. 175, OF APRIL 22, 2021 - Delegates authority to the Brazilian Institute of the Environment and Renewable Natural Resources — IBAMA, through the Directorate of Environmental Quality, to act as the designated authority responsible for receiving notifications, issuing consents, and ruling on requests for the import and export of substances that have been restricted in other countries, in accordance with the provisions of the Stockholm, Rotterdam, and Minamata Conventions.
 IBAMA REGULATORY INSTRUCTION No. 26, OF DECEMBER 10, 2024 - Establishes the requirements and environmental control procedures for the import, export, trade, transfer, recycling, recovery, use, and transport of metallic mercury, as well as the disposal of mercury waste within the national territory.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has comprehensive regulations governing mercury, covering its import, sale, and use. Law 1658 of 2013 is the primary legislation establishing provisions for the marketing and use of mercury in the country, with the aim of protecting human health and the environment.

The law establishes a Mercury User Registry, administered by the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, which allows for monitoring and control of the entire mercury supply chain, from import to final use. In addition, INVIMA (National Institute for Food and Drug Surveillance) controls the import of mercury and products containing it.

Peru_Justification: Yes, the import, sale, and use of mercury are regulated in Peru. Legislative Decree No. 1100 expressly prohibits the use of mercury in illegal mining. Furthermore, Supreme Decree No. 061-2015-RE approves the ratification of the Minamata Convention on Mercury, committing the country to reduce and control this metal. Additionally, Supreme Decree No. 073-2014-EF includes mercury in the list of controlled goods, establishing control measures for its sale and transport. Finally, Superintendency Resolution No. 207-2014/SUNAT creates the Registry for the Control of Controlled Goods (RCBF), which requires operators to register and obtain authorization to legally import, export, transport, or sell mercury.

Question: Are there specific restrictions or bans on mercury use in ASM?
Bolivia_Justification: The Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities (RAAM) stipulate in Article 27 that the use of mercury is only permitted in closed circuits, meaning that there is no emission of mercury vapors. Furthermore, it is evident that the Bolivian State has repeatedly submitted draft laws (Bill PL-356-21 in 2022, Bill PL-089/23 in 2023, and Bill PL-374/24 in 2024) with the aim of progressively reducing the use of mercury in mining.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Bill PL-356-21. Available at: https://diputados.gob.bo/leyes/pl-n-356-2021-2022/
 - Bill PL-089/23. Available at: https://diputados.gob.bo/leyes/pl-no-089-2023-2024/

 - InsightCrime (2023). The Toxic Mercury Trade. Available at: https://insightcrime.org/es/investigaciones/toxico-comercio-mercurio/
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, the use of mercury in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) is subject to specific restrictions in Brazilian legislation, although there is not yet a total ban in effect.

Brazil is a signatory to the Minamata Convention on Mercury, promulgated by Decree No. 9,470/2018, which obliges countries to reduce and, where possible, eliminate the use of mercury in artisanal gold mining activities. Nationally, Decree No. 97,634/1989 establishes controls on the production and marketing of substances that pose a risk to life and the environment, including mercury. Additionally, IBAMA Normative Instruction No. 26/2024 defines strict rules for the import, trade, transport, use, and disposal of metallic mercury waste, also applying to ASM activities.

Despite these restrictions, the use of mercury in artisanal mining still occurs irregularly, especially in Amazonian areas. The lack of traceability, illegal trade, and insufficient environmental oversight hinder the effective implementation of regulations. This regulatory gap motivated the presentation of Bill No. 1011/2023, currently under consideration in the Chamber of Deputies, which proposes a total ban on the production, import, export, and use of metallic mercury and its compounds in national territory, including artisanal mining.

Thus, although there are explicit legal restrictions, the absence of a specific prohibition and the limited effectiveness of control measures justify the partial score assigned.
Brazil_Sources:
Decree No. 9,470, of August 14, 2018 – Enacts the Minamata Convention on Mercury. Available at: Planalto
 
 Decree No. 97,634, of April 10, 1989 – Provides for the control of the production and marketing of substances that pose a risk to life and the environment.
 
 IBAMA Normative Instruction No. 26, dated December 10, 2024 – Establishes requirements and procedures for environmental control of the import, export, trade, and use of metallic mercury.
 
 Bill No. 1011/2023 – Provides for the prohibition of the production, import, export, and use of metallic mercury and its compounds.
Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, the use of mercury in mining is completely prohibited. Law 1658 of 2013 established a five-year transition period for the mining sector, especially artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), to adapt and migrate to cleaner technologies. This period ended on July 15, 2018, at which point the prohibition became total, definitive, and without exceptions throughout the national territory.

Prior to this law, Resolution 1402 of 2006 from the then Ministry of Environment, Housing, and Territorial Development had already established restrictions and technical guidelines to reduce the use of mercury in gold mining. Law 1658 consolidated and expanded these measures, establishing administrative and economic sanctions for those who violated the prohibition.

Additionally, the Colombian Penal Code, through Article 338A incorporated by Law 2111 of 2021, criminalizes the use, production, sale, or possession of mercury, strengthening control and enforcement mechanisms.

These provisions position Colombia as one of the first countries in Latin America to implement a total ban on mercury in mining, in line with the commitments made under the Minamata Convention on Mercury, ratified by Law 1892 of 2018.

Furthermore, Article 7 of the Minamata Convention on Mercury, ratified by Peru, stipulates that States must adopt measures to reduce and, where feasible, eliminate the use of mercury in artisanal and small-scale mining, and develop a National Action Plan when these activities are more than negligible.

Ecuador_Justification:
Since 2013, a ban on the use of mercury in mining activities has been in effect, as stipulated in Article 86.1 of the Mining Law (LM). This ban is general and contrasts with the specific regulation for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining (ASM): Article 37 of the Regulations for the Special Regime of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining, from 2009, mandates the avoidance of mercury use. This was the initial guideline for all mining activity and continues to be the de facto guideline currently in force for ASM.

For its part, Article Article 88 of the 2025 Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities (RAAM) establishes these guidelines for the management of mercury as a byproduct in the beneficiation and refining phases:

“If metallic mercury is unintentionally generated as a byproduct during mineral extraction and processing activities, it must be managed as a hazardous chemical or hazardous waste, in accordance with the corresponding regulations.

At any stage of the project's lifespan, the mining concession holder may manage the metallic mercury as a hazardous waste or chemical within the country, in compliance with the provisions of the Organic Environmental Code, its Regulations, and applicable national regulations.

If the mining concession holder needs to manage it outside the country as hazardous waste, it will be exported in accordance with the provisions of the Organic Environmental Code, its Regulations, the Minamata Convention, and the Basel Convention.

The metallic mercury byproduct may be stored until the project's lifespan and must comply with the technical conditions established in applicable national and international regulations.”

Regarding small-scale metallurgical mining (ASM), in 2022 Interministerial Agreement No. MAATE-MEM-2022-001 was issued, containing guidelines for the comprehensive management of mining waste and sterile material by informal recyclers within the special regime for small-scale metallic mining, which refers to mercury (see recitals).

The relative effectiveness of this confusing regulatory framework has been acknowledged in the National Action Plan on the Use of Mercury in Small-Scale Mining (ASM):

“The use of mercury in ASM activities has been prohibited since the Mining Law and is reiterated in other subsequent legal instruments related to ASM activities. Despite the prohibition, and as explained in the Diagnostic section of this document, mercury continues to be used and, contrary to expectations, its use has intensified since the enactment of the prohibition, but illegally. This has led to a nationwide black market for mercury, which is often linked to other illegal activities, such as drug trafficking or even human trafficking.”

Finally, it is worth noting that since 2014, the unauthorized management of hazardous products or waste has been considered a crime in Ecuador, under Article 254 of the Comprehensive Organic Criminal Code (COIP), with a prison sentence of up to five years.
Ecuador_Sources:
Comprehensive Organic Criminal Code.
Mining Law.
Regulations for the Special Regime of Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining.
Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities.
Interministerial Agreement No. MAATE-MEM-2022-001
Guyana_Justification:
The Mercury Act 2020 specifically prohibits the use of mercury in artisanal and small-scale gold mining operations in open systmes whjere mercury can be discharged into the environment. Also, there are certain restrictions for burning amalgam This is a critical measure for reducing theenvironmental and health impacts of ASM in Guyana and demonstrates the government's commitment to eliminating mercury use in the sector. The prohibition is comprehensive and aligns with Guyana's obligations under the Minamata Convention on Mercury. The challenge moving forward will be the effective enforcement of this ban, but the legal framework provides a
strong foundation for mercury elimination in the ASM sector.

Suriname_Justification: While Suriname has conducted a Minamata Initial Assessment (MIA) which identified the need to monitor and register mercury imports [1], and has a National Action Plan (NAP) for Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM) [2], the NAP explicitly states that the legal framework contains no regulations about the sale and use of mercury [2]. Furthermore, reports indicate that legal import permits for mercury have not been issued since 2003, suggesting that any existing import registry is either defunct or ineffective in tracking actual mercury flows, which are largely illicit [3]. Therefore, there are no comprehensive and effective national or subnational registries for mercury imports, sales, and use.

Question: Is there a system for tracking the origin, distribution, and final use of mercury?
Bolivia_Justification: In order to reinforce the systems mentioned in the justification, the Regulation for the Written Consent Procedure for Prior Authorization on Mercury includes in its Annex the purchase and sales form, which is intended to trace the origin and destination of the mercury.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Regulations for the Written Consent Procedure for Prior Authorization on Mercury. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/PDFs/RVMA/RM_639_2024.pdf
 - Supreme Decree No. 4959 (2023). Available at: https://www.lexivox.org/norms/BO-DS-N4959.xhtml
 - Ministerial Resolution No. 325. Regulations for the Single Mercury Registry – RUME. Available at: https://www.mmaya.gob.bo/marco-legal/resoluciones-ministeriales/
Brazil_Justification: Although Brazil has legal frameworks and initiatives in place to control mercury, especially through its adherence to the Minamata Convention and recent regulatory updates (such as IBAMA's IN No. 26), the practical implementation and effectiveness of a comprehensive tracking system for mercury in artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM) is still under development and faces significant challenges, including illegal trafficking and difficulties in monitoring the informal mining sector. According to Article 5 of this regulation, all importers, exporters, and traders must register with IBAMA and record operations in a digital system using the CTF/APP (Federal Technical Registry of Potentially Polluting Activities and Users of Environmental Resources). Article 6 establishes the mandatory prior notification and authorization for mercury movement, and Article 7 stipulates that companies must annually inform IBAMA of their stocks and the final destination of controlled substances.
Brazil_Sources: IBAMA Normative Instruction No. 26, dated December 10, 2024. Available at: https://www.in.gov.br/en/web/dou/-/instrucao-normativa-ibama-n-26-de-10-de-dezembro-de-2024-529602379
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has a system for tracking mercury through the Mercury Users Registry, established by Law 1658 of 2013. This registry, managed by the Ministry of Environment, allows for monitoring the entire mercury supply chain, from its importation to its final use.

In addition, the DIAN (National Tax and Customs Directorate) controls mercury imports, and only duly registered and authorized companies can import and trade this substance. This tracking system is essential for controlling the illegal trade of mercury and ensuring compliance with the ban on its use in mining.
Colombia_Sources: Law 1658 of 2013. Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=53781
Peru_Justification: In Peru, there are control mechanisms for mercury, although they are limited. Superintendency Resolution No. 207-2014/SUNAT created a special registry for the entry and exit of controlled goods (including mercury), requiring importers and distributors to declare transactions in the SUNAT system. Additionally, Supreme Decree No. 073-2014-EF regulates customs procedures for mercury imports, establishing documentary control requirements and prior authorization. However, these mechanisms focus on trade and imports, and there is still no comprehensive, digital, and public system that allows for continuous tracking of mercury distribution and end use within the country, especially in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Ecuador_Justification:
Currently, there is no specific system in place.

The National Action Plan on Mercury Use in Small-Scale Mining (ASM) includes a strategy to strengthen the control and monitoring of mercury trade and use, based on enforcement operations to verify its use and the strengthening of customs and port controls to identify and prevent smuggling. However, the systems applicable to the integrated management of hazardous chemicals and hazardous waste are currently being used.
Ecuador_Sources: National action plan on the use of mercury in artisanal and small-scale mining.
Guyana_Justification: Yes, Guyana has a mercury tracking system, but it is a regulatory and administrative tracking framework rather than a single digital end‑to‑end tracking platform. The Mercury Act 2020 requires licensing and record-keeping for the import, sale, and use of mercury, which provides a legal basis for tracking its flow through the supply chain. The Act mandates that persons engaged in mercury-related activities maintain records and submit reports to the relevant authorities. Mercury importation is capped annually andiImporters, sellers, and distributors must obtain an Environmental Authorization from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). However, the Act does not establish a comprehensive digital tracking system that would provide real-time monitoring and complete traceability of mercury from import to final use. While the legal framework for tracking exists, the implementation of a robust, technology-enabled tracking system would strengthen the effectiveness of mercury control measures.

a_Mercury_Act_2020.pdf

Suriname_Justification: Despite Suriname's accession to the Minamata Convention and the development of a National Action Plan (NAP) for Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM), there is a significant lack of clear legal procedures for the storage, transport, and disposal of mercury and mercury-containing waste. Multiple sources confirm this gap. The OAS report from March 2023 explicitly states, "At present, Suriname has no legal framework on mercury" [1]. Similarly, the political ecology report on mercury in Suriname notes that "there are no legal instruments that specifically mention mercury" [2]. While the Minamata Convention addresses interim storage and disposal, and efforts are underway to adopt standards under the Environmental Law, these have not yet translated into clear, implemented national procedures [3].

Venezuela_Justification: The legally imposed oversight obligations of the Venezuelan state should allow for the detection of mercury use and the monitoring of its distribution and origin. However, there are no public records indicating that these actions are being carried out effectively. Mercury continues to be widely used in mining activities, and the government is not taking effective measures to prevent its trade.

Question: Are there procedures in place for the seizure, transport, and final disposal of confiscated mercury, especially following operations against illegal gold mining?
Bolivia_Justification: Bolivia lacks protocols and/or procedures for the seizure, transport, and final disposal of confiscated mercury, creating a procedural gap and endangering the personnel who carry out the seizures. Article 11 of the Minamata Convention establishes very general guidelines on mercury waste, also indicating that it must comply with the Basel Convention. However, the Basel Convention provides very general guidelines for the transboundary transport and disposal of hazardous substances.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Minamata Convention on Mercury. Report of the workshop on the implementation of the provisions of the Minamata Convention in Bolivia (2019). Available at: https://minamataconvention.org/sites/default/files/documents/final_report/Informe-taller--Bolivia.pdf
 - Basel Convention. On the Control of Transboundary Movements of Hazardous Wastes and Their Disposal. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/pronacops/assets/files/Convenio-de-Basilea.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil has established a legal and regulatory framework to control mercury, particularly in the context of artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM), which inherently includes provisions for the seizure, transport, and final disposal of confiscated mercury. While specific public documents and step-by-step instructions detailing the exact procedures for confiscated mercury were not explicitly found in this initial research, the overall legal and operational context strongly indicates its existence and ongoing development.

The main regulatory instrument currently in force is IBAMA Normative Instruction No. 26, of December 10, 2024, which establishes the requirements and procedures for environmental control of the import, export, trade, transfer, recycling, recovery, use, transport, and disposal of metallic mercury waste.

According to Article 10 of this regulation, the transport of metallic mercury, including that seized in inspection operations, must follow specific safety and traceability rules, observing ANTT Resolution No. 5,947/2021, which regulates the land transport of hazardous products.

Furthermore, Article 11 of the same Normative Instruction determines that seized mercury must be temporarily stored in licensed facilities and subsequently sent for environmentally appropriate disposal, through recovery, recycling, or confinement in security cells duly authorized by the competent environmental agency.

To guide the application of these procedures, IBAMA published the technical document "Practical Guide for the Management of Seized Metallic Mercury Waste" (2023), which provides a detailed step-by-step guide for public agents and licensed entities responsible for handling confiscated mercury.
Brazil_Sources:
Decree No. 9,470, of August 14, 2018. Available at: https://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/_ato2015-2018/2018/decreto/d9470.htm
 Guide “Practical Guide for the Management of Seized Metallic Mercury Waste” (2023): https://mpmt.mp.br/portalcao/news/731/104827/livro---guia-pra769tico-para-o-gerenciamento-de-resi769duos-de-mercu769rio-meta769lico-apreendido
 IBAMA Normative Instruction No. 26, dated December 10, 2024. Available at: https://www.ibama.gov.br/component/legislacao/?view=legislacao&legislacao=139445
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has regulatory frameworks and technical guidelines that govern the seizure, transport, and final disposal of confiscated mercury; however, there are no standardized operating procedures or uniform implementation throughout the country.

Law 1658 of 2013 established the obligation to eliminate the use of mercury and to guarantee the environmentally sound final disposal of confiscated stockpiles. Subsequently, the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MinAmbiente) developed several technical guidelines for the management of hazardous chemicals, including mercury, such as the “Guide for the Responsible Management of Mercury” (ARM, 2018), the “Guide for the Management and Storage of Mercury” (UNEP, 2017), and the “Guide for the Management of 25 Chemical Substances” (MinAmbiente, 2021). These guidelines provide technical criteria for the temporary storage, safe transport, and final disposal of seized mercury.

In practice, the National Police and the DIJIN (Directorate of Criminal Investigation and Interpol) apply internal protocols for the chain of custody and transport of hazardous materials, but there is no unified national manual or mandatory inter-institutional protocol. This creates gaps in coordination and operational capacity, especially in mining areas where institutions lack the infrastructure for temporary storage and safe final disposal.

Therefore, although regulatory and technical guidelines exist, challenges remain in the effective implementation, inter-institutional coordination, and territorial coverage of these procedures.

Peru_Justification: While procedures exist for the seizure of mercury as part of the fight against illegal mining, the procedures for its transport and final disposal are neither clearly established nor publicly known. The safe management of confiscated mercury is a significant challenge for Peruvian authorities.

Ecuador_Justification:
Currently, there is no specific procedure in place, so the general procedures established for exercising sanctioning powers in administrative or criminal matters apply. This is confirmed in the Short Report on the Implementation of the Minamata Convention (2023), section 3.3 of which reports on the seizure of mercury in 2021 and 2022, which has been “stored in the facilities of the Judicial Police,” as is typical with evidence in any other infraction.

It should be noted that in recent years the Mining Law and its Regulations were amended to adapt sanctioning procedures to the regulations in force in the Andean Community in order to make them more effective. However, neither Article 57 of the Mining Law nor Article 99 of the Mining Regulations, as amended, include specific provisions for the retention (precautionary measure) or seizure (sanction) of mercury. In criminal matters, there are also no specific provisions for mercury, so the general procedures for its seizure (precautionary measure) or confiscation (penalty) provided for in art. 69 of the Comprehensive Organic Criminal Code (COIP) apply.

Guyana_Justification:
The Mercury Act 2020 provides authorities with the power to seize and confiscate mercury that is being used illegally, establishing the legal basis for enforcement actions.  Mercury is treated as a controlled toxic substance in Guyana. During operations against illegal mining, mercury may be lawfully seized by the Guyana Geology and Mines Commission (GGMC),  Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Guyana Revenue Authority / Customs, Law‑enforcement agencies supporting mining enforcement. Officials have publicly confirmed that mercury found during operations is confiscated as part of enforcement actions. Confiscated mercury is treated as hazardous material and the 
transport is handled by or under supervision of the EPA, GGMC and the Pesticides and Toxic Chemicals Control Board (PTCCB) which requires controlled handling and documentation, similar to other hazardous chemicals. However, the development of comprehensive procedures for mercury disposal, including the establishment of appropriate facilities and protocols, is not yet in place.

Suriname_Justification: Despite Suriname's commitment to the Minamata Convention and the development of a National Action Plan (NAP) for Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM), there is no clear legal framework or specific provisions for the remediation of mercury-contaminated sites. While the NAP mentions a systematic evaluation of communities within ASGM areas for risk assessment and monitoring [1], and the Minamata Convention itself addresses contaminated sites, the available sources do not detail national legal instruments or clear procedures for the actual remediation process. Previous research (Criterion 24) also highlighted a general absence of a legal framework for mercury management in Suriname [2].

Venezuela_Justification: There is no information confirming the existence of public, transparent, and systematic protocols for the seizure, transport, and safe final disposal of mercury confiscated in operations against illegal gold mining. However, mining oversight agencies and those responsible for conducting operations against illegal mining are obligated to properly dispose of mercury and other hazardous substances, in accordance with Decree No. 2,635 of 1998, which regulates the handling and treatment of hazardous substances in Venezuela.

Question: Is the country a signatory or Party to the Minamata Convention on Mercury?
Bolivia_Justification: The date indicated in the justification is incorrect, since Bolivia has been a signatory to the Minamata Convention since 2013.
Bolivia_Sources: - Minamata Convention. Available at: https://minamataconvention.org/es/parties/bol
Brazil_Justification: Brazil is a signatory and Party to the Minamata Convention on Mercury. The country signed the Convention in Kumamoto on October 10, 2013. The Brazilian Congress approved the Convention through Legislative Decree No. 99, of July 6, 2017. Brazil deposited its instrument of ratification with the UN Secretary-General on August 8, 2017, and the Convention entered into force for Brazil on November 8, 2017.
Brazil_Sources:
Ministry of the Environment and Climate Change - Minamata Convention on Mercury. Available at: https://www.gov.br/mma/pt-br/assuntos/meio-ambiente-urbano-recursos-hidricos-qualidade-ambiental/seguranca-quimica/convencao-de-minamata-sobre-mercurio
 LEGISLATIVE DECREE No. 99, OF 2017 - Approves the text of the Minamata Convention on Mercury, adopted in Kumamoto, Japan, on October 10, 2013.
 DECREE No. 9,470, OF AUGUST 14, 2018 - Enacts the Minamata Convention on Mercury, signed by the Federative Republic of Brazil in Kumamoto on October 10, 2013.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia is a signatory and Party to the Minamata Convention on Mercury. The country signed the convention on October 10, 2013, and ratified it through Law 1892 of 2018. The ratification was deposited on August 26, 2019, and the convention entered into force for Colombia on November 26, 2019.

Colombia's accession to the Minamata Convention demonstrates its commitment to protecting human health and the environment from the effects of mercury and strengthens national policies to eradicate its use in mining.

Article 417 of the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador empowers the President of the Republic to sign treaties (e.g., Conventions). For Ecuador to become a Party, ratification and subsequent deposit of the instrument of ratification are required, which is preceded by a legal and political review process also provided for by the Ecuadorian Constitution, as mentioned in criterion 28.
