Question: Are there public programs to support formalization through incentives to and capacity-building of ASM gold miners?
Bolivia_Justification:
Bolivia does not have public formalization programs for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). Law No. 535 provides guidelines for obtaining mining rights, the detailed procedure for which is found in the Regulations for Granting and Terminating Mining Rights.

The Mining and Metallurgical Authority (AJAM) does not provide technical assistance; the Geological, Mining and Mining Service (SERGEOMIN) offers paid technical assistance.

The PlanetGOLD Bolivia project, implemented by the United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO) and executed by Projekt-Consult GmbH, will come into effect in 2023. Its strategic partners are the Ministry of Mining and Metallurgy, the Ministry of Environment and Water, and the Ministry of Health and Sports. One of PlanetGOLD's components is the formalization of ASM.
Bolivia_Sources:
- SERGEOMIN. https://www.sergeomin.gob.bo/index.php/prospeccion-y-exploracion/
 - PlanetGOLD Bolivia. https://www.planetgold.org/es/bolivia
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil has public programs that support formalization through incentives and capacity building for artisanal and small-scale gold miners in the MAPE (Ministry of Agriculture, Livestock and Supply). The Program to Support the Development of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (Pró-Mape), the Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG), and the "ASGM – Coexistence in Brazil" project from USP (University of São Paulo) are clear examples of these initiatives, which aim at regularization, the adoption of best practices, and the training of artisanal miners.

Pró-Mape, established by Decree No. 10,966/2022, aims to integrate and strengthen public policies for the sustainable development of artisanal and small-scale mining. The program seeks to promote best practices, formalization, and cooperation, as well as encourage the health, assistance, and dignity of the communities involved.

The Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG) is a regime for the extraction of mineral substances with immediate exploitation of the mineral deposit that, due to its nature, size, location, and economic use, can be mined independently of prior research work, according to criteria established by the National Mining Agency (ANM). The PLG is an important instrument for formalizing artisanal mining activity.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has specific public programs to support the formalization of artisanal and small-scale (ASS) miners. Law 2250 of 2022 establishes a special framework for mining legalization and formalization, including differentiated procedures and support programs.

The Ministry of Mines and Energy has developed formalization programs that include technical assistance, training, and support for obtaining mining titles and environmental licenses. In addition, the country participates in the Better Gold Initiative, which provides comprehensive technical assistance to mining organizations.

Peru_Justification: Yes, the Peruvian government has implemented programs to support the formalization of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) maintains an official formalization program that includes training, technical assistance, and administrative support to help miners access the REINFO (National Registry of Informal Mining Operators) and complete their formalization process. MINEM has also promoted initiatives to facilitate access to credit and improved marketing channels for formalized miners (through announcements and pilot projects). Furthermore, the government announced the creation of a mining fund to support small-scale mining with financing at preferential rates, technical assistance, and marketing channels. The proposed new ASM Law also includes incentives for formalization.

Ecuador_Justification: Title IV of the Regulations for the Special Regime of Small-Scale Artisanal Mining provides for measures to promote, provide technical assistance, and foster innovation, including a program to improve the efficiency and competitiveness of these regimes. Within this framework, the PlanetGOLD Ecuador project, for example, aims to transform artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) by promoting sustainable practices and reducing mercury use. This project, led by the national environmental authority and supported by the UNDP and the GEF, focuses specifically on formalizing the ASM sector, including improving the integration of artisanal and small-scale mining into the formal regulatory framework and providing capacity building.

Guyana_Justification: Guyana has established comprehensive public programs to support the formalization of ASM gold miners through both incentives and capacity-building initiatives. The National Mineral Sector Policy Framework includes specific policies for optimizing the economic scope and technical capacity of small and medium-scale mining, which encompasses measures to support formalization. The government has actively promoted the formation of mining syndicates to help informal miners gain legal access to mining grounds. The Guyana National Action Plan for Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining outlines strategies for formalization, regulation, and updating of legislation. Additionally, the government has collaborated with international organizations like the Artisanal Gold Council and Conservation International to provide capacity-building programs focusing on mercury-free processing, mine safety, business planning, and legal compliance.

Suriname_Justification: The Surinamese government provides limited technical assistance and capacity-building programs for ASM, primarily through international partnerships. The most significant initiative is the World Bank's Competitiveness and Sector Diversification Project, launched in 2019, which includes a substantial component dedicated to strengthening mining sector governance, transparency, and administration.  This project provides technical assistance for regulatory reforms and capacity building for the Ministry of Natural Resources (Min NH) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Tourism (Min EZOTI).Two current ongoing initiatives are the, " Improving Environmental Management in the Mining Sector of Suriname, with Emphasis on Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold" (EMSAGS) Project and the "GEF GOLD+: Advancing formalization and mercury-free gold in Suriname", also referred to as Planetgold. These projects focus on the formalization of small-scale gold mining and the phasing out of mercury, which are crucial for environmental sustainability and the health of the mining community. The GEFGOLD project is particularly significant as it aims to address the environmental impacts of gold mining and promote sustainable practices in the sector. Additionally, recent capacity-building efforts have focused on Earth Observation and data analysis techniques, with over 30 public servants trained in these skills. However, these initiatives appear to be project-based and internationally supported rather than representing a systematic,government-led program for ASM technical assistance and capacity building.

The main state policy instrument aimed at regulating informal mining actors is the 2019-2025 National Mining Plan, managed by the Ministry of People's Power for Ecological Mining Development and Basic Industries and the Venezuelan Mining Corporation.

Question: Are there accessible financing mechanisms promoted by the State to support sustainable practices as an alternative to unsustainable ASM gold mining?
Bolivia_Justification: The Mining Financing Fund (FOFIM) is a public entity responsible for granting loans to mining cooperatives to promote and support projects for improving exploitation and concentration systems, purchasing concentration plants, marketing minerals, and acquiring equipment and/or machinery; thus enabling the optimization of productivity indices in their production. However, the signing of an inter-institutional agreement for responsible mining between FOFIM and PlanetGOLD Bolivia is recent.
Bolivia_Sources: - La Razón (2025). Government signs inter-institutional agreement for responsible mining. Available at: https://larazon.bo/economia-y-empresa/2025/06/18/118382/
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil has some public financing and credit mechanisms aimed at sustainable ventures, which could, in theory, benefit artisanal miners or formal cooperatives of the MAPE (Mining and Agricultural Production System). However, there are no specific and widely accessible financing lines aimed at encouraging small-scale sustainable mining practices.

For example, there are financing funds managed by the Bank of the Amazon and the Bank of Brazil, such as the Constitutional Financing Fund for the North and Midwest. These funds offer credit for sustainable productive ventures in the Amazon region and the Midwest.

However, there is no direct financing to promote sustainable small-scale mining in Brazil. At an event in October 2025 held by the newspaper Correio Braziliense, the president of the Federation of Mining Cooperatives of the State of Mato Grosso, Gilson Camboin, stated that the organized artisanal mining sector seeks to strengthen legality and eliminate the use of mercury, but faces difficulties due to a lack of support and financing.

Furthermore, the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) is forging a partnership with the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) for a small-scale gold mining project. This initiative, announced on July 4, 2025, aims to promote safer and more sustainable practices in the sector, aligning with Brazil's commitments to the Minamata Convention.

Colombia has some financing mechanisms to support sustainable practices, but they are not specifically designed as alternatives to unsustainable artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The National Royalties Fund can finance sustainable development projects in mining regions.
Programs such as the National Comprehensive Program for the Substitution of Illicit Crops (PNIS) include productive alternatives, but they are not specifically aimed at ASM miners.

Lack of access to credit is one of the main obstacles preventing small-scale miners from adopting clean technologies and improving their environmental practices.

Ecuador_Justification: Article 30 of the Regulations for the Special Regime for Small-Scale Artisanal Mining stipulates the availability of financing plans and sources through the National Finance Corporation, the Development Bank, and the State Bank. Accordingly, BANECUADOR offers a Mining Development credit line specifically for economic agents authorized by the Central Bank of Ecuador to trade gold from small-scale and artisanal mining operations whose annual income does not exceed $300,000.

Guyana_Justification:
While Guyana has some financing mechanisms available through the GuyanaGold Board, including "Access to Bank Loan Incentives," these are not specifically targeted at promoting sustainable practices as alternatives to unsustainable ASM gold mining. The financing mechanisms are general in nature and do not specifically incentivize the adoption of cleaner technologies or more responsible mining practices. The lack of accessible, targeted financing for sustainable ASM represents a significant barrier to the adoption of mercury-free
technologies and environmentally responsible mining practices. While international projects like planetGOLD have worked to address this issue through specific funding mechanisms, a more systematic and state-led approach to financing sustainable ASM practices is needed.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname has established some mechanisms aimed at ASM formalization, but these efforts are limited in scope and effectiveness. The primary initiative is the Ordening Goudsector (OGS) commission, established to oversee the small-scale gold mining sector and manage conflicts between different stakeholders. This represents a step toward bringing the ASM sector under formal regulatory oversight. The National Development Plan 2021-2026 acknowledges the environmental risks of unstructured ASGM and emphasizes the need for formalization, explicitly noting that "formalization and legality are two different concepts that need to be clarified and well understood." However, the 2017 IGF assessment noted that the ASM sector remains largely informal, indicating that existing formalization efforts have had limited success. The government has also expressed plans to establish a "Delfstoffen Instituut Suriname" (DIS) or Minerals Institute to improve governance and oversight, which could support formalization efforts, but this initiative appears to still be in planning stages.

Question: Are safer mercury-free technologies promoted through incentives or support programs?
Bolivia_Justification:
The National Action Plan (NAP) has not yet been approved and submitted to the Minamata Convention Secretariat, so it is incorrect to claim that this instrument has developed initiatives to promote safe and mercury-free technologies, as it is not yet an official document.

In the 1990s, the Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC), through the MEDMIN Foundation, carried out a project focused on small-scale mining and reducing the environmental impacts caused by this activity. Its main objective was the implementation of clean technologies in mining operations to mitigate environmental damage.

With support from WCS Bolivia, responsible mining pilot projects have been conducted in the Apolobamba Protected Natural Area (ANMIN) and the Cotapata National Park (ANMI), introducing equipment such as gravimetric tables to reduce and/or eliminate the use of mercury.

Subsequently, from 2023 the PlanetGOLD Bolivia project, implemented by the United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO) and executed by Projekt-Consult GmbH, came into effect. Among the components of PlanetGOLD is "Improving the capture of mercury-free technologies".

Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil is actively promoting safer, mercury-free technologies in artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASPM) through various incentives and support programs, driven primarily by its commitment to the Minamata Convention on Mercury. The most prominent initiative in this regard is the "Mercury-Free Gold Project."

This project, launched in November 2022, is a collaborative effort between Brazil's Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME), the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), and is funded by the Global Environment Facility (GEF). Its main objective is to support Brazil in developing and implementing a National Action Plan to reduce and ultimately eliminate mercury pollution from gold extraction, with a specific focus on ASPM.

Furthermore, the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MM) is coordinating a partnership with the UN for a small-scale gold mining project. The initiative aims to promote safer and more sustainable practices in the sector, in line with the commitments made by the country in the Minamata Convention on Mercury, which aims to protect health and the environment.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia promotes mercury-free technologies primarily through specific projects and international cooperation, but lacks a comprehensive, institutionalized national program. The country participates in the UNDP's Planet GOLD project, which promotes mercury-free technologies [19].
Law 1658 of 2013 prohibits the use of mercury in mining, which indirectly promotes alternative technologies, but does not establish specific incentives for their adoption.

Peru_Justification: Yes, the Peruvian government promotes the adoption of safer, mercury-free technologies, but it does so primarily through public and cooperative programs and projects (training, provision of gravimetric plants, technical assistance, and regulatory frameworks), rather than through a permanent government subsidy or a specific tax regime for these technologies. The National Implementation Plan for the Minamata Convention (Supreme Decree No. 004-2019-MINAM) mandates that public institutions implement measures to reduce and eliminate mercury use, including the promotion of alternative technologies. At the operational level, the planetGOLD Peru project (GEF/UNDP and national partners) was the main vehicle for introducing and disseminating mercury-free technologies: training, technical guides, and the provision and commissioning of gravimetric plants. The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) has signed technical cooperation agreements (Artisanal Gold Council) and has participated in the provision and promotion of clean technologies for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), in coordination with the Ministry of the Environment (MINAM) and partners.

Ecuador_Justification:
Articles 35 and 37 of the Regulations for the Special Regime of Small-Scale Artisanal Mining provide for the development of processes to promote clean technologies to reduce emissions and waste; and, specifically, they provide for the adoption of procedures that avoid the use of mercury.

In this regard, the PlanetGOLD project promotes the transition to mercury-free technologies and reduces the use of mercury in mining activities, in accordance with the Minamata Convention. The project provides access to clean technologies, financing, and ongoing training.

Guyana_Justification:
The promotion of safer mercury-free technologies is a key component of Guyana's efforts to comply with the Minamata Convention and represents one of the strongest areas of government support for sustainable ASM practices. The Guyana National Action Plan
for Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining includes comprehensive strategies for the reduction of mercury use and the promotion of mercury-free technologies. The government has collaborated extensively with international organizations, including the planetGOLD project, the Artisanal Gold Council, and Conservation International, to introduce and promote mercury-free processing technologies to miners. These initiatives have included the development of handbooks on mercury-free processing technology, workshops on low-impact exploration and mercury-free processing, and capacity-building programs. The Mercury Act 2020 specifically prohibits the use of mercury in artisanal and small-scale gold mining, creating a regulatory framework that necessitates the adoption of alternative technologies.

Suriname_Justification: The Surinamese government has recently begun supporting initiatives to promote mercury-free mining technologies, but these efforts are still in early stages and not yet part of a comprehensive national strategy. Significant developments occurred in late 2024 and 2025, including the launch of new initiatives to reduce mercury use and participation in regional conferences focused on sustainable alternatives to mercury in ASGM. In November 2024, Suriname launched a new initiative specifically aimed at reducing mercury use in mining operations. In July 2025, the EMSAGS Project and WWF-Guianas hosted a regional conference on sustainable alternatives to mercury use in artisanal and small-scale gold mining, indicating growing regional cooperation on this issue. Suriname's ratification of the Minamata Convention on Mercury in 2017 also demonstrates commitment to addressing mercury use. However, these are relatively recent developments, and there is limited evidence of systematic, long-termgovernment programs to promote mercury-free technologies or provide technical and financial support for miners to transition to alternative methods.

Venezuela_Justification: In formal terms, the government's legal and rhetorical framework promotes the use of clean technologies; however, the effective implementation of support programs remains deficient and lacks transparency. Venezuelan regulations promote the replacement of mercury with clean technologies, primarily under Joint Resolution No. 090 and No. 15-06-01 (2015) and the regulations of the Ministry of People's Power for Ecological Mining Development. These provisions establish the mandatory use of non-polluting technologies and include a commercial incentive for gold obtained through environmentally friendly methods, which can be sold directly to the Central Bank of Venezuela.

Question: Are women, traditional communities and Indigenous peoples specifically included in sustainable ASM initiatives?
Bolivia_Justification: Mining regulations only mention Indigenous peoples, recognizing their rights and including them in prior consultation processes and participation in environmental management. However, there are no regulations or institutional public programs that include women and Indigenous people in sustainable initiatives; it is NGOs and projects that generate these types of initiatives. For example, gender is a cross-cutting issue in the PlanetGOLD project. The Indigenous Women of the Amazon Program, promoted by Conservation International, can also be mentioned.
Bolivia_Sources:
- PlanetGOLD Bolivia. https://www.planetgold.org/es/bolivia
 - Conservation International. Available at: https://www.conservation.org/bolivia/INICIATIVASDESTACADAS/women-fellowship
Brazil_Justification:
Research on Criterion 51, concerning the inclusion of women and indigenous peoples in sustainable artisanal and small-scale mining (ASSM) initiatives in Brazil, reveals a complex and often contradictory scenario. While some initiatives and milestones recognize the importance of these groups, the reality on the ground, especially regarding illegal mining, presents significant challenges.

The IRMA Standard for Responsible Mining [1] provides a framework that, in principle, supports the inclusion of women and indigenous peoples in ASSM. Specifically, Chapter 3.6, dedicated to Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining, highlights the involvement of men and women in ASSM activities [1, p. 292]. It emphasizes the need to engage diverse stakeholders, including informal operators and associations, and suggests that different approaches may be necessary for men and women.

However, the reality of illegal mining, especially in indigenous territories, is marked by violence, exploitation, and human rights violations. Reports indicate that the presence of illegal miners on indigenous lands leads to increased violence, including sexual violence against indigenous women and girls.
Brazil_Sources: [2] CIMI. (2022). Report on Violence Against Indigenous Peoples in Brazil. Available at: https://cimi.org.br/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/relatorio-violencia-contra-os-povos-indigenas-no-brasil-2021-cimi.pdf
Colombia_Justification: Women and Indigenous Peoples are partially included in some sustainable ASM initiatives, but not comprehensively in all national policies. The Better Gold Initiative includes specific criteria for gender equity and the participation of Indigenous communities. Prior consultation with Indigenous Peoples is constitutionally mandated, but there are no specific programs that systematically integrate women and Indigenous Peoples into sustainable ASM initiatives at the national level.

•Establishes prior consultation for Indigenous Peoples

•URL: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=1392
•Regulates prior consultation
Peru_Justification: Yes, women and Indigenous peoples are explicitly included in policies and plans; however, their effective benefit depends on implementation (resources, inter-institutional coordination, quality of consultation processes, and regional reach). The Ministry of Energy and Mines approved the National Gender Plan for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining 2023–2025 (Ministerial Resolution No. 524-2023-MINEM/DM), which incorporates measures to promote the participation, training, and empowerment of women in small-scale and artisanal mining. Furthermore, the National Multisectoral Policy for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining to 2030 (Supreme Decree No. 016-2022-EM) requires territorial and intersectoral approaches that consider local populations and coordination with competent authorities; in addition, the State must respect the right to prior consultation for measures that affect Indigenous peoples (Law No. 29785 / Convention 169). Nevertheless, the participation of these vulnerable groups remains a challenge.

Ecuador_Justification: There are no regulations specifically aimed at including gender and intercultural approaches in sustainable artisanal and small-scale farming (ASF) initiatives. However, Ecuador does have a law on the purple economy that promotes the inclusion of these approaches from a labor perspective, and the UNDP is promoting a project to this end.
Ecuador_Sources: N/A
Guyana_Justification: While there are some efforts to include women and Indigenous peoples in sustainable ASM initiatives, these efforts are not comprehensive or systematically integrated across all programs. The National Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI) Policy for Guyana outlines a strategic line for 'Inclusion in the Mining Industry' which includes promoting the 'inclusion of women in decision-making in the mining industry through training.' However, the policy does not explicitly mention sustainable ASM initiatives, nor does it specifically address the inclusion of Indigenous peoples within these initiatives. The NAP does mention the inclusion of women to schaive gender equality in the ASM sector. Some international projects, such as those implemented by the Artisanal Gold Council, have included agreements with the Guyana Women Miners Organization to involve women in mining and enhance their capacity in business planning and mercury-free processing. However, these initiatives are project-specific rather than part of a comprehensive national strategy for inclusion.

ocialInclusionPolicy.pdf

Suriname_Justification: The EMSAGS project Actively works on gender assessments to understand how mining policies impact men and women differently in districts like Brokopondo and Sipaliwini. Women make up a smaller percentage of the direct mining workforce (est. 15–20%), they are centrally involved in the "service economy" surrounding mines (cooking, camps, trading). The NAP highlights that women face unique risks, including exposure to mercury in domestic settings (burning amalgam at home) and economic dependency that makes them less able to negotiate safer working conditions. It alos proposes strategies to include gender-mainstreaming in formalization processes to ensure women can hold mining titles and access financing, preventing them from being sidelined by formalization that privileges male equipment owners. In addition, does the planetGOLD Suriname project explicitly track gender metrics (aiming for ~50% female beneficiaries in knowledge sharing) and focuses on responsible supply chains that account for the social welfare of marginalized community members. However, ASGM activities in Suriname occur almost exclusively in the interior, on lands traditionally inhabited by Indigenous and Maroon (Tribal) communities which are formally not recognized by the government.The NAP process (and the EMSAGS project) emphasize Free, Prior, and Informed Consent (FPIC) and recognizes that these communities are "doubly" affected—both as participants in mining (for economic survival) and as victims of the resulting environmental degradation (mercury in fish, their primary protein source).Although this is emphasized, there is weak implementation of th FPIC process.

Question: Are there governmental programs to collaborate with NGOs, academic institutions, or international agencies to promote sustainable ASM practices?
Bolivia_Justification: In addition to what has already been mentioned in the justification, the work of several NGOs, such as WCS Bolivia, can be highlighted. WCS Bolivia has worked on the following projects: “Capacity Building for Reducing the Impacts of Mining in the Protected Areas of Apolobamba, Madidi, and Pilón Lajas,” funded by CEPF; “Incorporating Best Practices in Mining Operations in the Madidi-Pilón Lajas-Cotapata Conservation Corridor in Bolivia,” also funded by CEPF; and “Creation of a Regional Strategy to Integrate Environmental and Social Safeguards into Mining Practices in the Tropical Andes Hotspot.” Helvetas funded the project “Implementation of Cleaner Production Measures in the Upper Suches River Basin.” These are just some of the many NGOs that have developed research and projects related to gold mining and artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil demonstrates clear government programs and active collaboration with NGOs, academic institutions, and international agencies to promote sustainable practices in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASGM), as evidenced by its partnerships with UNEP, participation in the Global Coalition for Action on ASGM, and the National Action Plan for Mercury-Free Gold Mining.

The Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) is developing a partnership with UNEP for a small-scale gold mining project, aiming to promote safer and more sustainable practices in the sector. Brazil also participates in the Global Coalition for Action on Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASGM), which seeks to transform the sector into a catalyst for sustainable development.

The National Action Plan for Mercury-Free Gold Mining, launched in November 2022, is a collaborative effort involving several Brazilian ministries, with financial support from the GEF and technical assistance from UNEP and the Foundation for Technological Development in Engineering (FDTE).

Colombia has multiple government programs collaborating with NGOs, academic institutions, and international agencies to promote sustainable practices in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The Ministry of Mines and Energy officially collaborates with the Better Gold Initiative, which includes partnerships with numerous organizations.
The country collaborates with USAID through the "Legal Gold" program, with the UNDP on the Planet Gold project, and with the Alliance for Responsible Mining (ARM).

Peru_Justification: Yes, the Peruvian government does promote and implement collaborative programs with NGOs, universities, and international agencies to foster sustainable practices in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), generally through pilot projects, agreements/MoUs, and technical assistance. The PlanetGOLD project is an example of this collaboration, implemented by the UNDP in partnership with the Ministry of the Environment (MINAM) and the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM), to reduce/eliminate mercury use, introduce clean technologies (gravity sorting plants), provide training, and facilitate access to financing and markets in pilot regions. Furthermore, the government has signed memoranda of understanding and maintains collaborative relationships with international technical organizations and initiatives such as the Better Gold Initiative for training, traceability, and strengthening responsible value chains.

Guyana_Justification:
Guyana demonstrates an excellent track record of governmental programs that collaborate with NGOs, academic institutions, and international agencies to promote sustainable ASM practices. The National Mineral Sector Policy Framework explicitly supports stakeholder
participation and evidence-based decision-making, providing a foundation for collaborative approaches. The Environmental Protection Agency has partnered with the World Wildlife Fund Guianas with support from the Netherlands Commission on Environmental Assessment, leading to training workshops aimed at building capacity for Strategic Environmental Assessment in the ASGM sector. The Artisanal Gold Council has formed extensive partnerships with the
Government of Guyana through the Ministry of Natural Resources and the GGMC, resulting in projects focused on building a legal, professional, and mercury-free ASGM sector. The government has also collaborated with Conservation International, the United Nations Environment Programme, and the Global Environment Facility on major initiatives like the planetGOLD project and the development of the National Action Plan for ASGM.

Suriname_Justification: Yes, the Government of Suriname actively collaborates with various stakeholders through several major programs to promote sustainable Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASM). These initiatives primarily focus on phasing out mercury, formalizing the sector, and introducing environmentally responsible mining technologies. Two current programs facilitating these collaborations are EMSAGS and planetGOLD Suriname. Both are implemented in partnership with the Ministry of Natural Resources (MNR) and the National Institute for Environment and Development in Suriname (NIMOS), currently transformed to the National Environmental Authority (NMA). International agencies such as the UNDP, GEF and the IDB provide funding, technical oversight in alignment with global standards such as the Minamata convention. Academic institutions such as the Anton de Kom Univeristy, in particular the Geology and Mining Department facilitates knowledge exchange and student internships focused on sustainable development. Also, the Center for Agricultural Research in Suriname researches the rehabilitation of mined-out lands, often in coordination with government environmental mandates.

Venezuela_Justification:
The government excludes organized civil society from the mining sector, although it promotes certain "initiatives of the mining population" at a discursive level. There is no known experience of collaboration with NGOs.

Regarding other actors, there is relevant information about the Piar Mission, a government program that, in theory, aimed to train, organize, and empower small-scale and artisanal miners, fostering safer practices and "ecological mining development," including references to so-called "eco-mining" as a mechanism to reduce environmental impact.

Some state mining entities have received support from international mechanisms, such as the UNDP's Small Grants Programme (SMGP) and the GEF (Global Environment Facility). Technological partnerships have also been established with institutions like CENDITEL, which collaborates on the development of open-source technologies and tools for organizational improvement within the sector.

Question: Are there clear protection mechanisms for individuals working in areas of mining?
Bolivia_Justification: In addition to the General Law on Hygiene, Occupational Safety and Welfare, there is the Technical Safety Standard NTS 009/23 of the Ministry of Labor and Social Welfare. This technical standard establishes mandatory guidelines for the presentation and approval of the Occupational Safety and Health Management Program (PGSST). This document aims to prevent occupational risks, work accidents and occupational diseases, guaranteeing safe and healthy conditions for workers; however, there are no specific mechanisms for people who work in mining areas.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, there are protection mechanisms for mining workers. Brazilian labor law establishes occupational safety and health standards, which are monitored by the Ministry of Labor and Employment. Furthermore, inspection operations rescue workers from conditions analogous to slavery in illegal mining areas, demonstrating the State's role in protecting workers' rights.

Guyana has a legal framework for worker protection through the Occupational Safety and Health (OSH) Act. However, reports from the Ministry of Labour indicate significant challenges in the practical implementation and enforcement of these mechanisms, particularly in
the ASM sector, leading to high fatality rates and under-reporting of incidents. This suggests that while mechanisms exist on paper, their practical application is insufficient.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname lacks clear protection mechanisms for individuals working in mining areas, leaving workers vulnerable to various forms of violence, exploitation, and unsafe working conditions. This absence of protection is particularly problematic given the documented presence of criminal organizations and violence in mining zones. The informal nature of much of the ASM sector means that many workers operate outside the formal labor law framework and are not covered by standard worker protection mechanisms. The OAS report documents the involvement of criminal groups in mining operations, creating dangerous working environments where workers may face threats, violence, or exploitation without recourse to effective protection. The remote location of many mining areas compounds the protection challenges. Limited state presence in these regions means that workers cannot rely on timely intervention by security forces when threats arise. The vast and often inaccessible nature of mining zones makes it difficult for authorities to monitor working conditions or respond to incidents. The lack of formal employment relationships in much of the ASM sector means that workers often lack access to social protection systems, health insurance, or other safety nets that might provide some measure of security. Many workers are migrants or operate on informal arrangements that provide no legal protections. Environmental hazards, including mercury exposure and unsafe mining practices, create additional risks for workers. The absence of comprehensive health and safety regulations specific to ASM operations, combined with limited enforcement capacity, means that workers often face dangerous conditions without adequate protection. The involvement of organized crime in mining operations creates particular risks for workers who might witness illegal activities or who might be coerced into participating in criminal enterprises. Without clear protection mechanisms, workers have little recourse when facing such situations.
Suriname_Sources:
- OAS Report: On the Trail of Illicit Gold Proceeds: Strengthening the Fight Against Illegal
Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
- No evidence of specific worker protection mechanisms found in comprehensive research
Venezuela_Justification:
There is a legal obligation to guarantee safe and healthy working conditions in mines, although in practice these provisions are not met and are ignored during government inspections.

Generally speaking, the Organic Law on Prevention, Working Conditions and Environment (LOPCYMAT) establishes obligations for all companies, regardless of sector, regarding the protection of their workers. The applicable legal framework includes the Constitution, ILO Convention No. 169 concerning Indigenous and Tribal Peoples (adopted in 1989 and ratified by Law No. 41 of December 17, 2000), the Organic Law on Labor, Workers and Working Women (LOTTT) (2012), and LOPCYMAT (2005).

Brazil_Justification:
The Brazilian government, particularly through the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) and the National Mining Agency (ANM), demonstrates interest in recognizing and promoting certifications for responsible mining practices, including those relevant to artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM). While there is no explicit and direct governmental endorsement of 'Fairmined' or 'CRAFT' certifications as official government schemes, there are clear indications of related governmental and institutional efforts to align with international standards and promote traceability and responsible practices in the ASM sector.

Key findings:

Recognition of Standards-Based Certifications (MME/ANM): The Brazilian government, through the MME, has an initiative entitled 'Recognition of Standards-Based Certifications'. This initiative, active from November 1, 2023 to January 22, 2024, aimed to establish guidelines for the use of accredited inspection of mining companies. The central idea is to encourage the creation of and adherence to certifications based on technical standards and models that promote better environmental, social, and governance (ESG) practices.

ANM Global Initiative for Small-Scale Mining: The National Mining Agency (ANM) is integrating a global initiative to transform small-scale mining. This initiative, supported by the World Gold Council, governments, companies, and civil society, aims to transform a sector marked by informality and violations into a lever for sustainable development. Brazil is considered a strategic actor in this process.

Partnership between the OCB System and ARM for the CRAFT Code: The OCB System (Organization of Brazilian Cooperatives) and the Alliance for Responsible Mining (ARM) have established a partnership to bring the OECD's international Due Diligence benchmark to Brazil, adapted to the reality of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), through the implementation of the CRAFT Code. This partnership, formalized on June 27, 2023, aims to support Brazilian mining cooperatives in challenges related to traceability, management, and risk mitigation.

Fairmined and CRAFT as Fundamental Tools: An article by ApexBrasil (Brazilian Trade and Investment Promotion Agency) from May 14, 2025, highlights that tools such as Fairmined, CRAFT, and RJC certifications are fundamental to ensuring transparency and generating trust in responsible mining.

In short, although there is no direct governmental 'promotion' of Fairmined or CRAFT as official Brazilian certifications, the government and related institutions are actively engaged in creating frameworks and supporting initiatives that recognize and align with international standards such as CRAFT, and acknowledge the importance of certifications like Fairmined for responsible MAPE (Management of Endocrinology, Employment, and Tourism).

The Alliance for Responsible Mining (ARM), the organization behind the Fairmined standard, is a strategic partner of the BGI in Colombia. Furthermore, the CRAFT code has been piloted at artisanal gold mining sites in Colombia.

Suriname_Justification: Yes, the government of Suriname recognizes and promotes international certification schemes and standards for sustainable mining, primarily focusing on mercury reduction in artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM) and environmental management, largely through partnerships with international organizations and inittiatives. The government of Suriname acceded to the Minamata Convention on Mercury in 2018 and is working with the UN Development Programme (UNDP) and the Global Environment Facility (GEF) to implement its National Action Plan (NAP) to reduce and eliminate mercury use in the ASGM sector. Also, Suriname is an EITI-implementing country, promoting transparency in reporting on environmental, social, and economic impacts of mining, including future oil and gas revenues.

Question: Is there a government incentive for the adoption of gold certification?
Bolivia_Justification: The Regulations to Law No. 1503 have implicitly established incentives; however, these are not related to gold certifications. The incentives are related to domestic sales aimed at strengthening international gold reserves. Sellers are exempt from paying the Mining Royalty (Art. 10), and another incentive is the discount applied to the international price (Art. 6).

 - Bolivia Tributaria (2023). Royalties and bonuses: incentives for cooperatives that sell gold to the Central Bank of Bolivia. Available at: https://bibliotecatributariabolivia.com/2023/07/18/regalias-y-premios-incentivos-para-cooperativas-que-vendan-oro-al-banco-central-de-bolivia/

Brazil_Justification:
Brazil does not have legally structured direct incentives, but it does provide technical and political support for initiatives that promote certification and traceability, such as the "Green Gold Seal" and the policies of the DDSM/MME. These efforts constitute indirect and structural incentives for responsible gold certification.

Brazil has been actively pursuing initiatives to promote gold certification, particularly for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), as a way to combat illegality and foster sustainable practices. A significant initiative is the "Green Gold Seal" project, which focuses on the certification and traceability of gold produced in the Pan-Amazon region. This project involves Brazil and other member countries of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO).

The "Green Gold Seal" project is led by the Instituto Biotec Amazônia, which won a federal government tender to implement this initiative. The project aims to transform artisanal mining into legal and sustainable operations by providing a system for certified and traceable gold. The process involves microanalysis using X-ray fluorescence and other digital technologies to create a geochemical database for each gold-producing region.

From the perspective of government agencies, the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME), through its Department of Sustainable Development in Mining (DDSM), plays a crucial role in strengthening public policies for sustainable mining. The DDSM's objectives include integrating mineral policy with environmental, social, and territorial policies, and focusing on incentives for social responsibility and corporate sustainability in the mining sector. Its work is aligned with the Minamata Convention on Mercury, aiming to reduce and eliminate the use of mercury in MAPE (Mining, Energy, and Preservation).

While direct financial incentives for individual miners to adopt certification are not explicitly detailed as generalized programs, government support for projects like the "Green Gold Seal" and its broader policy framework for sustainable mining indicate a structure of indirect incentives. The certification itself, by increasing market value and ensuring legality, acts as a significant economic incentive for producers.

Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, there are no direct and specific government incentives aimed at promoting the adoption of responsible gold certifications. However, the country participates in international economic incentive schemes linked to certified and formalized production.

The main initiative currently in operation is the Better Gold Initiative (BGI), a public-private partnership between the Swiss Better Gold Association (SBGA) and the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO), with the Colombian Ministry of Mines and Energy acting as the official counterpart. Through this mechanism, formalized and certified miners receive a development premium of US$1.00 per gram of responsible gold, with 70% allocated to community social and environmental projects and 30% to strengthening the productivity of mining operations.

However, there are no official regulations that grant exclusive economic or regulatory benefits for adopting international certifications. The proposed new MAPE Law includes incentives for formalization, but it does not specify whether it will include incentives for certification.

Suriname_Justification:
There are no documented government incentives for the adoption of gold certification in Suriname. The research conducted across government websites, policy documents, and international assessments found no evidence of tax incentives, preferential treatment, financial support, or other mechanisms to encourage miners to pursue certification for responsible gold production. 
The Mining Decree of 1986 and subsequent regulations do not include provisions for incentivizing certification schemes. The absence of such incentives, combined with the lack of recognition of international certification schemes, creates a policy environment that does not encourage responsible mining practices or participation in certified supply chains. This gap in policy represents a missed opportunity to drive improvements in environmental and social performance in the ASM sector through market-based mechanisms.

Bolivia_Justification: The Plurinational Strategic Policy for the Integrated and Sustainable Management of Biodiversity, Action Plan 2019-2030, establishes in Strategic Area 4, under the action line "Implementation of actions for the maintenance of biodiversity in the development of economic-productive activities and strategic projects," the goal for 2025 of developing a protocol for the proper handling of biodiversity in mining. No specific actions for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) were found.

Question: Does the country have specific programs, targets, or funding mechanisms for ecosystem restoration in areas affected by ASM gold mining?
Bolivia_Justification: Environmental and mining regulations do not establish restoration activities. Environmental regulations require the submission of closure and abandonment plans (Supreme Decree No. 3549), and the RAAM (Regulations for the Management of Mining Areas) in various articles (Articles 45, 46, paragraphs 16 and 18), 50, paragraph 5), 51, 53, paragraph 4), 67, 68, 69, and Chapter IV of Title VIII) establish the submission of a closure and rehabilitation plan for the area. However, there are no specific programs for ecosystem restoration.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities. Available at: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/149_DS_24782.pdf
 - D.S. No. 3549. Available at: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Partially. Brazil has legal instruments and environmental programs of national scope that address the recovery of areas degraded by mining activities, including artisanal gold mining (MAPE). However, there is no specific federal program dedicated exclusively to the restoration of ecosystems degraded by MAPE, nor are there exclusive financing mechanisms for this purpose.

Brazilian legislation obliges entrepreneurs, including formalized artisanal miners, to recover areas degraded by mining, as stipulated in the Mining Code (Decree-Law No. 227/1967) – Article 47 and Decree No. 9.406/2018 (which regulates the Mining Code) – Article 98.

Law No. 12.651/2012 (New Forest Code) – Article 41: creates the Environmental Regularization Program (PRA) and mechanisms for the recovery of degraded Permanent Preservation Areas and Legal Reserves, also applicable to areas affected by mining.

Furthermore, the National Program for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Proveg), created by Decree No. 8,972/2017, and the National Plan for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Planaveg) aim to restore 12 million hectares by 2030, including areas degraded by mining, but without specifying the type of activity or source of degradation.

Axis 5.2 of Planaveg proposes to “support the implementation of restoration projects linked to large-scale compensation involving large financial volumes (originating from energy, mining, transportation, water, and sanitation projects)”.

Axis 8.3 of Planaveg proposes to “regulate mechanisms for the allocation and monitoring of a portion of the financial compensation resources from oil and natural gas exploration, mining, and energy generation activities for native vegetation restoration (RVN) projects, prioritizing public areas and socially and/or environmentally sensitive areas”.

These goals acknowledge mining as a driver of degradation and foresee the use of compensatory resources for ecosystem recovery, but do not directly address artisanal mining (MAPE).

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has policies, programs, and financing mechanisms aimed at restoring degraded ecosystems, although there is no specific national program directed exclusively at areas affected by artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM).

The National Restoration Plan 2015–2030, led by the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MinAmbiente), establishes general goals for ecological restoration, rehabilitation, and recovery of strategic ecosystems, including areas impacted by extractive activities. However, this instrument does not explicitly identify the areas degraded by ASM nor does it define specific funding lines for them.

The Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs) implement regional ecological restoration programs financed with their own resources, environmental royalties, and international cooperation. These actions have included interventions in territories affected by gold mining, but they are carried out in a fragmented and decentralized manner, without a national coordinating framework.

The program includes the development of a National Information System for Comprehensive Remediation (SINARI) to inventory sources of pollution from activities such as mining. However, no specific programs with clearly defined goals and funding were identified for ecosystem restoration in areas specifically affected by artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Guyana_Justification: Guyana has initiated several policy and regulatory frameworks that address environmental management and reclamation in the mining sector, including ASM, but lacks specific, dedicated programs with explicit targets and funding mechanisms solely for ecosystem restoration in ASM-affected areas. The National Mineral Sector Policy Framework mentions "reclamation of mined-out areas" as a strategy and emphasizes the "sustainable development principle." The Code of Practice for Mine Reclamation and Closure Plans provides detailed guidance for restoration and remediation, with its mission "To foster sustainable practices so as to prevent and minimize adverse long-term environmental impacts generated by small- and medium-scale mines in Guyana." The Guyana National Action Plan for ASGM addresses environmental management and includes strategies for mercury reduction. However, these frameworks provide guidance and general requirements rather than constituting comprehensive, funded programs with specific targets for ecosystem restoration in ASM areas.

Suriname_Justification: Yes, Suriname has several specific programs and is currently developing dedicated funding mechanisms to address ecosystem restoration and environmental management in areas affected by Artisanal and Small-scale Gold Mining (ASGM). The Improving Environmental Management in the Mining Sector of Suriname (EMSAGS) project is a flagship initiative funded by the Global Environment Facility (GEF) and implemented by the UNDP. This project focuses on reducing the negative effects of ASGM on biodiversity, forests, and water. This is done by disseminating environmentally responsible mining technologies and methods, and developing mechanisms to address impacts across the entire mine life cycle. Another project is the planetGOLD Suriname (GEF GOLD+), which is a five year project that aims to fomalize the ASGM sector and promote mercury-free technologies in key mining districts. Also, Suriname is part of the Amazon Sustainable Landscapes Program, that works on strengthening the management of protected and productive landscapes to combat land degradation. In the NBSAP specific targets regarding ecosystem restoration are mentioned.

Venezuela_Justification:
From a discursive standpoint, there are initiatives focused primarily on reforestation efforts following the cessation of illegal mining and the development of the Orinoco Mining Arc project. These include Mission Tree, the Reforestation Plan for the Orinoco Mining Arc, and the Reforestation Campaign in Yapacana National Park—which includes aerial seeding. However, these initiatives not only have a limited scope but also lack an adequate scientific and technical foundation, as well as the necessary monitoring and support to achieve their objectives. They are characterized by grand announcements without real follow-up, few or no results, and a marked lack of transparency in spending and implementation.

Within the framework of the official programs for the Orinoco Mining Arc, for example, a plan has been proposed to recover approximately 8,800 hectares in Bolívar State for the period 2024-2025. However, in addition to covering less than 15% of the estimated degraded areas, these plans lack technical support to guarantee their effectiveness and include in their budgets the use of exotic forest species, which further compromises their ecological viability.

Question: Are there requirements or guidance for restoration, remediation, or compensation for degraded areas?
Bolivia_Justification:
Indeed, environmental and mining regulations establish general requirements for rehabilitation. The RAAM, in addition to closure and post-closure obligations, stipulates that an Audited Report must be prepared three years after closure (Art. 69), the content of which was approved by Administrative Resolution VMABCCGDF No. 048/20, dated October 22, 2022.

However, the regulations do not establish specific requirements for carrying out the restoration or rehabilitation of degraded areas.
Bolivia_Sources: - Administrative Resolution VMABCCGDF No. 048/20 dated October 22, 2022. Available at: https://es.scribd.com/document/900917441/Ra-Vmabccgdf-048-2020-Informe-Cierre-Auditado
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil has a comprehensive legal and regulatory framework that requires the restoration, remediation, and compensation of areas degraded by mining activities. These requirements are enshrined in the Federal Constitution (Article 225, Paragraph 2) and detailed in various decrees, laws, and normative instructions. The Degraded Area Recovery Plan (PRAD), established by Decree 97.632/89, is a crucial instrument that requires mining projects to present a detailed plan for environmental recovery, generally during the environmental licensing process with the Environmental Impact Study (EIA) and the Environmental Impact Report (RIMA).

The Mining Code Regulations (Decree No. 9.406, of June 12, 2018) further details the responsibilities of miners, including the prevention, mitigation, and compensation of environmental impacts, the preservation of workers' health and safety, the prevention of environmental disasters, and the environmental recovery of impacted areas. This includes mine closure and decommissioning of all facilities, such as tailings dams. Failure to comply with these obligations may lead to administrative, civil, and criminal liabilities, with sanctions described in Law 9.605/98 (Law on Environmental Crimes and Administrative Offenses) and Federal Decree 6.514/08, including fines and imprisonment.

Although the National Plan for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Planaveg) and the National Program for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Proveg) are not specific to artisanal mining (MAPE), both recognize mining activity as a driver of degradation and propose mechanisms for financial compensation and large-scale ecological restoration. Established by Decree No. 8,972/2017, Planaveg presents guidelines also applicable to the mineral sector:

Action 5.2 – Support the implementation of native vegetation recovery (RVN) projects linked to large-scale environmental compensation, including mining, energy, transportation, water, and sanitation projects.

Action 8.3 – Proposes the regulation of the allocation of part of the financial compensation resources from mining (CFEM) and other extractive sectors to ecological restoration projects and funds, prioritizing public and sensitive areas.

For artisanal and small-scale gold mining (MAPE), the Program to Support the Development of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (Pró-Mape), established by Decree No. 10,966/2022, aims to integrate and strengthen policies for sustainable development, encouraging best practices, formalization, and the promotion of health, assistance, and dignity in the communities involved.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has clear requirements for the restoration, remediation, and compensation of degraded areas. Decree 1076 of 2015 establishes specific restoration and compensation obligations in environmental licenses [15].
Resolution 1517 of 2012 establishes the Manual for the Allocation of Compensation for Biodiversity Loss [25]. Furthermore, environmental licenses are required to include decommissioning and abandonment plans.

Peru_Justification: Yes, in Peru there are regulations that require mining operators to assume environmental responsibility, including the restoration, remediation, or compensation of degraded areas. Law No. 27446 (the Environmental Impact Assessment System Law) requires that projects include measures for the prevention, mitigation, correction, or compensation of environmental impacts. Supreme Decree No. 040-2014-EM, in Article 16, establishes that the mining operator is responsible for the impacts at all stages of the project and must adopt measures for prevention, rehabilitation, and closure. Supreme Decree No. 045-2006-EM contains general provisions on environmental management in mine closure. However, these obligations are broad in scope and lack specific technical criteria; therefore, the existing guidelines are minimal and general.

Ecuador_Justification: Article 1 of the Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities includes among its objectives the establishment of measures for the rehabilitation and restoration of natural areas degraded by these activities. Articles 108 to 110 establish guidelines, which are framed within the closure and abandonment phase.

Guyana_Justification: Guyana has comprehensive requirements and guidance for restoration, remediation, and compensation for degraded areas affected by mining activities. The Code of Practice for Mine Reclamation and Closure Plans provides detailed guidance and requirements for the restoration and remediation of mined-out areas, with clear objectives including restoring mining sites to a satisfactory condition, eliminating health hazards, limiting pollution, and reclaiming areas for future use. The Environmental Protection Act includes provisions for compensation for environmental damage and loss of property due to environmental harm. The Mining Act 1989 provides for the suspension or revocation of mining licenses for non-compliance with environmental and social obligations. These legal and policy instruments create a strong framework for holding mining operators accountable for the environmental impacts of their activities and ensuring that degraded areas are properly restored.

Suriname_Justification: Yes,  the Mining Decree of 1986 provides the basic legal structure for mining rights, restoration and compensation, however it is often cited as outdated regarding modern remediation standards. A requirement is that mining permit holders to submit quarterly reports and follow general regulations.Because the 1986 Decree is broad, the government often negotiates specific Mineral Agreements with larger companies. These agreements function as standalone laws and typically include much stricter requirements for mine closure, remediation, and financial guarantees for restoration, however this is not the case in ASGM operations. For all mining operations, The Environmental Framework ACT (2020) establishes the National Environmental Authority (NMA) and mandates that all major projects, including mining, undergo an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA). The EIA must include specific mitigation and management plans for environmental impacts throughout the project's life cycle. Current recommendations emphasize the need for clearer liability regulations to ensure that those responsible for degradation are held accountable for clean-up. Compensation Mechanisms: While formal financial compensation for ecosystem loss is still evolving, the Environmental Fund Decree mentioned in the Environmental Framework ACT is one of the priority regulations being drafted to provide a structured way to finance the restoration of abandoned and orphaned mine sites. Therefor a score of 0.5 is assigned.

Venezuela_Justification: Each mining project must be based on a duly approved technical project that establishes environmental remediation and restoration measures through a Mine Closure Plan. There is no publicly available information demonstrating that any closure plan has been implemented by any mining company or group of small-scale miners. The legal basis consists of the Constitution, Decree No. 1,257 of April 25, 1996, and the specific authorizations for the impact on natural resources that must be granted by the ministry with environmental jurisdiction.

Question: Does the country require formal registration of all actors in the ASM gold supply chain (miners, traders, and exporters)?
Bolivia_Justification:
It should be clarified that, since mining is not characterized and therefore small-scale mining (ASM) is not defined in mining regulations, there is no specific formal registry for ASM.

However, Law No. 535, in Article 56, paragraph IV, states that SENARECOM will be responsible for registering and controlling the marketing of minerals and metals in the domestic market and international trade. Thus, SENARECOM is authorized to maintain the Mining Identification Number (NIM) registry, which is used to monitor mining operators and allows for the marketing of minerals, including gold (Article 87, paragraph b)). Furthermore, mineral traders must also have a registration, called the Withholding Agent Identification Number (NIAR) (Article 87, paragraph c)).

Ministerial Resolution No. 165/2017 regulates the scope and control of gold marketing from marginal deposits and small-scale mining. The same Resolution expands registration to include mineral trading companies, manufacturing companies, and jewelry stores.

In 2017, Supreme Decree No. 3405 was enacted, creating the Single Mining Registry (RUM) to provide legally recognized Mining Producers and Mining Operators, as defined by Law No. 535, with a unique identification code to expedite and facilitate various procedures within the state-owned mining and metallurgical sector. However, no evidence has been found that this RUM has been created or that a specific system exists.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil requires formal registration of the main actors in the MAPE gold supply chain. Artisanal miners must obtain a Mining Permit (PLG) from the National Mining Agency (ANM). Financial institutions and cooperatives that buy gold at the source must be authorized by the Central Bank and the ANM, and all transactions must be documented with invoices. A decision by the Supreme Federal Court (STF) in March 2025 eliminated the presumption of good faith, obligating all buyers, including exporters, to prove the legal origin of the gold, which reinforces the need for formal registrations throughout the chain.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has legal mechanisms that regulate the formal registration of certain actors in the gold supply chain, but these do not comprehensively cover all levels of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Decree 276 of 2015 established the Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM), administered by the National Mining Agency (ANM). This registration is mandatory for all mineral traders and buyers, who must prove the legal origin of the gold through certificates issued by mining titleholders or sales authorizations from formally registered artisanal miners. RUCOM is a key tool for traceability and control of mineral trade.

However, registration is not universally required for all artisanal and small-scale (ASSM) miners, especially in the case of subsistence or artisanal mining, defined in Article 31 of Law 685 of 2001 (Mining Code) and regulated by Decree 1666 of 2016. This type of mining is exempt from some formal requirements, and artisanal miners can trade gold through simplified authorizations, without mandatory individual registration with the National Mining Agency (ANM).

Meanwhile, gold exporters must be formally registered with the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN), comply with foreign exchange regulations, and present certificates of origin issued by the owners or traders registered in the Single Registry of Gold Exporters (RUCOM).

Peru_Justification: Yes, Peru requires the formal registration of all actors in the gold supply chain. In the case of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASM), producers must register in the Comprehensive Mining Formalization Registry (REINFO) for the duration of their formalization process, in accordance with Supreme Decree No. 1000-2016-2017. No. 018-2017-EM and Law No. 31007. Once this process is completed, they are transferred to the Registry of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining Producers, regulated by Law No. 27651 and its regulations (Supreme Decree No. 013-2002-EM). In the case of medium and large miners, they do not enter the REINFO, but rather require a mining concession granted by INGEMMET or the Regional Directorates of Energy and Mines, according to the Consolidated Text of the General Mining Law (Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM), in addition to having an approved EIA and the authorization to start operations granted by the MINEM. For their part, gold traders and processors must be registered in the Registry of Gold Traders and Processors (RECPO) created by Supreme Decree No. No. 012-2012-EM, a mandatory requirement for buying, selling, or stockpiling minerals of legal origin. Finally, gold exporters must be registered in the Single Taxpayer Registry (RUC) of SUNAT, complying with the provisions of the General Customs Law (Legislative Decree No. 1053), which requires proof of traceability and legal origin of gold for export.

In accordance with Article 9, paragraph d, of the Mining Law, the Mining Regulation and Control Agency maintains a mining registry. Guyana_Justification: Guyana has a comprehensive legal and regulatory framework for the formal registration of ASM gold supply chain actors, including miners, traders, and exporters. The Mining Act 1989 and the Guyana Gold Board (Dealers) Regulations 1997 clearly outline the requirements for obtaining licenses and authorizations, as well as the duties and obligations of these actors. The system includes provisions for application, fees, eligibility, operational conditions, record-keeping, and export procedures, indicating a well-established formal registration process.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname has established formal registration requirements for certain actors in the gold supply chain, but these requirements do not comprehensively cover all participants, particularly in the informal ASM sector. The regulatory framework requires mining rights holders to obtain formal licenses for exploration and exploitation activities, and gold exporters must obtain special category licenses issued by the Foreign Exchange Commission. According to the 2025 EITI Suriname Report, "The license granting the right to export gold is a special category license and is issued by the Foreign Exchange Commission." This demonstrates formal registration requirements for exporters. The Surinamese legal framework recognizes five types of mining rights: reconnaissance, exploration, exploitation, small-scale mining, and quarrying of building materials, indicating a structured licensing system for miners. However, significant gaps exist in the registration framework. The OAS report notes that an estimated 50-60% of the regional mining industry operates informally, creating substantial challenges for state supervision and control. Small and medium-sized gold mining companies do not directly export their product but sell to local buyers, who then sell to licensed export companies. This intermediate layer of local buyers and traders often operates without formal registration requirements, creating a significant gap in supply chain oversight. The informal nature of much ASM activity means many individual miners and small-scale operators work without formal permits or registration. While the legal framework exists for formal registration, enforcement and compliance in the ASM sector remain limited, particularly in remote mining areas where state presence is minimal.

Question: Are there specific permits or procedures required for exporting ASM gold?
Bolivia_Justification: The permits and requirements for gold exports are not exclusive to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), as ASM is neither characterized nor identified in Bolivian mining regulations. Therefore, the requirements and permits apply to all artisanal and small-scale mining operators (APMs) that export gold. Supreme Decree No. 5076 aims to regulate gold exports, authorizing the Central Bank of Bolivia (BCB) to issue the Export Certificate (CEO), which serves as the supporting document for customs clearance of exports. This procedure also includes completing Form M-03 of the National Service for Customs and Information Technology (SENARECOM) through the National System for Customs and Information Technology (SINACOM) platform, as mentioned in the justification.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, although there are no direct export permits for MAPE (Mining Areas of the Amazon), the Brazilian legal system channels MAPE gold into the formal domestic market through authorized financial institutions before export. Law No. 7,766/89 requires that gold from mining be sold to institutions authorized by the Central Bank. The recent overturning of the "good faith" rule by the Supreme Federal Court and the implementation of electronic invoicing for gold have increased control over the origin of gold, ensuring that only legally acquired gold can be exported.
Brazil_Sources:
Law No. 7,766, of May 11, 1989
 Exclusive: End of Good Faith in Gold Trade Reduces Declared Gold Mining Activity in the Amazon - Repórter Brasil
Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, gold exports are subject to general customs permits and procedures applicable to all actors in the mining sector, without distinction between gold from artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) and gold from medium- or large-scale operations.

Gold exporters must be registered with the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN) as foreign trade users, comply with the exchange regulations of the Central Bank of Colombia (Banco de la República), and submit the documentation required by Decree 1165 of 2019 (customs regime), including export declarations, commercial invoices, and certificates of origin for the mineral.

The Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM), established by Decree 276 of 2015, requires that all mineral transactions be accompanied by a legal certificate of origin issued by the mining titleholder or authorized trader. This requirement does not differentiate between types of mining, so gold from formalized artisanal and small-scale mining or from artisanal panning is exported under the same procedures as that from large-scale mining.
Colombia_Sources:
Decree 1165 of 2019 (establishing the customs regime in Colombia). Available at: https://www.suin-juriscol.gov.co/viewDocument.asp?id=30036618

Decree 276 of 2015 (regulating the Single Registry of Mineral Traders – RUCOM). Available at: https://www.suin-juriscol.gov.co/viewDocument.asp?id=30019750

National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN) – Procedures for gold exports. Available at: https://www.dian.gov.co/

Central Bank of Colombia – Foreign exchange regime for mineral export operations. Available at: https://www.banrep.gov.co/
Peru_Justification:
Yes. In Peru, the export of gold from Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASM) is subject to specific permits and procedures that ensure its formality and traceability:
Producer Formality: The gold must come from miners registered in the Comprehensive Registry of Mining Formalization (REINFO), according to Supreme Decree No. 018-2017-EM (Articles 1 and 2), or from those who have completed the process and are listed in the Administrative Registries of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining Producers, created by the Regulations of Law No. 27651 (Supreme Decree No. 013-2002-EM, Articles 4 and 11).
Authorized Marketing: Sales can only be made through individuals or legal entities registered in the Special Registry of Gold Traders and Processors (RECPO/RCMPM), in accordance with Supreme Decree No. 013-2002-EM. No. 027-2012-EM (Art. 4.1).
Export:
Exports are processed through the Customs Declaration of Goods (DAM) in the Single Window for Foreign Trade (VUCE).
Crime Prevention and Traceability:
Gold traders and exporters are considered obligated parties under Law No. 27693 (Financial Intelligence Unit - Peru, Art. 8), which requires them to prove the legal origin of the mineral.
Art. 9 of the same law imposes specific obligations on them: to identify and verify clients, maintain documentation, and report suspicious transactions to the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF).

Export guide: https://www.cooperacionsuiza.pe/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/guia_para_exportacion.pdf

Ecuador_Justification:
Gold traded with the Central Bank of Ecuador (BCE) must be of legal origin. Therefore, all interested individuals or legal entities must be authorized as Economic Agents for Gold Trading, which requires meeting the following requirements:
Having registered mining rights with the relevant Ministry, currently the Ministry of Energy and Non-Renewable Natural Resources.
Being up-to-date with tax obligations as determined by the Internal Revenue Service (SRI); and not being registered as a defaulting supplier to the State according to information provided by the National Public Procurement Service.
Not being involved in money laundering or the financing of terrorism, nor in crimes sanctioned for the production or illicit trafficking of narcotic and psychotropic substances.
Registering and obtaining authorization as an Economic Agent with the Central Bank of Ecuador (BCE).
Ecuador_Sources: Central Bank of Ecuador. Gold Trading. https://www.bce.fin.ec/comercializacion-del-oro/autorizacion-de-agentes-economicos-para-comercializacion-del-oro/
Guyana_Justification: Guyana has specific permits and procedures required for the export of gold from ASM. The Guyana Gold Board Act of 1981 establishes the Guyana Gold Board as the central authority for gold transactions, including export. While it initially restricts export to the Board, it also provides a mechanism (Section 9(1)) for the Board to authorize other persons to export gold under prescribed conditions. This indicates a clear regulatory framework for gold export.
