Suriname_Justification: Suriname has established specific permits and procedures for gold export, but these are not specifically tailored to ASM gold, creating challenges in distinguishing and tracking ASM-origin gold through the export process. The regulatory framework requires all gold exporters to obtain special category licenses from the Foreign Exchange Commission. According to the OAS report, "Conditions for granting a gold buying and export license" are established in the legal framework. All gold intended for export must be processed at the Kaloti Mint House Suriname (KMHS), which serves as the central processing facility for gold exports. However, the system lacks specific procedures for ASM gold. The 2025 EITI report notes that legal gold from various sources is often mixed in gold shops and processing facilities, making it extremely difficult to distinguish ASM gold from other sources during the export process. This mixing of gold from different sources undermines the ability to apply specific procedures or requirements to ASM gold exports. The absence of ASM-specific export procedures represents a significant gap in supply chain governance. Without the ability to distinguish ASM gold at the export stage, it becomes impossible to apply targeted due diligence, traceability requirements, or responsible sourcing standards specifically to the ASM sector. This limitation also makes it difficult to track the volume and value of ASM gold exports separately from other gold production. Royalty payments are collected from exports made by licensed companies, with fees deposited into the State's bank account at the Central Bank of Suriname (CBoS). However, since small and medium-sized miners sell to intermediaries rather than exporting directly, the royalty system does not provide direct oversight of ASM operations.

https://eitisuriname.gov.sr/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/A-25.56_EITISR-Final-Report-150725-Final-Version.pdf

Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
Venezuela_Justification: The exploitation and commercialization of gold are centralized by the government. Small-scale miners are required to sell their entire production to the Central Bank of Venezuela (BCV). Under the 2014 Gold Law, Joint Resolution No. 090 and No. 15-06-01 of the Ministries of People's Power for Petroleum and Mining establishes that all individuals, companies, or forms of association that carry out gold exploration and exploitation activities within the national territory must sell all gold-bearing material obtained, in any of its forms, to the BCV through the centers designated by said institution (Official Gazette No. 40,692, dated June 30, 2015). The legal basis is Decree No. 2,165 (2015) and the regulations pertaining to the Central Bank of Venezuela.

Question: Are files on ASM areas, including titles, licenses, embargoes, and monitored zones, available in open, updated formats compatible with geospatial analysis systems?
Bolivia_Justification:
It is evident that while the Mining Administrative Authority (AJAM) has a system that compiles information on mining areas, Special Administrative Entities (ATEs), mining contracts by grid and by ownership, reserves, and immobilized areas, its level of updating is unknown, as the information is not publicly accessible. Publication in the National Mining Gazette occurs when the AJAM orders the continuation of the Mining Administrative Contract (CAM) process and subsequently when the CAM is approved or rejected.

Although the Law of Administrative Procedures establishes in Article 18, paragraph I, that “individuals have the right to access archives, public records, and documents held by the Public Administration, as well as to obtain certificates or legalized copies of such documents, regardless of the form of expression—graphic, sound, image, or other—or the type of physical medium on which they appear,” the information at the AJAM is handled confidentially; that is, it is not easily accessible to the public.

On the other hand, the GEOBolivia portal has an atlas where general information on mining activities in Bolivia can be viewed; however, this would not be useful for the purposes of geospatial analysis and identification of MAPE.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Administrative Mining Jurisdictional Authority (AJAM). (2017). Mining Registry Regulations. Available at: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/reglamento_registro_minero-1.pdf
 - GeoBolivia. https://geo.gob.bo/apps/3181/embed#
 - Law No. 2341 on Administrative Procedures, dated April 23, 2002. Available at: https://www.dgac.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/2341.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Brazil offers open and up-to-date data on mining through SIGMINE and the ANM Mining Registry, in formats compatible with geospatial systems. However, the explicit and easily accessible categorization of all MAPE areas, including embargoed and monitored zones, and their direct link to inspection and judicial data, is not yet fully clear and accessible to the public, justifying the partial score.
Brazil_Sources:
Mining Geographic Information System (SIGMINE)
 Mining Registry System
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia makes some georeferenced information on the mining sector publicly available, but the files are not fully available in open formats or uniformly updated, and there is no specific identification of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) areas within official systems.

The National Mining Registry, managed by the National Mining Agency (ANM), offers georeferenced information on current, pending, or expired mining titles, available through its online map viewer. However, this system does not classify or distinguish areas associated with ASM, nor does it include specific information layers on subsistence mining, artisanal panning, or formalization processes. Furthermore, much of the information is published in limited PDF or KML formats, which restricts its interoperability with advanced GIS (Geographic Information Systems) platforms.

The Colombian Mining Information System (SIMCO), for its part, provides statistics and reports on production, marketing, and licensing, but not in an open format or downloadable in editable geospatial layers (such as interoperable shapefiles, GeoJSON, or WMS).

Although Law 1712 of 2014 (Transparency and Access to Public Information Law) requires state entities to publish their data in open and reusable formats, the updating and geospatial standardization of mining information still presents significant gaps, especially regarding artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) and areas under embargo or environmental monitoring measures.
Colombia_Sources:
National Mining Agency (ANM) – National Mining Registry. Available at: https://www.anm.gov.co/

Colombian Mining Information System (SIMCO). Available at: https://minenergia.gov.co/es/repositorio-normativo/normativa/sistema-de-informaci%C3%B3n-minera/

Law 1712 of 2014 (Law on Transparency and the Right of Access to National Public Information). Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=56882
Peru_Justification:
Yes, INGEMMET maintains the national mining registry with updated geospatial information that is publicly accessible through GEOCATMIN. REINFO also provides updated information on mining formalization areas in formats compatible with geospatial analysis. SENACE, MINEM, and platforms such as GeoBosques (SERFOR) make relevant data available that complement the traceability and monitoring of these areas.

However, certain specific information, such as liens or encumbrances (SUNARP), or some environmental management instruments, is often published in closed formats (PDF or direct queries), which partially limits its automatic integration into geospatial analysis systems.

GEOBOSQUES: https://geobosques.minam.gob.pe/geobosque/view/index.php
Ecuador_Justification: The Mining Regulation and Control Agency maintains a geoportal of the mining registry, which includes maps and information on special mining areas, protected areas, and restricted zones, but it is not specific to MAPE areas, nor does it include information on embargoes. This geoportal is available in open format.

Guyana_Justification: While geospatial data for medium and large-scale mining is available in compatible formats, the explicit lack of small-scale mining (ASM) tenure data significantly hinders full compliance with the criterion. The availability of data at a fee and the absence of an open data policy also suggest limitations in 'open' access.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname has made significant progress in digitizing and making mining-related information publicly accessible, but the system falls short of providing comprehensive, up-to-date, and fully geospatial-compatible data for all ASM-related information. The country has established online systems for mining license applications and has improved cadastre management. The EITI Suriname website provides access to a register of mining licenses, representing a positive step toward transparency. According to the 2024 EITI validation report, "Suriname should maintain a publicly available register or cadastre system with timely and comprehensive information on mining licenses," indicating ongoing efforts to improve the system. However, significant limitations remain. The 2016 EITI report noted that "there was no systematic register at GMD [Geological Mining Service] or MONR [Ministry of Natural Resources] displaying the flow, status, or outcome of applications." While improvements have been made since 2016, the research indicates that a fully comprehensive and regularly updated system is not yet in place. The current system lacks complete integration of all relevant ASM information, including embargoes and monitored zones, in formats that are fully compatible with modern geospatial analysis systems. The absence of real-time updates and comprehensive coverage of all ASM areas represents a significant limitation for effective monitoring and enforcement. The digitization efforts are part of broader regional trends, as noted in development minerals research highlighting the importance of digital mapping for mining safety and governance.

Question: Is there a strategy in place to ensure the traceability of gold from extraction to final sale?
Bolivia_Justification: Although the RUM (Unique Mining Registry) was established by regulation to provide a unique mining code to APMs (Mining Producers), no evidence of its implementation has been found, which could contribute to traceability. While APMs are registered through the NIM (Mining Identification Number), allowing them to sell their minerals, the primary traceability record would be through mineral sales, specifically the completion of Form M-02 and, in the case of exports, Form M-03, both issued by SENARECOM (National Mining and Metals Registry). SENARECOM, according to Article 87 of Law No. 535, is responsible for monitoring compliance with legal regulations governing the domestic and international trade of minerals and metals, maintaining the Mining Identification Number Registry, and other duties. However, since no supporting documentation is required during mineral trade, with the exception of the BCB (Central Bank of Bolivia), traceability mechanisms are lacking.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Law No. 535 on Mining and Metallurgy. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
 - SENARECOM. (n.d.). Requirements for the Commercialization of Minerals. Available at: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/requisitos-comercializacion-minerales.php
 - SGBI. Gold Value Chains in Bolivia, Colombia, and Peru: Incentives and Rationale for Formalization. Available at: https://ororesponsable.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Gold-Value-Chains-in-Bolivia-Colombia-and-Peru_-Incentives-and-Rationale-around-Formalisation-021224.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Brazil has traceability mechanisms, but they are still insufficient and have loopholes that allow illegal gold to enter the market. The Supreme Court's decision to end the presumption of good faith and the proposal of technologies such as DLT and molecular marking indicate an effort to improve the system, but full and effective implementation has not yet been achieved.
Brazil_Sources:
Escolhas Institute. (2022). Gold Tracking System: Challenges and Recommendations.
 Brasil Mineral. (March 20, 2025). GOLD | STF Ruling Ends Presumption of Good Faith in the Sale of Gold from Small-Scale Mines.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has regulatory instruments that establish the traceability of gold and other minerals, but it still lacks a comprehensive, fully operational, and nationwide system that allows tracking the mineral from extraction to final sale.

The Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM), created by Decree 276 of 2015, constitutes the first link in the traceability chain. Administered by the National Mining Agency (ANM), this registry requires gold traders and buyers to register and submit certificates of origin that prove the mineral's legality. However, RUCOM does not allow for continuous digital tracking of gold throughout the entire supply chain, limiting its traceability to the initial commercialization stage.
Colombia_Sources:
1.Decree 276 of 2015 - RUCOM
•URL: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=61724
Peru_Justification: In Peru, several registries regulate actors in the gold supply chain, such as the Comprehensive Registry of Mining Formalization (REINFO) for artisanal and small-scale miners, the Special Registry of Gold Traders and Processors (RECPO/RCMPM) for those who acquire and process the mineral, and the Single Taxpayer Registry (RUC) of SUNAT (the Peruvian tax authority) for exporters. These instruments allow for the identification of authorized entities and verification that operations are conducted formally, but they do not, in themselves, constitute a traceability mechanism, as they do not allow for continuous tracking of the gold's journey from the mine to the market. A true traceability mechanism is currently in a pilot phase in Pataz (La Libertad region), using the digital application QORI, created by Emergency Decree No. 006-2025. This system requires traders to register each gold transaction, generating a history that tracks the mineral from its extraction to its final destination. Although it represents a significant step towards effective traceability, it has not yet been implemented nationwide and faces interoperability and control challenges. In conclusion, the Peruvian regulatory framework ensures formality through mandatory registrations, but the only real traceability mechanism is QORI, whose expansion and strengthening will be key to guaranteeing comprehensive gold tracking and reducing the risks of illegal trade.
Peru_Sources:
QORI: https://www.rumbominero.com/peru/noticias/mineria/qori-implementan-aplicativo-para-trazibilidad-obligatoria-de-oro-en-pataz/
D.U. 006-2025: https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H1404173
Ecuador_Justification: There is no specific regulation that requires concrete traceability mechanisms.
Ecuador_Sources: Global Initiative Against Transnational Organized Crime. (2025, June 25). Illegal mining and gold supply chains in Ecuador. https://globalinitiative.net/analysis/illegal-mining-and-gold-supply-chai
Guyana_Justification: Guyana has established traceability mechanisms for gold through the Guyana Gold Board (GGB) and its associated regulations. However, despite these regulations, secondary sources indicate existing gaps in the supply chain that affect transparency and efficiency, particularly in the ASM sector. The lack of a fully integrated digital system and challenges in monitoring remote mining operations are key weaknesses.
Guyana_Sources:
Primary Sources:

https://ggb.gov.gy/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/Guyana-Gold-Board-Dealers-Regulations

- PlanetGOLD Guyana. 2022. Training Module 3. Chain of Custody & Due Diligence:
https://www.planetgold.org/sites/default/files/planetGOLD%20Guyana.%202022.%20Trai
ning%20Module%203.%20Chain%20of%20Custody%20%26%20Due%20Diligence.pdf
Suriname_Justification: Suriname lacks comprehensive traceability mechanisms to track gold from extraction to final sale, representing one of the most significant gaps in the country's mining governance framework. This absence of end-to-end traceability creates substantial vulnerabilities for illicit gold entering legitimate supply chains. The fundamental challenge is the widespread practice of mixing legal gold from various sources in gold shops and processing facilities. The OAS report explicitly states that "legal gold from various sources is often mixed in gold shops, making it difficult to track its origin." This mixing occurs at multiple points in the supply chain, effectively breaking any potential chain of custody that might exist. The absence of comprehensive laws governing the entire gold supply chain exacerbates traceability challenges. According to investigative research by the Global Investigative Journalism Network (GIJN), "There are no laws applicable to the whole supply chain, so certifiers are doing this job. Certifiers are industry schemes, paid for by the industry." This indicates that Suriname relies on voluntary industry schemes rather than mandatory government-enforced traceability systems. The EMSAGS project report notes that "To meet these standards, an increasing percentage of the gold sold on the international market should be traceable back to its source in Suriname." However, this statement reflects an aspiration rather than current reality, acknowledging that comprehensive traceability is not yet achieved. The lack of traceability mechanisms has serious implications for combating illegal mining and money laundering. Without the ability to track gold from mine to market, it becomes extremely difficult to distinguish legally mined gold from illegally extracted gold, facilitating the laundering of proceeds from illegal mining operations. The Security Law contains some rules on the value of processed and unprocessed gold and silver, stating that "Silver and gold can only be sold as such if their value is secured," but this does not constitute a comprehensive traceability system that tracks the physical movement and transformation of gold through the supply chain.
Suriname_Sources:
- OAS Report: On the Trail of Illicit Gold Proceeds: Strengthening the Fight Against Illegal Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
- GIJN: Investigating How Illegal Gold Gets Into the Legitimate Supply Chain (April 25,2025). URL:
https://gijn.org/stories/investigating-illegal-gold-mining-legitimate-supply-chain/
- EMSAGS: Final TSA Report ASGM in Suriname. URL:
https://emsags.org/media/yszg3k5z/final-tsa-report-asgm-in-suriname.pdf
- WWF: The Gold Marketing Chain in Suriname (2010). URL:
https://wwflac.awsassets.panda.org/downloads/2010__gold_mining_marketing_chain_heemskerk.pd
Venezuela_Justification: While the government monopoly and centralization formally mandate the sale of gold to the Central Bank of Venezuela—including registration and certification requirements, the formation of alliances, and mechanisms for the controlled collection of gold from small-scale miners—as declared traceability strategies, the reality falls significantly short of these objectives. Neither the origin of the gold nor the circumstances under which it was obtained are effectively recorded or publicly disclosed. Therefore, the quality and provenance of the gold are neither effectively verified nor monitored.

Categories: Gold Supply Chain Regulation and Transparency
Question: Is disclosure of transactions between miners, traders, and exporters mandatory?
Bolivia_Justification:
Mineral sales transactions must be registered using Form M-02, and in the case of exports, Form M-03 from SENARECOM.

However, the information provided by buyers (authorized trading companies) is only submitted to SENARECOM and is not subject to mandatory disclosure to the public.
Bolivia_Sources:
- SENARECOM, Regulatory Compendium. Available at: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/files/leyes/Compendio_Normativo_Minero_Metalurgico_SENARECOM.pdf
 - SENARECOM, Forms M-02 and M-03. Available at: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/formularios.php
Brazil_Justification: Yes, the disclosure of transactions is mandatory in Brazil. The implementation of the Electronic Invoice for Gold (NF-e Ouro) in 2023 made the issuance of an electronic invoice mandatory for the first purchase of raw gold, for imports, exports, and domestic transactions. The Supreme Court's decision against the presumption of good faith further reinforces the need for verification and disclosure of the origin of gold.
Brazil_Sources:
RFB Normative Instruction No. 2,138, dated March 29, 2023
 STF rules that the origin of gold in Brazil cannot be attested to by the seller’s word
Colombia_Justification:
There is no specific obligation to publicly disclose transactions between miners, traders, and exporters in Colombia. Although the RUCOM (Single Registry of Traders) requires certificates of origin, it does not mandate the public disclosure of specific transactions [3].
Commercial transactions are considered private information, and there is no legal framework requiring their systematic public disclosure.
Colombia_Sources:
1. Decree 276 of 2015
•URL: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=61724
•Does not establish public disclosure of transactions
Peru_Justification: In Peru, there is no general obligation to publicly disclose transactions between gold miners, traders, and exporters. What the regulations establish are registration and reporting mechanisms directed to the competent authorities, to which access is restricted. For example, traders and processors registered with the RECPO/RCMPM must keep a record of their operations and report periodically to the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM), while exporters are required to declare their shipments through the Customs Declaration of Goods (DAM) and the Single Window for Foreign Trade (VUCE), under the supervision of the National Superintendency of Tax Administration (SUNAT) and the Customs Administration. Likewise, within the framework of money laundering prevention, Law No. 27693 considers traders and exporters of precious metals as obligated parties, imposing the duty to identify clients, maintain documentation, and report suspicious transactions to the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF-Peru). More recently, the Emergency Decree... Decree No. 006-2025 has implemented a pilot system of mandatory digital traceability in Pataz through the QORI application, which allows for the real-time recording of transactions for auditing purposes. In conclusion, while transactions must be recorded and reported, this information is not publicly disclosed in open formats. The data remains under the control of state entities such as the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM), the National Superintendency of Tax Administration (SUNAT), and the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF), meaning that gold traceability is an administrative and fiscal process, rather than an exercise in public transparency.
Peru_Sources:
SBS: https://www.sbs.gob.pe/boletin/detalleboletin/idbulletin/2357

Ecuador_Justification: No evidence was found of specific mandatory requirements for the disclosure of transactions between miners, traders, and exporters. Although due diligence requirements exist for financial institutions and the Central Bank, there is no mandatory system for disclosing transactions across the entire supply chain.
Ecuador_Sources:
Global Initiative Against Transnational Organized Crime. (2025, June 25). Illegal mining and gold supply chains in Ecuador.

https://globalinitiative.net/analysis/illegal-mining-and-gold-supply-chains-in-ecuador/

Guyana_Justification: The disclosure of transactions between miners, traders, and exporters is mandatory in Guyana. The Guyana Gold Board (Dealers) Regulations 1997 require licensed dealers to maintain detailed records of all transactions and submit bi-weekly extracts to the GGB. This provides a clear legal basis for mandatory disclosure.
Guyana_Sources:
Primary Sources:
- Guyana Gold Board (Dealers) Regulations No. 4 of 1997:
https://ggb.gov.gy/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/Guyana-Gold-Board-Dealers-Regulations
-No.-4-of-1997.pdf
Suriname_Justification: Suriname does not have mandatory disclosure requirements for transactions between miners, traders, and exporters throughout the gold supply chain. This absence of transaction-level transparency represents a critical gap in the country's ability to monitor and regulate gold flows and detect potential illicit activities. While licensed gold exporters are required to report their exports for royalty calculation purposes to the Central Bank of Suriname (CBoS), this reporting focuses on aggregate export volumes and values rather than detailed transaction-level information that would reveal the specific sources and intermediary transactions. The 2023 Investment Climate Statement notes that "There are no domestic transparency measures requiring the disclosure of beneficial ownership information," indicating broader limitations in transparency requirements that extend to mining sector transactions. Although Suriname became a member of the Extractive Industry Transparency Initiative (EITI) in 2017, EITI reporting focuses on aggregate payments and revenues rather than individual transaction disclosure. The informal nature of much of the ASM sector means that many transactions occur outside formal banking systems and regulatory oversight. Small and medium-sized miners typically sell their gold to local buyers in cash transactions that are not systematically recorded or reported to authorities. These local buyers then aggregate gold from multiple sources before selling to licensed exporters, creating multiple layers of transactions that are not subject to mandatory disclosure. The absence of mandatory transaction disclosure requirements facilitates money laundering and makes it extremely difficult for authorities to track suspicious financial flows or identify the ultimate sources of exported gold. This gap is particularly problematic given the documented presence of criminal organizations in Suriname's gold sector.
Suriname_Sources:
- US State Department: 2023 Investment Climate Statements: Suriname. URL:
https://2021-2025.state.gov/reports/2023-investment-climate-statements/suriname/
- EITI Suriname Report 2025. URL:
https://eitisuriname.gov.sr/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/A-25.56_EITISR-Final-Report-150725-Final-Version.pdf
- OAS Report: On the Trail of Illicit Gold Proceeds: Strengthening the Fight Against Illegal
Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
Venezuela_Justification: With regard to gold, this obligation does not apply in the conventional sense, since the only legally permitted sale is to the Central Bank of Venezuela. No other financial institution may acquire gold directly from producers, nor are producers allowed to sell it to any other party.
Venezuela_Sources: Decree No. 2,165, with the Rank, Value and Force of an Organic Law, reserving to the State the activities of exploration and exploitation of gold and other strategic minerals. Official Gazette of the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela No. 6,210 Extraordinary of December 30, 2015.

Question: Does the legal framework require financial institutions to verify the legal provenance of gold-related transactions?
Bolivia_Justification: The regulations for financial institutions establish due diligence in a general way, without specifying for the mining sector or for transactions related to gold, so there is no record of it.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Financial Services Regulations BOOK III - TITLE X. Available at: https://bolivia.infoleyes.com/norma/4576/recopilaci%C3%B3n-de-normas-servicios-financieros-libro-iii-t%C3%ADtulo-x
 - Due Diligence in the Fulfilment of Commercial Obligations in Bolivia. Available at: https://ecglegal.com.bo/blog/debida-diligencia-en-el-cumplimiento-de-obligaciones-comerciales-en-bolivia
 - Law No. 393 on Financial Services. Available at: https://www.asfi.gob.bo/images/MARCO_NORMATIVO/LEY/LEY_393.pdf
 - ASFI - Compilation of Regulations. Available at: https://www.asfi.gob.bo/index.php/marco-normativo/recopilacion-de-normas-para-servicios-financieros.html
 - UIF Bolivia - Law No. 1768. Available at: https://www.uif.gob.bo/uif/images/stories/docs/marco_legal/ley_1768.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Yes, financial institutions are required to conduct due diligence. The Supreme Federal Court's decision in March 2025, which eliminated the presumption of good faith, imposes the obligation to verify the legal origin of gold. Furthermore, the Money Laundering Law (Law No. 9.613/98) and regulations from the Central Bank and COAF (Council for Financial Activities Control) require financial institutions to implement anti-money laundering policies, including client identification and reporting of suspicious transactions.
Brazil_Sources:
Supreme Federal Court (STF) invalidates rule establishing presumption of good faith in gold trading - STF News
 Law No. 9,613, of March 3, 1998
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia formally requires financial institutions and commercial entities involved in transactions related to gold and other precious metals to implement due diligence measures through mandatory anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing (AML/CFT) risk management regimes.

The Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Risk Management System (SARLAFT), overseen by the Financial Superintendency of Colombia, and the Comprehensive AML/CFT Self-Control and Risk Management System (SAGRILAFT), established by the Superintendency of Companies, require companies in the real sector, including those engaged in the trade, refining, or export of precious metals, to implement due diligence procedures, customer identification, beneficial ownership identification, and suspicious transaction reporting.

External Circular 100-000016 of 2020 from the Superintendency of Companies updated the SAGRILAFT regulations, establishing a risk-based approach that requires commercial companies, branches of foreign companies, and sole proprietorships to periodically assess the risk associated with transactions involving precious metals and stones, as well as the obligation to maintain verifiable records and reports to the Financial Information and Analysis Unit (UIAF).

The UIAF, attached to the Ministry of Finance and Public Credit, is the agency responsible for centralizing, analyzing, and cross-referencing financial and commercial information from obligated entities, including gold trading, export, and financing operations. Its work allows for the detection of irregularities and the prevention of the use of the gold sector for illicit activities.

Additionally, Law 2177 of 2021 (Mining Banking Law) promotes the formal financial inclusion of mining actors, while reinforcing transparency and regulatory compliance obligations, ensuring that access to the banking system is conditional upon compliance with the due diligence and traceability requirements established by the competent authorities.
Colombia_Sources:
External Circular 100-000016 of 2020 (Superintendency of Companies). Available at: https://www.supersociedades.gov.co/nuestra-entidad/normatividad/Paginas/circulares-externas.aspx

Law 2177 of 2021 (Mining Banking Law). Available at: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=174792

Financial Information and Analysis Unit (UIAF) – Functions and guidelines on the prevention of money laundering. Available at: https://www.uiaf.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: In Peru, financial institutions are required to apply due diligence measures to gold-related transactions, and good faith regarding the origin of the gold is not automatically presumed. Law No. 27693, which created the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF-Peru), includes financial institutions and precious metal traders and exporters as obligated parties, imposing on them the responsibility to identify their clients, verify information, maintain transaction records, and report any suspicious transactions. Additionally, the Superintendency of Banking, Insurance, and Private Pension Fund Administrators (SBS), through Resolution No. 2660-2015, requires supervised entities to implement Know Your Customer (KYC) policies and enhanced due diligence in transactions considered high-risk, including those related to gold trading. In practice, this means that banks must evaluate and document gold-related transactions and immediately report any indication of irregularity to the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF). In conclusion, Peruvian regulations establish that the financial traceability of gold depends on compliance with these control and verification mechanisms, which strengthens the transparency of operations and reduces the risk of illegally sourced gold entering the formal financial system.
Peru_Sources:
SBS: https://www.sbs.gob.pe/boletin/detalleboletin/idbulletin/2357
Law 27693: https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H824112
SBS Resolution 2060-2015: https://www.sbs.gob.pe/Portals/0/jer/Auto_Nuevas_Empresas/Normas_Comunes/7.%20Reg.%20Gesti%C3%B3n%20de%20Riesgos%20de%20Lavado%20de%20Activos_Res.%20SBS%20N%C2%B0%202660-2015.pdf
Ecuador_Justification: Financial institutions are involved in the due diligence process. The Central Bank of Ecuador conducts an initial due diligence review based on its internal compliance procedures for linking mining activities. Requirements for economic agents include a compliance certificate from the Financial and Economic Analysis Unit (UAFE).
Ecuador_Sources:
• Central Bank of Ecuador. (n.d.). Authorization of economic agents for gold trading. https://www.bce.fin.ec/comercializacion-del-oro/autorizacion-de-agentes-economicos-para-comercializacion-del-oro/
• UAFE. Financial and Economic Analysis Unit. https://www.uafe.gob.ec/
Guyana_Justification: While Guyana's Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (AML/CFT) Act of 2009 (amended in 2015) requires financial institutions to conduct due diligence, there are gaps in its specific application and enforcement in the gold sector. The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) has noted deficiencies in Guyana's AML/CFT framework, and the US Department of State has highlighted the vulnerability of the gold sector to money laundering. This suggests that while due diligence requirements exist, their implementation is not fully effective.
Guyana_Sources:
Primary Sources:
- Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Act 2009:
https://www.antimoneylaundering.gov.gy/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/AML-CFT-Act-No13-of-2009.pdf
Secondary Sources:
- Financial Action Task Force (FATF) - Guyana:
https://www.fatf-gafi.org/en/countries/detail/Guyana.html
- 2022 International Narcotics Control Strategy Report (INCSR) - Guyana:
https://www.state.gov/reports/2022-international-narcotics-control-strategy-report/volume
-ii-money-laundering/guyana/
Suriname_Justification: Suriname does not have specific legal requirements for financial institutions to perform enhanced due diligence on gold-related transactions beyond standard anti-money laundering (AML) procedures. This represents a significant vulnerability in the financial system's ability to detect and prevent the laundering of proceeds from illegal gold mining. The OAS report extensively documents the challenges of money laundering in Suriname's gold sector, noting the involvement of transnational criminal organizations and the use of sophisticated financial schemes to launder illegal gold proceeds. The report highlights that "there is a chain of illicit activities that range from the irregular importation of precursors and machinery to money laundering and the involvement of criminal syndicates." The absence of sector-specific due diligence requirements means that financial institutions generally operate under a presumption of good faith regarding the legal provenance of gold-related transactions. This presumption is particularly problematic given the documented prevalence of illegal mining and the difficulty in distinguishing legal from illegal gold in the marketplace. Standard AML procedures may not be sufficient to address the specific risks associated with gold transactions, which often involve large cash payments, complex ownership structures, and cross-border movements that can obscure the true source of funds. The mixing of legal and illegal gold in the supply chain makes it extremely difficult for financial institutions to assess the legitimacy of gold-related transactions without specific guidance and requirements. The European Union's due diligence requirements for 3TG (Tin, Tungsten, Tantalum, and Gold) importers, which became operational in 2021, highlight the international trend toward enhanced due diligence for gold transactions. However, Suriname has not implemented similar domestic requirements for its financial institutions.
Suriname_Sources:
- OAS Report: On the Trail of Illicit Gold Proceeds: Strengthening the Fight Against Illegal
Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
- EUR-Lex: European Commission Regulation on 3TG Due Diligence (September 24,2024). URL:
https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:52024DC0415
Venezuela_Justification: Generally speaking, all financial transactions must demonstrate the origin of the funds, and any suspicious activity must be reported to the competent authority (SUDEBAN - Superintendency of Banking Sector Institutions). However, since the gold origination transactions are carried out directly between the miners and the Central Bank of Venezuela (BCV), the legality of subsequent transactions is presumed from that point onward.
Venezuela_Sources: Decree No. 2,165, with the Rank, Value and Force of an Organic Law, reserving to the State the activities of exploration and exploitation of gold and other strategic minerals. Official Gazette of the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela No. 6,210 Extraordinary of December 30, 2015.

Question: Is there a centralized digital/electronic registry for gold supply chain transactions accessible to regulatory authorities and civil society?
Bolivia_Justification: SENARECOM has the SINACOM system platform, in which forms M-02 (internal sales) and M-03 (mineral export) are reported; however, this system is not freely accessible, meaning the information is not accessible to the general public.
Bolivia_Sources:
- SENARECOM – SINACOM. Available at: https://servicios2.sinacom.gob.bo
 - SENARECOM - Official website: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/
 - Ministry of Mining and Metallurgy: https://mineria.gob.bo/
Brazil_Justification: Brazil is developing a centralized digital registration system, but it is not yet fully implemented and accessible. Bill PL 3025/2023 proposes the creation of SIG-Ouro, an electronic system to monitor and record all gold transactions. Currently, information is scattered across different systems, such as those of the Federal Revenue Service (NF-e) and the ANM (National Mining Agency), with limited access for civil society.
Brazil_Sources:
DRAFT LAW No. 3,025 OF 2023 - Planalto
 Escolhas Institute. (2022). Gold Traceability System: Challenges and Recommendations.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has made progress in creating a centralized digital registry for mineral transactions, including gold, through the Mineral Traceability Platform, established by Decree 2234 of 2023. This system is the main technological mechanism for registering, verifying, and controlling the extraction, transportation, processing, and marketing of minerals, with the aim of strengthening transparency, preventing money laundering, and ensuring traceability throughout the entire production chain.

The National Mining Agency (ANM) is the entity responsible for managing and integrating the information within the platform, as well as verifying production volumes, validating the identities of operators, controlling the quantities traded, and ensuring interoperability with other government institutions, such as the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN), the Financial Information and Analysis Unit (UIAF), and the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MinEnergía).

Resolution 759 of 2024 from the National Mining Agency (ANM) regulated the technical operation of the platform, defining the procedures for electronic registration of mining transactions and the mandatory reporting mechanisms for actors in the supply chain. However, access to information is primarily restricted to regulatory authorities, without open access mechanisms or public consultation for civil society or the academic sector.

Consequently, although the country has a centralized digital system in the implementation phase, its use and coverage are still limited, and access to data is restricted to the relevant state institutions. Therefore, the score of 0.5 reflects partial compliance with the criterion: the legal and technological framework exists, but not full operational capacity or open access to information.

National Mining Agency (ANM) – Functions of the Traceability System. Available at: https://www.anm.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: Partial registration exists through the RECPO and the QORI application, but there is no comprehensive, centralized digital record of all transactions in the gold supply chain. Information is available to regulatory authorities, but access for civil society is limited due to confidentiality concerns.

Ecuador_Justification: No publicly accessible records were identified, nor was there any record accessible to regulatory authorities.

•ARCOM. National Mining Cadastre. https://www.controlminero.gob.ec/catastro-minero/
Guyana_Justification: Guyana has a centralized registry of gold supply chain transactions managed by the GGB, but it is not publicly accessible to civil society. While regulatory authorities have access to the data, the lack of public access limits transparency and accountability. The GGB's website provides some information on licensed dealers but does not offer a comprehensive, publicly accessible database of transactions.

Primary Sources:

Secondary Sources:

https://eiti.org/countries/guyana
Suriname_Justification: Suriname does not have a centralized digital or electronic registry for gold supply chain transactions that is accessible to regulatory authorities and civil society. This absence represents a fundamental gap in transparency and accountability mechanisms for the gold sector. While the EITI Suriname website provides some aggregate data on gold exports and revenues, this information does not constitute a comprehensive, transaction-level registry of supply chain activities. The EITI reporting focuses on payments made by extractive companies to the government and revenues received by the government, rather than detailed supply chain transactions between private actors. The lack of a centralized registry is particularly problematic given the complex structure of Suriname's gold supply chain, where small and medium-sized miners sell to local buyers, who then sell to licensed exporters. Without a centralized system to track these transactions, it becomes impossible for regulatory authorities to monitor the flow of gold and money through the system or for civil society to conduct meaningful oversight. The absence of such a registry also limits the government's ability to implement effective anti-money laundering measures or to detect suspicious transaction patterns that might indicate illegal mining or other criminal activities. International best practices increasingly emphasize the importance of centralized, accessible registries for high-risk commodities like gold. Some progress has been made in digitizing mining license information, but this falls far short of a comprehensive supply chain transaction registry. The current systems focus on licensing and cadastral information rather than ongoing transaction monitoring and reporting.
Suriname_Sources:
- EITI Suriname: Official Website. URL: https://eitisuriname.gov.sr/en/
- No evidence of centralized transaction registry found in comprehensive research across government and international source
Venezuela_Justification: There is no publicly available information on the transaction chain associated with the gold supply. All the information that should exist is centralized and for the exclusive use of the government entities involved.

Question: Are there protocols for information sharing and joint enforcement with neighboring countries?
Bolivia_Justification: Bolivia is a member of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO), whose Treaty establishes the exchange of information among ACTO member countries. The Bogotá Declaration, issued in August 2025 during the Fifth Meeting of Presidents of the States Parties to the Treaty of Amazon Cooperation, reaffirms support for the Special Commission on Public Security and Transnational and Cross-Border Crimes in the Amazon Region (resolution RES/XIV/MRE-ACTO/09), to promote police, judicial, and intelligence cooperation in the fight against transnational illicit activities in the Amazon region, including information exchange, among other important aspects.
Bolivia_Sources:
- National Customs Service - Bolivia-Chile Agreement. Available at: https://www.aduana.gob.bo/aduana7/content/bolivia-y-chile-acuerdan-procedimientos-protocolos-e-intercambio-de-informaci%C3%B3n-en-el-%C3%A1mbito
 - Andean Community - First Report of the Andean Mercury Observatory. Available at: https://www.comunidadandina.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/I-Informe-Observatorio-Andino-del-Mercurio.pdf
 - COMIBOL - Chile-Bolivia Mining Forum. Available at: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/index.php/ultimas-noticias/629-bolivia-y-chile-intercambian-conocimientos-sobre-mineria
 - ACTO Amazon Cooperation Treaty. Available at: https://otca.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/TRATADO-DE-COOPERACI%C3%93N-AMAZ%C3%93NICA.pdf
 - Bogotá Declaration (2025). Available at: https://www.cancilleria.gov.co/newsroom/news/declaracion-bogota-termino-v-encuentro-presidentes-organizacion-tratado-cooperacion
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil has protocols for the exchange of information and joint law enforcement with neighboring countries. The Federal Police and the Armed Forces conduct joint operations with neighboring countries to combat cross-border crimes, including gold smuggling and illegal mining. The AURUM project also promotes the exchange of information and best practices among institutions in Amazonian countries.
Brazil_Sources:
Federal Police - International Cooperation
 AURUM - Prevention of Illegal Gold Mining in the Amazon
Colombia_Justification:
Yes. Colombia has established protocols, bilateral agreements, and multilateral mechanisms for information exchange and joint law enforcement regarding environmental control and the fight against illegal mining, particularly in border areas of the Amazon.

At the bilateral level, Colombia maintains cooperation agreements with Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, and Venezuela focused on controlling illegal mining, environmental protection, and the shared management of transboundary natural resources.

At the multilateral level, Colombia actively participates in:

The Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO), which promotes the regional exchange of information on illegal mining, mercury trafficking, and deforestation through the Amazon Regional Observatory (ORA). This mechanism facilitates the exchange of satellite data, early warnings, and joint action protocols among member countries.

The Andean Committee against Illegal Mining (CAMI), created by the Andean Community of Nations (CAN), of which Colombia is a member state along with Bolivia, Ecuador, and Peru. The CAMI promotes the coordination of public policies, regulatory harmonization, and technical cooperation to combat illegal mining.

This includes the exchange of information on illicit mercury trafficking routes and financial flows, as well as coordinated border enforcement actions.

Joint Operations Artemisa, led by the Colombian armed forces with support from Brazil, Peru, and Ecuador.

These joint operations aim to control deforestation, illegal mining, and other illicit economies in the Amazon.

They incorporate protocols for direct communication between environmental, customs, and police authorities and have been replicated as a model for regional cooperation.

Additionally, the SINCHI Amazonian Institute for Scientific Research, under the coordination of the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, participates in regional environmental monitoring platforms and scientific information exchange with counterpart institutions in Brazil, Peru, and Ecuador, facilitating the early detection of transboundary environmental impacts related to gold mining.

Together, these bilateral and multilateral mechanisms consolidate a regional network of police, environmental and scientific cooperation that strengthens the capacity for joint response to illegal mining and associated crimes, ensuring the effective implementation of the law in border areas.

However, these protocols are not specific to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), and their effectiveness in practice is limited due to differences in national legal frameworks.

Ecuador_Justification: There is no specific rule in the Mining Law, nor in its regulations, that is applicable to MAPE mining activity.

Key efforts include, Interinstitutional Cooperation Agreements with Brazil (focused on public security and police intelligence) and efforts to establish joint patrols with Brazil and Suriname.

Suriname_Justification: Yes, Suriname engages in regional information sharing regarding gold supply chain regulation, particularly through South-South cooperation with Guyana, focusing on Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM) best practices. While specific, public joint enforcement protocols are limited, Suriname works with international bodies (e.g., CARICOM) on standards and has established, via the Organization of American States, mechanisms for addressing illegal gold mining finances. Also, The FIU Suriname works on anti-money laundering measures that involve reporting on cross-border movement of valuable items, aligning with regional financial action task forces.Also. Suriname engages with INTERPOL to tackle cross‑border environmental crimes, including illegal timber exploitation and wildlife trafficking, with a strong focus on activities in tri‑border regions. It also partners with international organizations such as the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) to enhance cooperation in the investigation and prosecution of environmental offenses, frequently working alongside the Directorate of National Security.

Question: Do public security bodies have specific strategies in place for safeguarding Indigenous and traditional communities against crimes related to mining in their territories?
Bolivia_Justification: It is evident that public security forces do not have strategies for the protection of indigenous communities against mining-related crimes, although there is legislation such as the CPE where the rights of indigenous peoples are recognized, but these groups are vulnerable because they do not have public security forces.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Ombudsman’s Office - Madidi Ombudsman’s Report: https://www.defensoria.gob.bo/uploads/files/informe-defensorial-nuestra-casa-violation-of-rights-and-non-compliance-with-NPIOC-regulations-and-procedures-for-the-shared-management-of-the-Madidi-protected-area-in-relation-to-mining.pdf
 - Ministry of the Interior: https://www.mingobierno.gob.bo/
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil's public security forces, in conjunction with other government agencies, have specific strategies for protecting indigenous communities. The Kayapó Indigenous Land De-intrusion Operation (OD-TIKAY) and actions in the Yanomami Indigenous Land are examples of large-scale operations aimed at dismantling illegal mining and protecting indigenous territories and populations. These operations involve the Federal Police, the Armed Forces, FUNAI (National Indian Foundation), and IBAMA (Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources), among others.
Brazil_Sources:
Federal operation inflicts R$79 million in losses on illegal mining in the Kayapó Indigenous Territory — Ministry of Indigenous Peoples
 With more than 3,000 operations in 2024, the Federal Government inflicts R$267 million in losses on mining in the Yanomami Indigenous Territory
Colombia_Justification: Colombia has specific strategies in place to protect Indigenous communities from mining-related crimes. The National Police has a Group for the Protection of Ethnic Communities, and the National Army conducts specific operations in Indigenous territories affected by illegal mining.
Colombia_Sources:
1. National Police - Ethnic Communities Protection Group
•URL: https://www.policia.gov.co/
•Specialized unit for the protection of ethnic communities
2. National Army - Operations in Indigenous Territories
•Specific operations such as "Operation Artemis"
•Objective: Control of illegal mining in indigenous territories
3. Decree 2137 of 2018
•Establishes protection measures for social leaders and ethnic communities
Peru_Justification: There are some strategies and legal frameworks for the protection of Indigenous communities, such as Law No. 28736, which establishes special protection for peoples living in isolation. However, practical implementation is deficient, and Indigenous communities continue to face threats, violence, and criminalization when they defend their territories against illegal mining.
Peru_Sources: Law No. 28736: https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H917090
Ecuador_Justification: Currently, there is no specific strategy in place. However, in July 2025, the Organic Law for the Strengthening of Protected Areas came into effect. Article 6 of this law stipulates that the National Police, the Armed Forces, and the National Environmental Authority, within their respective areas of competence, will develop a coordinated Comprehensive Security Plan for the National System of Protected Areas, as well as specific intervention protocols. This plan must be updated periodically, at least annually. The Comprehensive Security Plan and the protocols must guarantee strict compliance with Article 57, paragraph 20, of the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador, which limits military activities in these territories. This is without prejudice to the intervention of security forces in the event of the presence of organized crime or organized armed groups in protected areas, in order to protect the sovereignty and population of these areas.
Ecuador_Sources: Organic Law for the strengthening of Protected Areas.
Guyana_Justification: While legal frameworks like the Amerindian Act 2006 acknowledge Indigenous rights, there is no evidence of specific, proactive strategies by public security forces to protect Indigenous communities from mining-related crimes. Government interventions appear to be reactive rather than part of a defined protective strategy, and reports indicate ongoing human rights abuses and conflicts related to mining in Indigenous areas.
Guyana_Sources:

https://www.parliament.gov.gy/new2/documents/acts/8532-act_20_of_1989_mining.pdf
Secondary Sources:

https://hrp.law.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2013/02/959_file_AllThatGlitters_FINAL_
.pdf
- 2023 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Guyana:
https://www.state.gov/reports/2023-country-reports-on-human-rights-practices/guyana/
Suriname_Justification: There is no evidence that Suriname's public security bodies have developed specific strategies to safeguard Indigenous communities against crimes related to mining in their territories. This represents a critical gap in protection mechanisms, particularly given the documented vulnerability of Indigenous communities to mining-related violence and environmental crimes. The OAS report extensively documents the presence of criminal groups and associated violence in mining areas, noting that these activities "disproportionately affect Indigenous communities" who often live in or near mining zones. The report highlights the involvement of transnational criminal organizations in illegal mining operations, which creates significant security risks for Indigenous populations. The lack of constitutional recognition of Indigenous land rights in Suriname exacerbates this vulnerability. Without formal recognition of their territorial rights, Indigenous communities have limited legal standing to exclude mining operations from their traditional territories or to seek protection from mining-related crimes. The remote location of many Indigenous territories and mining areas creates additional challenges for providing effective protection. The limited presence of state security forces in these areas means that Indigenous communities are often left to face mining-related threats without adequate support from public security bodies. The absence of specific protection strategies is particularly concerning given documented cases of violence, intimidation, and environmental destruction associated with illegal mining operations. Indigenous communities often face threats when they attempt to resist or report illegal mining activities in their territories. International human rights standards emphasize the state's obligation to provide special protection for Indigenous peoples, particularly in contexts where they face heightened risks from extractive activities. Suriname's failure to develop specific strategies for protecting Indigenous communities from mining-related crimes represents a significant gap in meeting these obligations.
Suriname_Sources:
- OAS Report: On the Trail of Illicit Gold Proceeds: Strengthening the Fight Against Illegal K58Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
- No evidence of specific Indigenous protection strategies found in comprehensive research
Venezuela_Justification: There are no specific strategies to prevent and protect Indigenous communities from mining-related crimes; in general, these are treated as common crimes, regardless of the territory in which they occur. However, Indigenous communities have a set of prerogatives established in the Organic Law of Indigenous Peoples and Communities, which sets forth clear procedures for carrying out any activity within their territories and habitats. In this regard, this Law includes preventative measures to avoid the development of mining activity in these areas and, consequently, reduce the likelihood of criminal acts.
Venezuela_Sources: Organic Law of Indigenous Peoples and Communities. Official Gazette of the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela No. 38344 of December 27, 2005

Question: Are there specialized police and prosecutor units for environmental crimes or illegal mining with effective coverage in critical areas?
Bolivia_Justification: For environmental crimes, POFOMA can be mentioned; however, there are no specialized units for illegal mining. According to AJAM regulations, specifically the Internal Regulations Against the Illegal Exploitation of Mineral Resources approved in 2018, police personnel accompany on-site inspections. Regarding the Public Prosecutor's Office, it is unclear whether they have specialized units; however, it has been shown that they are providing formal training to their personnel on illegal mining and strengthening investigative mechanisms.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil has specialized units. The Federal Police has specialized departments for environmental crimes and conducts large-scale operations against illegal mining. The Federal Public Prosecutor's Office (MPF) has specialized prosecutors for the environment and indigenous rights, who investigate and bring legal action against illegal mining. These units operate throughout the national territory, including critical areas of the Amazon.

Federal Police - Environment
 Federal Public Prosecutor’s Office - Environment and Cultural Heritage
Colombia_Justification: Colombia has specialized units within both the National Police and the Attorney General's Office to combat environmental crimes and illegal mining. The Police have the DIJIN (Directorate of Criminal Investigation and INTERPOL) with specialized groups, and the Attorney General's Office has prosecutors specializing in environmental crimes.

•Specialized technical investigation unit

Peru_Justification: In Peru, both the National Police and the Public Prosecutor's Office have specialized units to address environmental crimes and those related to illegal mining. The Peruvian National Police, through its Environmental Directorate (DIRMEAMB) and other divisions specializing in illegal mining, conducts operations to monitor, interdict, and protect natural resources in various regions of the country. Meanwhile, the Public Prosecutor's Office, through its Specialized Environmental Prosecutor's Office, investigates and prosecutes crimes related to illegal mining and environmental pollution, with specialized prosecutors in critical regions to coordinate joint actions with the police and other authorities.
Peru_Sources: Legislative Decree No. 1100: https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H1049650
Ecuador_Justification: Ecuador has the UN-IDCAN (National Unit for the Investigation of Crimes Against the Environment and Nature), a specialized unit within the Judicial Police that focuses on investigating crimes against the environment and nature, with a specific focus on illegal mining and wildlife trafficking. There is also the Specialized Unit for the Investigation of Crimes Against the Environment and Nature within the Attorney General's Office.

Guyana_Justification: While Guyana has an 'Environmental Police Unit' and an 'Environmental Intelligence Unit', and both police and prosecutors receive training on environmental crimes,there is a lack of detailed information on their specific mandates, staffing, and operational effectiveness. The effective coverage of these units in all critical mining areas is also not clear, suggesting that while specialized units exist, their capacity and reach may be limited.

Suriname_Justification: Suriname has established some specialized units to address illegal mining and environmental crimes, but their effectiveness is significantly limited by resource constraints, institutional capacity gaps, and the challenging geography of mining areas. The country has created the Ordening Goudsector (OGS) commission specifically to oversee the small-scale gold mining sector and manage conflicts between different stakeholders. This represents a dedicated institutional mechanism for addressing ASM-related issues, including illegal mining activities. Police forces do conduct operations against illegal mining, and there are some specialized capabilities within law enforcement agencies. However, the OAS report notes that while efforts exist to combat illegal mining, "a more specialized and coordinated approach is needed" to effectively address the scale and complexity of the challenges. The effectiveness of these specialized units is severely constrained by several factors. The vast and remote nature of mining areas makes it extremely difficult to maintain effective coverage of critical zones. Many mining operations occur in areas that are accessible only by boat or aircraft, requiring significant resources for effective monitoring and enforcement. Resource limitations represent a major constraint on the effectiveness of specialized units. The OAS report highlights the need for enhanced institutional capacity and resources to effectively combat illegal mining and associated criminal activities. Limited budgets, equipment, and personnel restrict the ability of specialized units to maintain a meaningful presence in critical mining areas. The involvement of well-organized criminal groups with significant resources creates additional challenges for law enforcement. These groups often have better equipment and local knowledge than government forces, making enforcement operations particularly difficult and dangerous. Coordination between different agencies and levels of government remains a challenge, limiting the effectiveness of specialized units that must work across institutional boundaries to address complex mining-related crimes.

Venezuela_Justification: Environmental protection is carried out by the Bolivarian National Guard (GNB). In 2017, the Public Prosecutor's Office created the Directorate for Comprehensive Environmental Defense and Environmental Crime. The law also provides for the creation of the Mining Guard, responsible for state inspection, surveillance, and control over all mining activities—exploration, exploitation, transportation, and commercialization—an auxiliary body that also falls under the Bolivarian National Guard, the same force that performs environmental police functions.

Question: Is there a clear procedure for investigating crimes related to mining?
Bolivia_Justification: Likewise, the Internal Regulation Against the Illegal Exploitation of Mineral Resources of the AJAM establishes the procedure for on-site inspections, which are carried out for the investigation of the facts and verification of the complaint.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, there is a clear procedure. The investigation of mining-related crimes follows the procedures of the Brazilian Code of Criminal Procedure. The Federal Police is primarily responsible for investigating federal crimes, such as illegal mining on federal lands or indigenous lands. The Federal Public Prosecutor's Office monitors the investigations and may request the initiation of inquiries. The investigations involve the collection of evidence, the carrying out of expert analyses, and the hearing of witnesses, culminating in the indictment of those responsible and the filing of charges in court.

Peru_Justification: Yes, there are established procedures for investigating mining-related crimes. The Penal Code criminalizes illegal mining and establishes the corresponding investigative procedures, while Legislative Decree No. 1100 defines the framework for the interdiction and control of illegal mining. In practice, the Specialized Environmental Prosecutor's Office leads criminal investigations, coordinating with the National Police and other authorities as needed. Entities such as the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM), the National Superintendency of Labor Inspection (SUNAFIL), and the National Water Authority (ANA) participate by providing technical and administrative support, conducting inspections, audits, and expert analyses that serve as evidence to support legal actions. Thus, the procedure combines a clear legal framework with inter-institutional coordination adapted to the complexity of illegal mining.

These procedures are the same as those applicable to crimes prosecuted by the state, and their phases and stages are regulated in Book II of the same code.
Ecuador_Sources: https://www.lexis.com.ec/biblioteca/coip
Guyana_Justification: While general criminal investigation procedures exist, there is no evidence of a clear, specific procedure for investigating mining-related crimes. The lack of specialized protocols can hinder effective investigation and prosecution of these complex offenses, which often involve environmental, social, and economic dimensions.
Guyana_Sources:
Primary Sources:
- Police Act (Cap. 16:01):
https://www.prosecutions.gov.gy/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Police-Act-Cap-16.01.pdf
Suriname_Justification: While Suriname has general procedures for criminal investigation, there is limited evidence of clear, specialized procedures specifically designed for investigating the complex crimes associated with mining operations. This represents a significant gap given the unique characteristics of mining-related crimes, which often involve environmental damage, financial crimes, human rights abuses, and transnational criminal organizations. Mining-related crimes typically require specialized investigative approaches that differ from conventional criminal investigations. These crimes often involve complex financial schemes, environmental evidence that requires technical expertise to collect and analyze, and cross-border elements that require international cooperation. The absence of specialized procedures can significantly hamper the effectiveness of investigations. The OAS report recommends strengthening investigative capabilities and developing more specialized approaches to combat illegal mining and associated criminal activities. This recommendation suggests that current procedures are inadequate for addressing the full scope of mining-related crimes. The complexity of mining-related crimes often requires coordination between multiple agencies, including environmental authorities, financial intelligence units, mining regulators, and traditional law enforcement. Without clear procedures for inter-agency coordination and information sharing, investigations may be fragmented and ineffective. Environmental crimes associated with mining often require specialized technical expertise to document and prosecute effectively. The collection and analysis of environmental evidence, assessment of ecological damage, and establishment of causal links between mining activities and environmental harm require specialized knowledge and procedures that may not be available in general criminal investigation frameworks. The transnational nature of many mining-related crimes, including money laundering and the involvement of international criminal organizations, requires procedures for international cooperation and mutual legal assistance that may not be well-developed in the context of mining crimes.
Suriname_Sources:
- OAS Report: On the Trail of Illicit Gold Proceeds: Strengthening the Fight Against Illegal Mining Finances - Suriname's case (March 2023). URL:
https://www.oas.org/en/sms/dtoc/docs/suriname-eng-digital.pdf
Venezuela_Justification: There are no special protocols for investigating mining-related crimes. Depending on the classification of the crime, the applicable procedures—all established in the various laws of the Republic—may vary between the administrative and criminal spheres.

Question: Does the country have bilateral or multilateral protocols or agreements in place to coordinate joint police operations for the enforcement of environmental and mining laws?
Bolivia_Justification: Bolivia cooperates with its neighbors in the fight against illegal mining through bilateral agreements and its participation in regional organizations. It is a member of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty and the Andean Community (CAN), which adopted Decision 774 (2012) on combating illegal mining, promoting joint actions, border control, and information sharing. In 2018, Bolivia and Peru signed the Cobija Protocol, which establishes the coordination of simultaneous operations to eradicate illegal mining in border areas such as Madre de Dios and Pando. At the institutional level, Bolivia has the Binational Border Commission (COMBIFRON) with Peru and participates in the CAN's Ad Hoc Commission on Illegal Mining, in addition to collaborating with INTERPOL and UNODC in tracing the illegal trade in mercury.
Bolivia_Sources: Cobija Protocol (2018); CAN Decision 774; OTCA (Belém Declaration, 2023); INTERPOL (LEAP Program, 2025).
Brazil_Justification: Brazil actively participates in regional agreements and initiatives aimed at combating illegal mining and environmental crimes in the Amazon. It is a founding member of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty (1978), which provides for joint actions among Amazonian countries to preserve the environment and control cross-border illicit activities. It also maintains bilateral agreements with neighbors, such as the Brazil-Colombia security agreement, which includes joint operations on the Amazonian border against illegal mining and deforestation, and the Brazil-Peru action plan (2009) for sustainable development and combating environmental crimes in the Acre-Madre de Dios-Pando region.
Brazil_Sources: https://news.mongabay.com/2025/08/cross-border-operation-cracks-down-on-environmental-crimes-in-the-amazon/#:~:text=Police%20seized%20more%20than%20310,bulldozers%2C%20trucks%2C%20crushers%20and%20other
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia actively participates in bilateral and multilateral agreements to combat illegal mining and environmental crimes in the Amazon. It is a party to the Amazon Cooperation Treaty (1978) and the Leticia Pact (2019), through which it committed to coordinating actions with Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, and Bolivia to combat deforestation and illegal mining. Within the framework of the Andean Community (Decision 774, 2012), Colombia collaborates with its neighbors on joint operations, information sharing, and the seizure of machinery and materials used in illicit mining activities.

There are also binational commissions with Peru, Ecuador, and Brazil that address security and environmental issues. For example, in 2023, a binational operation was carried out with Brazil on the Puré River, where 19 illegal mining dredges that were contaminating the water with mercury were destroyed. With Peru, Colombia participates in binational cabinets focused on controlling illegal mining along the Putumayo River.

Colombia hosted the Amazon Summit of the ACTO (Bogotá, 2025), where countries agreed to strengthen police and judicial cooperation and create a Special Commission for Amazonian Security to combat transboundary environmental crimes.
Colombia_Sources: OTCA (Bogotá Declaration, 2025); Colombian National Police (Operation Puré, 2023); INTERPOL (Green Shield, 2025); Andean Community (Decision 774, 2012).
Peru_Justification: Peru has a broad network of bilateral and multilateral agreements to combat illegal mining and environmental crimes. It is a signatory to the Amazon Cooperation Treaty and an active member of the Andean Community, within which framework it participates in the Andean Policy against Illegal Mining (Decision 774, 2012). At the bilateral level, it maintains the Cobija Protocol (2018) with Bolivia to conduct joint mining interdiction operations in border areas, as well as agreements with Brazil (Action Plan 2009) and Colombia (Binational Cabinets) that include cooperation on environmental security. Institutionally, Peru's Commissioner for the Formalization and Oversight of Illegal Mining maintains direct coordination with its Bolivian and Brazilian counterparts. The Environmental Prosecutor's Office, the National Police, and the Customs Superintendency collaborate with their counterparts in neighboring countries through information exchanges and joint operations.
Peru_Sources: Cobija Protocol (2018); CAN Decision 774; ACTO (Bogotá Declaration, 2025); INTERPOL (Green Shield, 2025).
Ecuador_Justification: Ecuador participates in various regional cooperation frameworks for the control of illegal mining and environmental protection. It is a party to the Amazon Cooperation Treaty and the Andean Community, complying with Decision 774 (2012) on combating illegal mining. At the bilateral level, it maintains the Binational Commission for the Integrated Management of Border Basins with Peru, created under the 1998 peace treaty, which coordinates actions in the Chinchipe and Santiago-Cenepa river basins to combat illegal mining. With Colombia, Ecuador has been developing joint military and police surveillance plans along the Putumayo border since 2011, where binational illicit mining operations are taking place.
Ecuador_Sources: Amazon Cooperation Treaty (1978); CAN (Decision 774, 2012); OTCA (Bogotá Declaration, 2025); INTERPOL (Green Shield, 2025).
Guyana_Justification: Guyana utilizes both national inter-agency mechanisms and international partnerships to enforce environmental and mining laws. The government, through the Ministry of Natural Resources and the Guyana Geology and Mines Commission (GGMC), executes joint operations with the police to curb illegal mining. These operations are increasingly coordinated through the newly launched National Anti-Illegal Mining Operations Secretariat (NAIMOS).
Guyana_Sources: https://dpi.gov.gy/ggmc-calls-on-all-miners-to-comply-with-mining-laws-or-face-consequences/#:~:text=The%20Guyana%20Geology%20and%20Mines%20Commission%20(GGMC),Environmental%20stewardship%20*%20Employment%20of%20undocumented%20persons
Suriname_Justification: Yes, Suriname has established both bilateral and multilateral protocols and agreements to coordinate joint actions for the enforcement of environmental and mining laws, largely focused on controlling illegal mining, deforestation and cross-border environmental crimes. Suriname ratified the Minamata Convention on Mercury in 2018 to address the importation, trade, and use of mercury, particularly within the artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM) sector. Suriname collaborates through INTERPOL to combat transnational environmental crimes, including illegal timber and wildlife trade, particularly in tri-border areas and works with international partners such as the UNODC (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime) to strengthen partnerships for investigating and prosecuting environmental crimes, often involving the Directorate of National Security.
Suriname_Sources:
https://www.interpol.int/en/News-and-Events/News/2026/198-arrests-in-cross-border-illegal-gold-mining-operation-in-South-America
https://international-partnerships.ec.europa.eu/policies/programming/programmes/el-paccto-20_en

Question: Have courts issued binding decisions related to illegal gold mining or its socio-environmental impacts in the last five years?
Bolivia_Justification: There is Constitutional Resolution No. 05/2023, which CPILAP won in the Rurrenabaque court, regarding illegal activities in the Beni and Kaka rivers, among others, related to illegal mining. Likewise, there are Judgments No. 004/2023/AP and Plurinational Constitutional Judgment No. 1326/2023, which uphold the rejection of mining and the flawed prior consultation process by the communities and agricultural cooperatives of the municipalities of Palos Blancos and Alto Beni.

The Supreme Court's decision in March 2025, which ended the presumption of good faith in the gold trade, is an example of a decision with an impact on the entire production chain. Furthermore, the Federal Courts have issued preliminary injunctions to halt illegal mining activities and hold offenders accountable, as in the case of the Japurá River in Amazonas.
